text: Additional Volumes in Preparation
To the memories of Professors
W. Orlicz and M. A. Krasnoselskii
for originating and deepening
the subject on which this book and our earlier one are based
Preface

The following account consists of some important applications in which the theory of Orlicz spaces plays a crucial role. A general account of Orlicz spaces was given in considerable detail in our companion volume in 1991. The applications discussed here often specialize and sharpen the general theory as well as show a need for further new investigations. These include specific results with the underlying measure spaces restricted to purely discrete or purely diffuse cases and also concentrating on subclasses of $N$ -functions from general Young functions, to study some finer aspects of the problems. This motivated us to consider various properties of Orlicz sequence and function spaces useful for current and future applications. In fact, the material covers a very wide spectrum including geometric, Fourier analytic, stochastic, PDE, composition, and Fréchet metric function space results among others. A brief description of the contents of various chapters gives a better idea of our treatment, planned and executed. More detailed summations appear at the beginnings of the chapters.

First we recall some background material from the earlier volume in Chapter I with several additions, in a form for immediate use. These include quantitative indices of $N$ -functions, properties of general Young functions as well as certain interpolation inequalities.

Then the next four chapters present aspects of quantitative geometry, specialized from the general Banach space theory, for Orlicz sequence and function spaces the latter signifying that the underlying measure is diffuse or nonatomic. These include nonsquare constants in the sense of James, von Neumann-Jordan constants in Chapter II, weakly convergent sequence and normal structure coefficients in Chapter III, Jung constants in Chapter IV as well as Kottman constants and packing problems in Chapter V all of which prove very convenient technical results for many applications here and elsewhere. Thus they have some independent interest. The coefficients and these constants constitute geometric invariants which find an immediate use of the quantitative indices of $N$ -functions and the interpolation results included in Chapter I.

The following four chapters, almost independent of each other, deal with substantial accounts of applications to different parts of analysis where in each case Orlicz spaces play a significant role, demanding much additional
V

work. Thus Chapter VI is devoted to Fourier analysis including conjugate functions with Ryan's extension of the classical M. Riesz theorem, a Hausdorff-Young theorem on compact and locally compact abelian groups as well as some results on generalized (or nonlocally convex) Orlicz spaces. Chapter VII treats prediction analysis including work on vector valued functions and nonreflexive Orlicz spaces as well as a discussion of conditions for linearity of predictors. Next Chapter VIII, which is one of the longest chapters of the book, deals with an analysis of large deviations of probability theory and exponential (vector) Orlicz spaces. The analysis here illustrates the methods by showing how the basic concepts are closely related to a class of Fenchel-Orlicz spaces, thereby enlarging the scope of the subject. Also (vector and operator) martingale convergence and regularity properties of stochastic functions are considered. In Chapter IX, similar ideas play a key role in applications of this work to partial differential equations by studying Orlicz-Sobolev spaces for removable singularities, extensions of (strong and weak) Hardy-Littlewood type inequalities and continuity properties of composition and Nemitsky operators on these spaces.

We conclude the work with Chapter X, containing supplements to the previous chapters. These include a section on Beurling-Orlicz classes of convolution algebras, geometric invariants with F. Riesz angles, embedding theorems for Orlicz sequence spaces as well as sharp norm differentiability properties of Orlicz spaces when the measures are purely discrete or diffuse. We end the book with a detailed analysis of nonlocally convex Orlicz spaces, complementing the last third of Chapter VI, when the measures are restricted as above, since it is possible to give a more refined analysis of the subjects considered.

There is much new material in these chapters, and the details appear here for the first time. We have given credit to authors in a bibliographical notes section for each chapter, as far as possible. Many of the applications can be studied independently. But we observe that they are all based on the fundamental and common properties of Orlicz spaces included here and in our companion volume. We have repeated a few concepts at different places, instead of referring the reader to sources where they first appeared, for better comprehension.

The work presented has been in preparation for nearly four years. During this period, we have tried to unify, refine and generalize the available original contributions. We learned a great deal in this investigation and tried to show
vi
Preface

the fascination of these applications. We also attempted to exemplify the saying that the life of a theory is derived from real applications. We shall be happy if our readers share this feeling with us.

We never met Professor W. Orlicz (1903-1990). One of us had a brief correspondence with him in earlier times. We sadly learned of his passing when our companion volume was in press. The very interesting life history and mathematical work of Professor Orlicz have been provided to us by Dr. Lech Maligranda, one of his former doctoral students and an able associate, along with his own important research and a book (1989). Although for space reasons we could not include much of this material, we are very grateful to him for this information and detail. Then again Professor M. A. Krasnoselskii (1920-1997) passed away [we learned this from the notices of the journal Russian Math. Surveys, 53:1 (1998), 195-198] while this book was over half completed. He had been provided with a copy of our first volume soon after it appeared, and one of us met him later at a conference in 1996, but could not communicate well because of language difficulties. However, it is clear that we and much of the world mathematical community have gained considerably by the fundamental research of both these stalwarts. As an expression of our deep appreciation we dedicate this volume to the memories of both these fine mathematicians.

The preparation of the monograph has taken considerable time and effort. The typing was aided by Jan Carter in the early Chapters. It was then continued with the able assistance of Ren's doctoral students in the Department of Mathematics, Suzhou University, in the People's Republic of China. They are Jincai Wang, Tao Zhang and Yaqiang Yan whose help is very much appreciated. Ren's visits to UCR have been enthusiastically supported by his wife Qingzeng Qiu. Also in 1999-2001, Ren was supported financially by Suzhou University ("211" program) and Jiangsu Province (Natural Science Fund), enabling him to spend the year at UCR. Some problems with AMS-LaTeX in obtaining the final version were resolved with some help by our colleague Y. Kakihara and earlier by the UCR systems group member L. Timmermans. We shall be gratified if this volume helps further the analysis of the important subjects covered here.
M. M. Rao
Z.D.Ren

Chapter I
Introduction and Background Material

This chapter contains an account of the background material on Orlicz spaces to be used in the rest of the book. Since for applications some additional and specialized results other than those presented in our earlier volume are needed, such material is included here with details. In particular, a new quantitative index and inequalities based on certain interpolation theorems for a class of Orlicz spaces (the $L^{\Phi_s}$ -spaces) are established. A characterization of the Lebesgue $L^p$ spaces, from $L^{\Phi}$ , is also given. These results are utilized in a study of particular geometrical aspects of Orlicz spaces, especially in the next four chapters. They are useful in several applications.

Since the primary goal here is to apply the material to various interesting problems, we concentrate on ("smooth" and) nice Young functions, or the $N$ -functions, for most of the following analysis. Extensions of these results for general Young functions and arbitrary measure spaces need further work, and we shall often omit it, referring the interested reader to our earlier volume where such a treatment was considered.
1.1 $N$ -functions and quantitative indices
A mapping $\Phi : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^+$ is termed an $N$ -function (nice Young function) if

list_items: (i) $\Phi$ is even and convex.
(ii) $\Phi (x) = 0$ iff $x = 0$ , and

text: (iii) $\lim_{x\to 0}\frac{\Phi(x)}{x} = 0,\lim_{x\to \infty}\frac{\Phi(x)}{x} = +\infty .$

The left (right) derivative $\varphi(t) = \Phi_{-}^{\prime}(t) (= \Phi_{+}^{\prime}(t))$ exists and is left (right) continuous, nondecreasing on $(0, \infty)$ , satisfies $0 < \varphi(t) < \infty$ for $0 < t < \infty$ , $\varphi(0) = 0$ and $\lim_{t \to \infty} \varphi(t) = \infty$ . [Similar description is valid for the right derivative, but we concentrate on the left one.] The left inverse $\psi$ of $\varphi$ is, by definition, $\psi(s) = \inf \{t > 0 : \varphi(t) > s\}$ for $s > 0$ . Then $\Phi, \Psi$ given by
are called a pair of complementary $N$ -functions which satisfy the Young inequality:

with equality iff $\varphi(|x|) \leq |y| \leq \varphi_{+}(|x|)$ when $x$ is given, or $\psi(|y|) \leq |x| \leq \psi_{+}(|y|)$ when $y$ is given ( $\psi_{+} = \Psi_{+}'$ ; $\varphi_{+} = \Phi_{+}'$ ). The $N$ -function $\Psi$ complementary to $\Phi$ can equally be defined by:
A classification of $N$ -functions based on their growth rates is facilitated by the following:

Definition 1. An $N$ -function $\Phi$ is said to obey the $\Delta_2$ -condition for large $x$ (for small $x$ , or for all $x$ ), written often as $\Phi \in \Delta_2(\infty)(\Phi \in \Delta_2(0)$ , or $\Phi \in \Delta_2$ ), if there exist constants $x_0 > 0, K > 2$ such that $\Phi(2x) \leq K\Phi(x)$ for $x \geq x_0$ (for $0 \leq x \leq x_0$ , or for all $x \geq 0$ ); and it obeys the $
abla_2$ -condition for large $x$ (for small $x$ , or for all $x$ ), denoted symbolically as $\Phi \in 
abla_2(\infty)$ ( $\Phi \in

Quantitative indices of an $N$ -function $\Phi$ are provided by the following six constants.

Theorem 2. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be complementary $N$ -functions. Then the following are equivalent statements:

(iii) there exist constants $\alpha > 1$ and $s_0 > 0$ such that $\frac{s\psi(s)}{\Psi(s)} > \alpha$ , for $s \geq s_0$ , i.e., $A_{\Psi} > 1 (A_{\Psi}^0 > 1, \bar{A}_{\Psi} > 1)$ ;

so that $B_{\Phi} \leq \beta < \infty$ .

1.2 Some basic results on $L^{\Phi}$ -spaces

We set down the basic concepts and some results of Orlicz spaces for convenience and constant use below. Hereafter $\Phi$ usually denotes an $N$ -function and $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ a measure space to be specialized from time to time.
Definition 1. The set of functions $\tilde{L}^{\Phi}$ is defined on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ by:

$$
\tilde {L} ^ {\Phi} = \{f: \Omega \to \mathbb {R}, \quad \text {m e a s u a r a b l e}, \quad \rho_ {\Phi} (f) = \int_ {\Omega} \Phi (f) d \mu <   \infty \}.
$$
If $0 < \mu(A) < \infty$ , $A \in \Sigma$ , then we have the Jensen inequality:

In case $\mu$ is a discrete measure on $A$ , then one has the corresponding inequality of (1), replacing the integral by sums with appropriate weights. If $\mu$ is discrete on $\Omega$ itself, where $\Omega$ is also countable, and $\mu$ is a counting measure (take $\Omega = \{1,2,\ldots\}$ for definiteness, and so $\mu(\{i\}) = 1$ ) the space $\tilde{L}^{\Phi}$ becomes the Orlicz sequence class, denoted $\tilde{\ell}^{\Phi}$ . These classes are not necessarily linear. We have:

Proposition 2. (i) If $\Phi \in \Delta_2(\infty)[\Phi \in \Delta_2]$ , then $\tilde{L}^{\Phi}$ is a vector space when $\mu (\Omega) < \infty [\mu (\Omega) = \infty ]$ . The converse holds if $\mu$ is diffuse on a set of positive measure.
(ii) $\tilde{\ell}^{\Phi}$ is a vector space iff $\Phi \in \Delta_2(0)$ .

Proof. Consider the case that $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ , and $\Phi \in \Delta_2(\infty)$ so that there are $K > 2, u_0 > 0$ , such that $\Phi(2u) \leq K\Phi(u)$ , for all $u \geq u_0$ . For linearity let $f \in \tilde{L}^{\Phi}$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}$ . Then we can find an integer $n_0 \geq 1$ such that $|c| \leq 2^{n_0}$ and so $\Phi(cu) \leq \Phi(2^{n_0}u) \leq K^{n_0}\Phi(u), u \geq u_0$ . Hence

By the convexity of $\rho_{\Phi}$ (or $\Phi$ ), it follows that for any $f_i \in \tilde{L}^{\Phi}, i = 1,2$ , $\rho_{\Phi}(f_1 + f_2) \leq \frac{1}{2} [\rho_{\Phi}(2f_1) + \rho_{\Phi}(2f_2)] < \infty$ , with $c = 2$ in the above. Thus $\tilde{L}^{\Phi}$ is linear. If $\mu(\Omega) = +\infty$ , then $u_0 = 0$ so that the same argument is valid showing the linearity of $\tilde{L}^{\Phi}$ .

Definition 3. The space $L^{\Phi} = \{f : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}, \text{ measurable}, \rho_{\Phi}(af) < \infty \text{ for some } a > 0\}$ is called an Orlicz space on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ . Similarly let $M^{\Phi} = \{f : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}, \text{ measurable}, \rho_{\Phi}(af) < \infty \text{ for all } a > 0\}$ , called the Morse-Transue space (after the study by the latter authors [1]).
It is evident that $M^{\Phi} \subset L^{\Phi}$ , and both are vector spaces. We shall introduce a norm functional through the following:
Proposition 4. Let $\Phi, \Psi$ be a complementary pair of $N$ -functions.
(i) If $f \in L^{\Phi}$ , then the functional $\| \cdot \|_{\Phi}$ (called Orlicz norm), defined by
is indeed a semi-norm, i.e., a positively homogeneous subadditive functional.
(ii) Also $(L^{\Phi},\| \cdot \|_{\Phi})$ is a Banach space if $f,g$ are identified when $\| f - g\|_{\Phi} = 0$ . [This identification can and will be assumed hereafter.]

The proof of this proposition is a slight modification of the classical Lebesgue case. The details are found in either Krasnoselskii and Rutickii ([1], Theorem 9.2) or our companion volume (Rao and Ren [1], pp. 67-68). They will not be reproduced here. As the latter reference shows, the result is true for arbitrary measures and all Young functions (not only the $N$ -functions), but the proofs need additional arguments and they are found in our companion volume.

For an Orlicz space $L^{\Phi}$ , a Young or $N$ -function $\Phi$ is termed $\Delta_{2}$ -regular if $\Phi \in \Delta_2(\infty)$ when $\mu (\Omega) < \infty$ , or $\Phi \in \Delta_2$ when $\mu (\Omega) = \infty$ , or with $\mu$ a counting measure (so $L^{\Phi} = \ell^{\Phi}$ ) and $\Phi \in \Delta_2(0)$ . We can now state the following properties to be used later.

and $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ is separable, then $L^{\Phi}(= M^{\Phi})$ is separable. [Separability refers to the existence of a dense denumerable subcollection.]

The proof is somewhat involved and we refer the detail given in our earlier volume (Rao and Ren [1], pp. 87-91). Especially the last part needs more analysis even in the $L^p$ -space case ( $1 \leq p < \infty$ ).

Remark. If $\Omega$ is a locally compact space and $\mu$ is a regular Borel measure (e.g., $\Omega = \mathbb{R}^n$ and $\mu = \text{Lebesgue measure}$ ), then bounded functions with compact supports are dense in $M^\Phi$ . In particular, if $\Omega$ is compact then the bounded functions are dense so that $L^\infty$ is dense in $M^\Phi$ . These statements follow from the above proposition and standard results of Real Analysis.

text: Proof. Since $\mu$ is a counting measure, $f_{n}(t) \to 0$ for each $t$ with $0 < \mu(\{t\}) < \infty$ . Hence,

$$
\mu (\{t \}) f _ {n} (t) = \int_ {\{t \}} f _ {n} (t) d \mu (t) \rightarrow 0 = \mu (\{t \}) f (t).
$$

But $f_{n}(t) \to f(t) = 0$ for each $t, 0 < \mu(\{t\}) < \infty$ , and then $f_{n} \to 0$ on each set of finite $\mu$ -measure. Now the characteristic functions of such sets form a norm determining subspace of $m^{(\Psi)}(m^{\Psi})$ . Thus $f_{n} \to 0$ weakly. By a well-known result (cf. Rao and Ren [1], p. 150, Prop.

A curious fact about these norms is that even if $\Phi(u) = |u|^p, 1 < p < \infty$ , so that $L^{(\Phi)} = L^p$ , the norm $\| \cdot \|_{(\Phi)} = \| \cdot \|_p$ , but $\| \cdot \|_{\Phi} \neq \| \cdot \|_p$ since $\Psi(u) \neq |u|^q, q = \frac{p}{p-1}$ . Thus the asymmetry persists, and motivates a definition of "normalized $N$ -pairs", discussed in (Zygmund [1]), and in our book (Rao and Ren [1], p. 35). The above result was given in (Rao [5], Lemma 1), and a streamlined proof, using Theorem 16, is presented in our book ([1], p. 73). It will be omitted here. Remarks. 1. An interesting point raised by this theorem should be recorded. Since the Orlicz and gauge norms are the analogs of the adjoint and the original norms in a Lebesgue space, Theorems 18 and 19 show that (in the generalization to Orlicz spaces) they are never equal. Indeed even the proportionality of these norms characterizes Lebesgue spaces. But instead of considering $\Phi(u) = |u|^p$ and $\Psi$ as its complementary function, one can use the additional fact available in the context of Lebesgue spaces, namely that $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$ for the conjugate exponents. For $\Phi$ taken above, this identity is not included. If $\Phi(u) = \frac{|u|^p}{p}$ , then $\Psi(v) = \frac{|v|^q}{q}$ and $\Phi(1) + \Psi(1) = 1$ . Thus for a complementary pair $(\Phi, \Psi)$ this is an additional condition. Fortunately, it does not restrict the class of $(\Phi, \Psi)$ ; and it was shown by Billik [1] and made available to the public in Zygmund ([1], Vol. I, p. 125), that for every complementary pair of Young functions $(\Phi, \Psi)$ satisfying $\Phi(1) + \Psi(1) = 1$ , [one calls them a normalized pair] it is true that the Hölder inequality becomes:

2. The inequalities in (36) cannot be improved in general. Thus a consequence of Theorems 18 and 19 is that none of these norms is proportional if one is not in the Lebesgue spaces $L^p, p > 1$ . This means one has to use the Orlicz [or adjoint] and gauge norms alternatively in the adjoint spaces. For this reason, one may use the complementary $N$ -functions with or without normalization indifferently.
1.3 Notes on Young functions and general measures

In the preceding two sections we discussed results for $N$ -functions and their (Orlicz) spaces. Specialized to the $L^p$ spaces, these exclude the important classes $L^1$ and $L^\infty$ which are both Banach function spaces and also Banach algebras. To include these, the $N$ -functions have to be replaced by the more extensive Young functions and also the general measures. These have been treated in detail in our earlier volume, and here we indicate some glimpses of the generalization, for a comparison and use in applications in some later chapters.

Definition 1. A mapping $\Phi: \mathbb{R} \to \bar{\mathbb{R}}^+$ is a Young function if it is symmetric and convex, and $\Phi(0) = \lim_{u \to 0} \Phi(u) = 0$ , $\lim_{u \to \infty} \Phi(u) = +\infty$ . The complementary Young function $\Psi$ to $\Phi$ is then given by
which is also a Young function. The pair $(\Phi, \Psi)$ satisfies
[(2) is the Young inequality.]
We find it convenient to classify Young functions based on:
Definition 2. A Young function $\Phi$ is classified as:

(i) $\Phi \in I$ if $\Phi$ is continuous, (ii) $\Phi \in II$ if $\Phi$ is strictly increasing on $[0,a), 0 < a \leq \infty$ , and $\Phi(u) = +\infty$ for $u \geq a$ , (iii) $\Phi \in III$ if $\lim_{u \to +\infty} \frac{\Phi(u)}{u} = +\infty$ , and (iv) $\Phi \in IV$ if $\lim_{u \to 0} \frac{\Phi(u)}{u} = 0$ .
As a consequence of these definitions and of an $N$ -function, one has the following result whose verification is not difficult.
Proposition 3. Let $\Phi$ be a Young function. Then $(i)$ $\Phi \in I$ iff $\Phi (u) < \infty$ for all $|u| < \infty$ , and $\Phi \in II$ iff $\Phi (u) = 0 \Rightarrow u = 0$ .

(ii) Suppose $\Phi \in I \cap II$ , then $\Phi \in III$ iff $\varphi(\infty) = \lim_{u \to \infty} \varphi(u) = +\infty$ and $\Phi \in IV$ iff $\varphi_+(0) = \lim_{u \to 0^+} \varphi(u) = 0$ , where $\varphi$ and $\varphi_+$ are, as usual, the left and right derivatives of $\Phi$ .
I. Introduction and Background Material
(iii) $\Phi$ is an $N$ -function iff $\Phi \in I \cap II \cap III \cap IV$ .
An important property of $N$ -functions is the following:

Proposition 4. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a complementary Young pair. If $\Phi \in I \cap II$ and $\Psi \in I \cap II$ , i.e., $\Phi$ and $\Psi$ are both continuous and vanish only at the origin, then $\Phi, \Psi$ are $N$ -functions. Thus, $\Phi$ is an $N$ -function iff $\Phi, \Psi \in I \cap II$ .

Proof. Let $\Phi(u) = \int_{0}^{|u|} \varphi(t) dt$ , and $\Phi \in I \cap II$ . If $\Psi \in I \cap II$ , we claim that $\Phi \in III \cap IV$ . Let us show $\Phi \in III$ . If not, then $\lim_{u \to \infty} \varphi(u) = b < \infty$ by Proposition 3 (ii). Therefore, its left inverse $\psi(v) = +\infty$ for $b \leq v < \infty$ and so, $\Psi(v) = +\infty$ if $v > b$ , i.e., $\Psi \notin I$ , a contradiction since we assumed $\Psi \in I \cap II$ . Next we show $\Phi \in IV$ . If not, then $\varphi_{+}(0) = \lim_{u \to 0^{+}} \varphi(u) = a > 0$ . Therefore, $\psi(v) = 0$ for $0 \leq v \leq a$ and $\Psi(v) = \int_{0}^{|v|} \psi(s) ds = 0$ if $0 \leq |v| \leq a$ , i.e., $\Psi \notin II$ . A contradiction. By Proposition 3 (iii), $\Phi$ is an $N$ -function. Finally, $\Psi$ is also an $N$ -function, since the complementary function of an $N$ -function is an $N$ -function as pointed out in Section 1.1.
Now we give some examples, which are Young functions but not $N$ -functions.

$$

As another example, let $\Phi$ be a continuous Young function that vanishes only at the origin and suppose that the complementary Young function $\Psi$ of $\Phi$ has the same properties (by Proposition 4, $\Phi$ is an $N$ -function), then it is shown that $\|f\|_{\Phi} = \|f\|_{(\Phi)}$ iff $f = 0$ (cf. Rao [5], Lemma 1). Since for $\Phi_3$ of the above Young function, which is not an $N$ -function, this result is not applicable (it cannot hold since, in fact, the equality is valid for $L^1$ ).

One of the important features of Orlicz spaces is that the two norms $\| \cdot \|_{\Phi}$ and $\| \cdot \|_{(\Phi)}$ are equivalent and rarely equal, in contrast to the Lebesgue spaces. The spaces $L^{\Phi}$ and $L^{(\Phi)}$ are isomorphic. In some ways this is a weaker property, but it is compensated by the existence of a rich collection of isomorphically equivalent Orlicz spaces. This is exemplified by the following result of considerable importance in applications.

Theorem 11. Let $\Phi$ be a continuous Young function such that $\Phi(u) = 0$ iff $u = 0$ , i.e., $\Phi \in I \cap II$ . Then there exists a strictly convex continuous Young function $\Phi_1$ such that $L^{\Phi}(\mu) = L^{\Phi_1}(\mu)$ , on any measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ , and their norms are equivalent. Thus the spaces are isomorphic (but not isometric). Moreover, if $\Phi$ is $\Delta_2$ -regular, so is $\Phi_1$ .
Proof. This result was proved in our earlier volume ([1], p. 153), but we include a simple construction for completeness. Thus we have
1.3 Notes on Young functions and general measures
Define a new function $\varphi_{1}(t) = \varphi (t)(2 - e^{-t})$ for $t\geq 0$ and set

Since $\varphi_{1} \geq 0$ and strictly increasing, $\Phi_{1}$ is strictly convex. Moreover, it is clear that $\Phi(u) \leq \Phi_{1}(u) \leq 2\Phi(u), u \in \mathbb{R}$ . Hence $f \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ iff $f \in L^{\Phi_{1}}(\mu)$ and $\|f\|_{(\Phi)} \leq \|f\|_{(\Phi_{1})} \leq 2\|f\|_{(\Phi)}$ . Thus, the spaces are isomorphic. The $\Delta_{2}$ -regularity is immediate from construction. $\square$

In the above reference an uncountable collection of distinct Young functions generating equivalent norms is given. Note in particular, if $\Phi(u) = |u|$ , then $\Phi_1$ constructed above is strictly convex and $\Delta_{2^{\prime}}$ -regular, and hence even though $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu) = L^{1}(\mu)$ is not strictly convex, its isomorphically equivalent (collection) $L^{(\Phi_1)}(\mu)$ is strictly convex (cf. our [1], p. 272). On the other hand, $\Phi(u) = |u|$ and its (Young) complementary function $\Psi$ are not $N$ -functions, but are necessary to consider these "boundary" functions if the Orlicz space theory is to generalize the Lebesgue theory. Moreover, $L^1$ and $L^{\infty}$ are some of the most important concrete Banach spaces which are also algebras. These spaces cannot be included in Orlicz spaces based on $N$ -functions alone. Let us start with the following:

Definition 12. A vector space $\mathcal{X}$ is called a commutative Banach algebra if $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)$ is a Banach space and for each $x,y$ in $\mathcal{X}$ a product $xy$ is defined and the latter satisfies $xy = yx$ as well as the inequality $\| xy\| \leq \| x\| \| y\|$ , so that multiplication is a continuous operation in the norm topology of $\mathcal{X}$ .

As examples, (i) $\mathcal{X} = C_0(\mathbb{R})$ , the space of continuous functions vanishing at $\pm \infty$ , with uniform norm; (ii) $L^{\infty}(\mathbb{R})$ , under pointwise multiplication; (iii) $\mathcal{X} = L^{1}(\mathbb{R})$ , the Lebesgue space of integrable functions on $(\mathbb{R}, \Sigma, \mu)$ with $\mu$ as Lebesgue measure and multiplication as convolution, i.e., for $f, g \in L^{1}(\mathbb{R})$ , the convolution $f * g$ is given by

These are commutative, and if $\mathcal{X} = B(\mathcal{Y})$ , the space of all bounded linear operators on a Banach space $\mathcal{Y}$ with operator norm, then it is a noncommutative Banach algebra. A change of variables, and translation invariance of $\mu$ shows that $f*g = g*f$ in (6), and $\| f*g\|_1 \leq \| f\|_1 \| g\|_1$ (by Fubini's theorem). Here we include some extensions of (6) in the context of Orlicz spaces, indicating the availability of a class of interesting applications and research problems in this area.

On the other hand, if $\Phi \in I \cap II$ , and if there is an absolute constant $K$ such that (7) holds for all simple functions $f$ and $g$ , then (7) again obtains for all $f, g \in L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ ; so $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is closed under convolution.

Proof. It was already noted that $f*g = g*f$ whenever it is defined, and by hypothesis, $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is closed under convolution as multiplication, so that it is an algebra. Then the mapping $f \mapsto T_f$ defined as $T_f(g) = f*g$ on the spaces $T: L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R}) \to B(L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R}))$ , is a homomorphism, since for any $h \in L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ one has

and the left side is also $(f*g)*h = T_{f*g}(h)$ , so that $T_{f}T_{g} = T_{f*g} = T_{g*f} = T_{g}T_{f}$ , and $f\mapsto \bar{f}$ (the complex conjugate) is an "involution". Consequently, by the abstract theory the spectral radius of $T_{f}$ , namely $\| T_f\|_{sp} = \lim_{n\to \infty}\| T_f^n\|^{\frac{1}{n}}$ exists, and satisfies $\| T_f\|_{sp}\leq K\| f\|_{\Phi}$ for some $0 < K < \infty$ (cf., Loomis [1], Lemma on p. 92). Moreover, $\| T_f\| = \| T_f\|_{sp}$ in this case. Hence, we have
which implies (7), since

$$
\left\| T _ {f} (g) \right\| _ {\Phi} = \left\| f * g \right\| _ {\Phi} \leq K \left\| f \right\| _ {\Phi} \left\| g \right\| _ {\Phi}.
$$

In the reverse direction, suppose (7) holds for simple functions $f, g$ . Then $f * g \in L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ and $0 < K < \infty$ is an absolute constant. It suffices to consider $0 \leq f, g$ since for the general case $f = f_1 - f_2, g = g_1 - g_2$ implies $f * g = f_1 * g_1 - f_2 * g_1 - f_1 * g_2 + f_2 * g_2$ and the result will follow, by linearity, for all $f, g$ in $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ (and then similarly for complex functions). Now we can find simple $0 \leq f_n, g_n$ in $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ such that $f_n \uparrow f, g_n \uparrow g$ . Let $n_0$ be arbitrarily fixed, and from (6), $f_{n_0} * g_n \uparrow f_{n_0} * g$ , a.e. and hence by the monotone property of the
1.3 Notes on Young functions and general measures

norm: $\| f_{n_0} * g_n\|_{\Phi} \leq K\| f_{n_0}\|_{\Phi}\| g\|_{\Phi}$ yields, on letting $n \to \infty$ , that $\| f_{n_0} * g\|_{\Phi} \leq K\| f_{n_0}\|_{\Phi}\| g\|_{\Phi} \leq K\| f\|_{\Phi}\| g\|_{\Phi}$ . Since $f_{n_0} * g = g * f_{n_0}$ , this implies, on letting $n_0 \to \infty$ , that (7) holds. $\square$

Remark. The continuity of $\Phi$ , in the statement is used in approximating $f, g$ in $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ by simple function sequences. If $\Phi(u) = 0$ for $0 \leq |u| \leq a, a > 0$ , or $\Phi \notin I$ , the simple function (pointwise) approximation has to be replaced by uniform sequences, and the argument runs into trouble.

The question arises whether there are Young functions $\Phi \in I \cap II$ for which (7) holds. In fact, if $\Phi(u) = |u|$ , then $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R}) = L^{1}(\mathbb{R})$ and the result is classical (with $K = 1$ in (7)). However, by Theorem 11 there are strictly convex continuous Young functions $\Phi_{1}$ equivalent to $\Phi$ here so that (7) holds for some $K > 0$ (not necessarily $= 1$ ). We now present a sufficient condition on $\Phi$ , for which $\Phi \in I \cap II$ and (7) again holds for some $K > 0$ .

Example 15. (a) Let $\Phi(u) = e^{|u|} - 1$ so that $\varphi(0) = 1$ again, but $\Phi \notin \Delta_2$ , i.e., $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is not even separable. By Proposition 14, $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is a convolution algebra.

(b) Let $\Phi(u) = (e + |u|) \ln(e + |u|) - e$ . This also satisfies the hypothesis of the proposition. Since $\Phi \in \Delta_2$ (and the measure $\mu$ is separable), $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is separable, as well as closed under convolution.

The above examples show that the Young (not $N$ -) functions have some important applications in analysis, in particular for the Young class $I \cap II$ . Some further discussion, including the Beurling-Orlicz algebra, will be given later in Chapter IX.

We also can obtain the modulus of convexity (and of smoothness) for a large class of uniformly convex (and smooth) Orlicz spaces from (17) and (18). First recall that the modulus of convexity (smoothness) of a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is defined as:

$$
\delta (\mathcal {X}, \varepsilon) = \operatorname * {i n f} \left\{1 - \frac {1}{2} | | f + g | |: f, g \in S (\mathcal {X}), \| f - g \| = \varepsilon \leq 2 \right\},
$$
I. Introduction and Background Material
and

$$
\left(\rho (\mathcal {X}, \tau) = \sup  \left\{\frac {1}{2} (\| f + g \| + \| f - g \|) - 1: f \in S (\mathcal {X}), \| g \| = \tau \right\}\right).
$$
where $S(\mathcal{X}) = \{x\in \mathcal{X}:\| x\| = 1\}$ is the unit sphere of $\mathcal{X}$ .

The space $\mathcal{X}$ is uniformly convex (smooth) if $\delta(\mathcal{X}, \varepsilon) > 0$ for every $0 < \varepsilon \leq 2$ (if $\lim_{\tau \downarrow 0} \frac{1}{\tau} \rho(\mathcal{X}, \tau) = 0$ ). It can be verified that the uniform smoothness condition here is equivalent to uniform Fréchet differentiability of $S(\mathcal{X})$ .

Remark. It is known for a long time that between any two $L^p$ spaces one can construct an Orlicz space $L^{\Phi}$ where the $N$ -function $\Phi$ is not $\Delta_2$ -regular so that the space is not reflexive (cf., e.g., Krasnoselskii and Rutickii [1], pp. 28-29). On the other hand, the above corollary implies that for any $N$ -function $\Phi$ one can find uniformly convex and smooth Orlicz spaces, namely $L^{\Phi_s}(\mu), 0 < s \leq 1$ , between the given $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ and the Hilbert function space $L^2(\mu)$ . This shows the vastness of the class of Orlicz spaces in comparison with the Lebesgue classes. The interpolation Theorem 6, a useful specialization of the Orlicz space extension of the Riesz-Thorin convexity theorem given as Theorem 2, reveals these noteworthy geometrical facts.

Bibliographical notes. As stated at the beginning, this chapter contains a summary of the results from the general theory of Orlicz spaces, and we concentrated mostly on $N$ -functions which will be adequate for many applications. We included the details of the results which are not readily available in our companion volume [1]. Here we treated the quantitative indices at some length because of their use in the following geometric applications, but the analysis is also important in obtaining some finer aspects of Orlicz spaces with the Young functions. The generalized Clarkson inequalities, as applications of the interpolation theory, given in the text are adapted from the recent work due to Ren [3], which when specialized to the $L^p$ -spaces have a simpler
1.4 Interpolation results on Orlicz spaces

proof of the classical work. [However, the classical work did not use interpolation theory and hence involved longer computations and special techniques.] We also included some aspects of the (general) Young functions which are not $N$ -functions in such studies as the convolution algebras. More on these will be given in the last chapter. Most of the references and due credits to the work are already pointed out in the text at appropriate places. We now proceed to the stated theoretical and other applications.
I. Introduction and Background Material
Chapter II
Nonsquare and von Neumann-Jordan Constants

The following work is devoted to calculating the nonsquare and von Neumann-Jordan constants, defined for any Banach space, specialized to Orlicz spaces based on $N$ -functions. The generalized concepts allow a finer analysis of the geometric properties of Orlicz spaces using the quantitative indices introduced and detailed in Chapter I. Thus the first section contains the abstract concepts. Sections 2 and 3 are devoted to obtaining the lower and upper bounds with the respective concepts. Finally Section 4 treats the von Neumann-Jordan constants of Orlicz spaces and gives exact values for certain intermediate spaces.
2.1 Introduction

We first recall various concepts and immediate consequences related to an arbitrary Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ and find specialized conditions for them when $\mathcal{X}$ is an Orlicz space. The latter work will be useful for concrete applications. As usual, let $S(\mathcal{X}) = \{x\in \mathcal{X}:\| x\| = 1\}$ denote the unit sphere of $\mathcal{X}$ . Following James [1], $\mathcal{X}$ is termed uniformly nonsquare if there is a constant $0 < c < 1$ such that for $x,y\in S(\mathcal{X})$

It is known that (cf. James [1], or Beauzamy [1], pp. 256-261) a uniformly nonsquare $\mathcal{X}$ is reflexive. In view of this property, it is useful to introduce the following concept, as was done by Gao and Lau [1]:
Definition 1. For a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ , the parameter $J(\mathcal{X})$ is termed a nonsquare constant (in the sense of James), where

From (1) and (2) it is easy to deduce that (cf. Gao and Lau [1]) $\mathcal{X}$ is uniformly nonsquare iff $J(\mathcal{X}) < 2$ . On the other hand, James [1] has already shown that the following holds (in this terminology):
Proposition 2. If $\mathcal{X}$ is a uniformly convex Banach space, then $J(\mathcal{X}) < 2$ , (so it is uniformly nonsquare).
Proof. Recall that the modulus of convexity of a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is defined as:

$$
\delta (\mathcal {X}, \varepsilon) = \inf  \left\{1 - \frac {1}{2} \| x + y \|: x, y \in S (\mathcal {X}), \| x - y \| \geq \varepsilon \right\}, \varepsilon > 0,
$$
and $\mathcal{X}$ is uniformly convex if $\delta(\mathcal{X}, \varepsilon) > 0$ for $0 < \varepsilon \leq 2$ . Thus fix $0 < \varepsilon_0 < 2$ , and if $\|x - y\| < \varepsilon_0$ , then
If, on the other hand $\| x - y\| \geq \varepsilon_0$ , then by hypothesis $\delta (\mathcal{X},\varepsilon_0) > 0$ , so
From (2) - (4), it follows that $J(\mathcal{X}) < 2$ .

Remark. Gao and Lau [2] noted that for any Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ , $J(\mathcal{X}) = \sup \{\varepsilon > 0 : \delta(\mathcal{X}, \varepsilon) \leq 1 - \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\}$ holds. On the other hand, Schäffer [1] termed $\mathcal{X}$ uniformly nonsquare if there exists $a > 1$ such that

$$
\max  (\| x + y \|, \| x - y \|) \geq a, \quad x, y \in S (\mathcal {X}),
$$
and introduced a nonsquare constant $g(\mathcal{X})$ as:

$$
g (\mathcal {X}) = \inf  \left\{\max  \left(\| x + y \|, \| x - y \|): x, y \in S (\mathcal {X}) \right\}. \right.
$$

But then Gao and Lau [1] have shown that if $\dim(\mathcal{X}) \geq 2$ , then $1 \leq g(\mathcal{X}) \leq \sqrt{2} \leq J(\mathcal{X}) \leq 2$ and $g(\mathcal{X})J(\mathcal{X}) = 2$ . Hence $g(\mathcal{X}) > 1$ iff $J(\mathcal{X}) < 2$ so that $\mathcal{X}$ is uniformly nonsquare in the sense of James. Thus both definitions of James and Schäffer are equivalent.

2.2 Lower bounds for $J(L^{(\Phi)})$ and $c_{NJ}(L^{\Phi})$

In this and the following sections, we obtain lower and upper bounds for the nonsquare and von Neumann-Jordan constants for a fairly large class of Orlicz spaces. Then in Section 4 we obtain exact values of these constants for a certain class of reflexive Orlicz spaces, namely the special interpolation spaces considered in the last chapter which however properly contain the corresponding Lebesgue spaces. Thus we start with an $N$ -function $\Phi$ and the $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ (with both gauge and Orlicz norms) on a diffuse $\sigma$ -finite or counting $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ often taking $\Omega = [0,1]$ or $\Omega = \mathbb{R}^{+}$ or $(\Omega = \mathbb{N})$ and $\mu$ as Lebesgue (or a counting) measure to simplify writing. Bibliographical notes. From the classical Jordan-von Neumann [1] characterization of a Hilbert space by the parallelogram identity $\|f + g\|^2 + \|f - g\|^2 = 2(\|f\|^2 + \|g\|^2), f, g \in \mathcal{X}$ , Clarkson [2] introduced the constant $c_{NJ}(\mathcal{X})$ of a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ to measure its "departure" from Hilbert space. Based on the work of James [1], Gao and Lau [1] introduced the nonsquare constant $J(\mathcal{X})$ for a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ . The relationship between these constants is given in Theorem 1.5, due to T. F. Wang (communicated to the second author in October 1997 and included here with his permission). The work of Section 2.3 is generally known (cf., e.g., Chen [1]) since the two definitions of uniform nonsquareness, due to R. C. James and J. J. Schäffer, are equivalent. Here the results are presented with a somewhat different type of arguments. Sections 2.2 and 2.4 are adapted from Ren [4]. The computations of the coefficients $c_{NJ}(L^{\Phi_s}(\Omega))$ are included for comparison.
II. Nonsquare and von Neumann-Jordan Constants
Chapter III
Normal Structure and WCS Coefficients

This chapter is also devoted to some geometric properties of Orlicz spaces, specialized from the general Banach spaces, to be used in concrete applications. The results include normal structure and WCS coefficients of Orlicz spaces, and their relations with the quantitative indices of these spaces considered in the preceding chapter, using both the gauge and Orlicz norms. The work refines and extends the corresponding results of earlier authors.
3.1 Introduction

The concept of normal structure was introduced by Brodskii and Milman[1] to describe the centers of convex sets and fixed points of isometries in normed linear spaces. Later Kirk[1] has shown that a weakly compact convex subset of a normed linear space with normal structure has the fixed point property for nonexpansive mappings. This led to an extended study of Banach spaces with normal structure. Then Gillespie and Williams[1] introduced the uniform normal structure concept and soon after Bynum[1] and Maluta[1] defined certain other geometric coefficients for these spaces and analyzed some of the interrelations between them. We recall these concepts and then present the relevant abstract results to specialize and refine them in the context of Orlicz spaces.
Definition 1. The normal structure coefficient $N(\mathcal{X})$ of a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ , is defined as:
where $d(A) = \sup \{\| x - y\| :x,y\in A\}$ is the diameter, and $r(A)$ is the relative Chebyshev radius of $A$ , namely,

$$
r (A) = \inf  \left\{\sup  \left\{\| x - y \|: x \in A \right\}: y \in \bar {c o} (A) \right\},
$$
$\overline{\varpi} (A)$ being the closed convex hull of $A$

Since $1 \leq N(\mathcal{X}) \leq 2$ , one says that $\mathcal{X}$ has a uniform normal structure provided $N(\mathcal{X}) > 1$ . Some basic properties of $N(\mathcal{X})$ can be presented as follows:
Theorem 2. (i)(Maluta[1]) If $\mathcal{X}$ is nonreflexive, then $N(\mathcal{X}) = 1$ , and if $\mathcal{X}$ is infinite dimensional, then $N(\mathcal{X}) \leq \sqrt{2}$ .

Proof. To get a feeling for these concepts, we sketch a proof of the results.

(i) (After Amir[1]). It is known that in a nonreflexive Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ , there exists, for each $\varepsilon > 0$ , a sequence $\{x_n, n \geq 1\}$ such that for each $m$ and $y' \in co\{x_1, \dots, x_m\}$ , $y'' \in co\{x_i, i \geq m + 1\}$ one has $1 - \varepsilon < \| y' - y'' \| < 1 + \varepsilon$ , (cf., e.g., Milman and Milman[1]). If $A = co\{x_n, n \geq 1\}$ , then $d(A) \leq 1 + \varepsilon$ and $r(A) \geq 1 - \varepsilon$ . Hence, $1 \leq N(\mathcal{X}) \leq \frac{1 + \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon}$ so that $N(\mathcal{X}) = 1$ since $\varepsilon > 0$ is arbitrary.

Regarding the second inequality, let $d(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}) = \inf \left\{\| T\| \| T^{-1}\| : T : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}, \text{ an isomorphism onto } \right\}$ , the "Banach-Mazur distance" between a pair of Banach spaces. Then

On the other hand, by a known result of Dvoretzky's, if $\dim \mathcal{X} = +\infty$ for each $\varepsilon > 0$ and integer $n > 0$ one can find a subspace $\mathcal{Y} \subset \mathcal{X}$ such that $\mathcal{Y}$ is isomorphic to the Euclidean space $l_n^2$ of dimension $n$ , and $d(\mathcal{Y}, l_n^2) \leq 1 + \varepsilon$ . Thus taking $\mathcal{Y} = \mathcal{Y}_{\varepsilon}$ here and noting that $N(l_n^2) = \sqrt{\frac{2(n+1)}{n}}$ (cf. Routledge[1]), we get from (3)

$$
Since $\varepsilon$ and $n$ are arbitrary, this implies $N(\mathcal{X})\leq \sqrt{2}$

We present a few other results, without proofs, that will be analyzed in the context of Orlicz spaces later on. Recall that $\mathcal{X}$ is said to have the fixed point property if for each closed, bounded convex set $\emptyset \neq K \subset \mathcal{X}$ and mapping $T: K \to K$ satisfying $\| T^n x - T^n y \| \leq \gamma \| x - y \|, x, y \in K, n \geq 1$ , then $T$ has a fixed point in $K$ . Here $T$ is also called a $\gamma$ -Lipschitz mapping.

Theorem 5. (Casini and Maluta[1]) If $\mathcal{X}$ satisfies $N(\mathcal{X}) > 1$ , then it has the fixed point property for $\gamma$ -Lipschitz mappings, satisfying $\gamma < \sqrt{N(\mathcal{X})}$ .
Recall that $\mathcal{X}$ has Schur property iff weak and strong convergences are equal (as in the case of the classical $\ell^1$ ).
Definition 6. (Bynum[1]) For a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ without the Schur property, the weak convergent sequence (WCS) coefficient is defined as:

$$
W C S (\mathcal {X}) = \inf  \left\{\frac {A (\{x _ {i} \})}{r (\{x _ {i} \})}: \left\{x _ {i}, i \geq 1 \right\} \subset \mathcal {X} \text {i s w e a k l y b u t} \right.
$$
where $A(\{x_i\})$ and $r(\{x_i\})$ are the asymptotic diameter and the relative Chebyshev radius with respect to $\overline{co}(\{x_i\})$ .
It is seen that $1 \leq WCS(\mathcal{X}) \leq 2$ , and $\mathcal{X}$ is said to have weakly uniformly normal structure when $WCS(\mathcal{X}) > 1$ .

$$

Maluta[1] proved that $D(\mathcal{X}) = 0$ iff $\dim \mathcal{X} < \infty$ ; $\frac{1}{2} \leq D(\mathcal{X}) \leq 1$ if $\dim \mathcal{X} = \infty$ , and $D(\mathcal{X}) = 1$ for nonreflexive $\mathcal{X}$ .

Bibliographical notes. Wang and Shi [1], and Chen and Sun [1] studied uniformly normal structures of Orlicz spaces for the Orlicz and gauge norms respectively. Inequality (12) in Theorem 2.3 is proved by T, F. Wang (communicated to Ren). The WCS coefficients of Orlicz sequence spaces satisfying $\triangle_2(0)$ -condition were considered by Dominguez Benavides and Rodriguez[1]. Most of the results with gauge norm are taken from Ren ([2], [4]) and Yan [1]. The corresponding results for the Orlicz norm have not appeared in print.
III. Normal Structure and WCS Coefficients
Chapter IV
Jung Constants of Orlicz Spaces

In studying the geometric properties of Banach spaces, W. Jung [1] introduced in 1901 certain invariants, for Banach spaces, with values in $[\frac{1}{2}, 1]$ , and calculated their values for finite dimensional Euclidean spaces. Later several authors found the exact values for certain $L^p$ and other spaces. In fact some workers in the subject think that "estimation of Jung constants is one of the directions of research of the geometric theory of normed spaces". This chapter, like the preceding ones, is devoted to results on Jung constants for Orlicz spaces under both the Orlicz and gauge norms. Also for a class of intermediate Orlicz spaces exact values are calculated, and for general Orlicz spaces lower and upper estimates are obtained.
4.1 Introduction to Jung constants

This parameter of a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is defined in terms of the ratio of the Chebyshev radius and the diameter of a set in $\mathcal{X}$ . Recall that for a set $A \subset \mathcal{X}$ , the diameter of $A$ is $d(A) = \sup \{\|x - y\| : x, y \in A\}$ and if $A, B \subset \mathcal{X}$ are sets then the relative Chebyshev radius $r(A, B) = \inf \{\sup [ \|x - z\| : x \in A] : z \in B\}$ , and the (absolute) Chebyshev radius of $A$ is $r(A, \mathcal{X})$ . By definition $r(A, B) = r(\overline{\omega}(A), B)$ and $r(A, \mathcal{X}) = r(\overline{\omega}(A), \mathcal{X})$ , where $\overline{\omega}(A)$ is the closed convex hull of $A$ .
The following concept for a normed linear space was introduced by Jung [1] in 1901 and it was termed by Grünbaum [1], the 'Jung constant':
Definition 1. The Jung constant of a normed linear space $X$ , $JC(X)$ is defined as:

$$
J C (X) = \sup  \left\{\frac {r (A , X)}{d (A)}: A \subset X, b o u n d e d, d (A) > 0 \right\}. \qquad (1)
$$
The definition implies $\frac{1}{2} \leq JC(X) \leq 1$ , and in some works (cf., e.g., Amir
[1], Franchetti and Semenov [1]) $2JC(X)$ is termed the Jung constant. Jung [1] showed that

where $l_{n}^{2}$ is the Euclidean $n$ -space. He also showed for an arbitrary $n$ -dimensional normed linear space $X_{n}$ , $JC(X_{n}) = \frac{1}{2}$ iff $X_{n} = l_{n}^{\infty}$ . Also Bohnenblust [1] established that $JC(X_{n}) \leq \frac{n}{n + 1}$ for any $n$ -dimensional normed linear space $X_{n}$ . For some infinite dimensional spaces the following results are available: $JC(l^{2}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (Routledge [1]), $JC(L^{\infty}[0,1]) = JC(l^{\infty}) = \frac{1}{2}$ and $JC(L^{1}[0,1]) = JC(l^{1}) = JC(c_{0}) = JC(C(T)) = 1$ where $T$ is a compact Hausdorff space, without isolated points (cf., Berdyshev [1], Papini [1] and Amir [1]). Davis [1] showed that $JC(X) = \frac{1}{2}$ iff $X$ is a $B_{1}$ -space, i.e., a space which is norm one complemented in every super space (cf., Franchetti [1]). Obtaining lower and upper bounds respectively by Berdyshev [1] and Pichugov [1], the following result was deduced:

For $L^p(\Omega)$ with $\Omega = [0,1]$ or $\mathbf{R}^{+}, 1 < p < \infty$ , (3) will be reproved by using Orlicz space results in Section 3. Indeed computing exact values $JC(X)$ for different normed linear spaces $X$ is a nontrivial task.
We start with the following general result:
Proposition 2 (Amir [1]). If $\mathcal{X} = Y^{*}$ , the dual space of a normed linear space $Y$ , then

Proof. Let $A \subset \mathcal{X}$ be a bounded set with $d(A) = 1$ . Then the Chebyshev radius $r(A, \mathcal{X}) \leq d(A) = 1$ and so for each $0 < a < r(A, \mathcal{X})$ the closed balls $\{B(x, a), x \in A\}$ cover $A$ where $B(x, a) = \{y \in \mathcal{X} : \| y - x \| \leq a\}$ and at least a pair of balls must be disjoint. In particular $\bigcap_{x \in A} B(x, a) = \emptyset$ . But by the Alaoglu-Birkhoff-Kakutani theorem each $B(x, a)$ is weak*-compact (since $\mathcal{X} = Y^*$ ) and hence there is a finite subset $A_1 = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\} \subset A$ such that $\bigcap_{x_i \in A_1} B(x_i, a) = \emptyset$ . This means each $r(A, \mathcal{X})$ can be approximated by $r(A_1, \mathcal{X})$ since $a, (0 < a < r(A, \mathcal{X}))$ is arbitrary. So using this in (1), one gets (4).

Definition 6. An Hadamard (Jacques Hadamard, 1865-1963) matrix of order $(n + 1)$ is a square matrix $H_{(n + 1)\times (n + 1)}$ such that (i) its elements consist of 1 and $-1$ , and (ii) the elements of any pair of rows or columns coincide precisely for half the number of positions.
If $H$ is an Hadamard matrix of order $(n + 1)$ , then

$$
\left( \begin{array}{c c} {H} & {H} \\ {H} & {- H} \end{array} \right)
$$

is a similar one of order $2(n + 1)$ . It is suspected, but not yet established, that there exists Hadamard matrix of order $n + 1$ if $n + 1 = 4m$ for some integer $m$ . Such matrices have been constructed for many values of $m$ , in particular for $1 \leq m \leq 67$ (cf., e.g., Golomb and Baumert [1]). If $n + 1 = 2^k$ , then $H_{(n + 1) \times (n + 1)}$ exists for all $k \geq 2$ . An Hadamard matrix $H_{(n + 1) \times (n + 1)}$ is said to be in normal form if its first row and column consist of 1s. We denote by $H_{n \times (n + 1)}$ by removing the first row of $H_{(n + 1) \times (n + 1)}$ . The following example $H_{4 \times 4}$ illustrates its use for Jung's constants.

$$

IV Jung Constants of Orlicz Spaces
Chapter V
Packing in Orlicz Spaces

The structure of a unit ball in Orlicz sequence and general space is revealed, in the sense of its geometry, when one attaches an invariant for each space (as in the preceding chapters), and finds exact bounds (or best inequalities) for them. After introducing the relevant concepts and immediate consequences in the next section, we study the problems for Orlicz sequence spaces in Section 2, and then consider the corresponding problems for the spaces on the Lebesgue measure triples in the following section. Finally, Section 4 refines some of these results when the spaces are reflexive.
5.1 Preliminaries
In a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ , a sequence of balls with centers $x_{1}, x_{2}, \dots$ and a fixed radius $r > 0$ is said to be packed into the unit ball if the following two conditions hold:

list_items: (ii) $\| x_{i} - x_{j}\| \geq 2r,i\neq j,i,j = 1,2,\dots$

text: With this concept, one can associate a constant (or an invariant) to $\mathcal{X}$ as:
Definition 1. A packing constant $P(\mathcal{X})$ of a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is

$$
P (\mathcal {X}) = \sup  \{\quad r > 0: \text {i n f i n i t e l y m a n y b a l l s o f r a d i u s} r \text {} \\ \text {a r e p a c k e d i n t o t h e u n i t b a l l o f} \mathcal {X} \}.
$$
Thus if $\mathcal{X}$ is of finite dimension, then $P(\mathcal{X}) = 0$ . If $\mathcal{X}$ is an infinite dimensional Hilbert space, then Rankin [1] established

Definition 2. For an infinite dimensional Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ , the Kottman constant of $\mathcal{X}$ is defined as

$$
K (\mathcal {X}) = \sup  \left\{\inf  _ {i \neq j} \| x _ {i} - x _ {j} \|: \{x _ {i} \} _ {1} ^ {\infty} \subset S (\mathcal {X}) \right\},
$$
where $S(\mathcal{X})$ is the unit sphere of $\mathcal{X}$ .
Clearly, $1 \leq K(\mathcal{X}) \leq 2$ . The following relationship between $P(\mathcal{X})$ and $K(\mathcal{X})$ was noted by Kottman [1] (cf. also Ye [1]).

Remark 4. (i) Elton and Odell [1] proved that for each infinite dimensional Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ there exist an $\varepsilon >0$ and a sequence $\{x_{i}:i\geq 1\} \subset S(\mathcal{X})$ such that $\| x_{i} - x_{j}\| \geq 1 + \varepsilon$ for $i\neq j$ , so that $K(\mathcal{X}) > 1$ for all infinite dimensional spaces. This fact and (5) imply that for such a space, $P(\mathcal{X})\geq \frac{1 + \varepsilon}{2 + (1 + \varepsilon)} >\frac{1}{3}$ since $\phi (x)$ is increasing and $\geq \frac{1}{3}$ .
(ii) For an infinite dimensional Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ , we therefore have: $1 < a \leq K(\mathcal{X}) \leq b \leq 2$ , iff

$$
\frac {1}{1 + \frac {2}{a}} \leq P (\mathcal {X}) \leq \frac {1}{1 + \frac {2}{b}}.
$$

Cleaver [2] found upper and lower bounds for $P(l^{\Phi})$ under certain conditions, which however were found to be restrictive, and in a review Zaanen observed that they are satisfied only for the $l^p$ spaces. Later Ye [1] and
5.1 Preliminaries

Wang [1] investigated $P(l^{(\Phi)})$ and $P(l^{\Phi})$ respectively, under weaker conditions. Somewhat later Wang and Liu [1] generalized these results for certain more inclusive sequence spaces with simpler formulas for $K(\mathcal{X})$ . The following two results from their works will be used in the sequel.
Theorem 5. (Ye [1])

list_items: (i) $\Phi \notin \triangle_2(0) \Rightarrow K(l^{(\Phi)}) = 2$ (or $P(l^{(\Phi)}) = 1/2$ ).
(ii) $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0)\Rightarrow K(l^{(\Phi)}) = \sup_{\| f\|_{(\Phi)} = 1}\left\{c > 0:\rho_\Phi (f_c) = \frac{1}{2}\right\} .$

text: A proof of (i) is in the lecture notes of Orlicz ([1], p.180) and of (ii) is in Wang and Liu [1], as well as in Chen ([1], p.149).
Theorem 6. (Wang [1])

list_items: (i) $\Phi \not\subset \triangle_2(0)\Rightarrow K(l^\Phi) = 2$ (or $P(l^{\Phi}) = 1 / 2$
(ii) $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0)\Rightarrow K(l^\Phi) = \sup_{||f||_\Phi = 1}\left\{\inf_{k > 1}\left[c > 0:\rho_\Phi (\frac{k f}{c}) = \frac{k - 1}{2}\right]\right\} .$

text: A proof of this theorem is again in Wang and Liu [1], as well as in Chen ([1], pp.149-151).

Remark 7. (i) Ye and Li [1] (and Wang [1]) showed that $l^{(\Phi)}[l^{\Phi}]$ is reflexive iff $P(l^{(\Phi)}) < 1/2[P(l^{\Phi}) < 1/2]$ . The necessity of these assertions can be proved using the concept of $p$ -convexity. Recall that a Banach space is $p$ -convex if there exist $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 3$ such that for all $\{x_i, 1 \leq i \leq n_0\} \subset S(\mathcal{X})$ , one has

A $p$ -convex Banach space is known to be reflexive (cf. Kottman [1]), and if $\mathcal{X}$ is $p$ -convex then $K(\mathcal{X}) < 2$ so that $P(\mathcal{X}) < 1/2$ , by (5) [see also Chen ([1], Theorems 3.30, 3.45 and 3.46)]. From a result we show in the next section, the reflexivity of $l^{(\Phi)}[l^{\Phi}]$ implies $P(l^{(\Phi)}) < 1/2[P(l^{\Phi}) < 1/2]$ using the corresponding estimation for the Kottman constants (see Corollary 2.4).

(ii) Hudzik [2] showed that if $\mathcal{X}$ is a nonreflexive Banach lattice then $K(\mathcal{X}) = 2$ and thus $P(\mathcal{X}) = 1/2$ , by (5). Since $l^{(\Phi)}$ and $l^{\Phi}$ are lattices, this implies the desired conclusion when $\Phi \notin \triangle_2(0) \cap \nabla_2(0)$ .

5.3 Packing in Orlicz function spaces

Bounds for the packing constants of Orlicz spaces $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ , with Orlicz norm, were considered in Cleaver [2], and the corresponding results, with the gauge norm, have been obtained by Ren in 1985 and included in our companion volume ([1], pp.256-258). A more detailed discussion appeared in Ren [1]. We start here with lower bounds of $K(L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega))$ and $P(L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega))$ where $\Omega = [0,1]$ or $\pmb{R}^{+}$ with Lebesgue measure.

5.4 Packing in reflexive Orlicz spaces

The main purpose of this section is to establish that the packing constant of reflexive Orlicz space $L^{\Phi}(\Omega)[L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega)]$ is strictly less than half. We begin with the case of the Orlicz norm.

Corollary 2. If $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ so that $L^{\Phi}(\pmb{R}^{+})$ is reflexive, then it is $p(3)$ -convex. Consequently $K(L^{\Phi}(\pmb{R}^{+})) < 2$ and $P(L^{\Phi}(\pmb{R}^{+})) < \frac{1}{2}$ .

For the gauge norm, Ye, He and Pluciennik ([1], Th.2) and Kolwicz [1] established the following:
Theorem 3. Let $\Omega$ be $[0,1]$ or $\pmb{R}^{+}$ . If $L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega)$ is reflexive, then it is $p(3)$ -convex so that $K(L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega)) < 2$ and $P(L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega)) < \frac{1}{2}$ .
By Corollary 3.2 (ii) and 3.4(ii) along with the above results we can state the following:

Theorem 4. Let $\Phi$ be an $N$ -function and $\mathcal{X}_{\Phi}$ be any one of the spaces $L^{\Phi}(\Omega)$ or $L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega)$ , where $\Omega = [0,1]$ or $\pmb{R}^{+}$ with Lebesgue measure. Then $\mathcal{X}_{\Phi}$ is reflexive iff $K(\mathcal{X}_{\Phi}) < 2$ or equivalently $P(\mathcal{X}_{\Phi}) < \frac{1}{2}$ .

Bibliographical notes. Rankin [1] was the first to introduce the packing constant. Theorem 1.3 is essentially due to Kottman [1], but formula (7) there was given by Ye ([1], Lemma 1). The results of Section 2 are taken form the authors [2] and Yan [1]. Sections 3 and 4 are adapted form Ren [1], and are a completion of Cleaver's [2] work for function spaces. We now proceed to other applications of Orlicz spaces to an analysis on Fourier series among others.
5.4 Packing in reflexive Orlicz spaces
Chapter VI
Fourier Analysis in Orlicz Spaces

This chapter presents an account of the convergence of Fourier series in Orlicz spaces on compact abelian groups, and especially contains an analysis of conjugate functions when the underlying space is a circle (torus) group $G$ . We start with $G = [0,2\pi]$ and present Ryan's theorem that the conjugate function mapping on $L^{\Phi}(G)$ is bounded iff the space is reflexive. Then we extend the result for certain subspaces (especially $M^{\Phi}$ determined by trigonometric polynomials) in $L^{\Phi}(G)$ for a general class of $N$ -functions, $\Phi$ , and then give some extensions of these results for more general groups $G$ . Only brief accounts of the latter can be included, since the necessary auxiliary results and applications are too numerous, demanding separate book length treatments.

6.2 Conjugate functions and Orlicz spaces

Since $L^{\Phi}(\Omega)$ is a subspace of $L^{1}(\Omega)$ and hence for each $f \in L^{\Phi}(\Omega)$ , whose Fourier series is well-defined, it is natural to study the conjugates $\tilde{f}$ of such $f$ . We first recall the concepts of a conjugate function, and proceed to discuss some properties, and then to prove the converse of Theorem 1.3.
VI. Fourier Analysis in Orlicz Spaces
Definition 1. For any $f \in L^{\Phi}(\Omega) \subset L^{1}(\Omega), \Omega = [0,2\pi)$ , the conjugate $\tilde{f}$ of $f$ is given by
It is a basic result in the subject that the conjugate function $\widetilde{f}$ of (1) exists a.e., and $\widetilde{f}$ is also given by

$$
\widetilde{f} (x) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0}\left\{-\frac{1}{\pi}\int \limits_{\varepsilon \leq |x|\leq \pi}\frac{f(x + t)}{2\tan\frac{t}{2}} dt\right\} .
$$
(For a proof, see Zygmund [1, Vol.I, p.131].) The definition, as given, is unmotivated, but can be justified as follows. For each $f$ and $x \in \Omega$ consider its even and odd parts defined as:

$$
(e v e n) \quad e _ {x} (t) = \frac {1}{2} [ f (x + t) + f (x - t) ]; \quad (o d d) \quad o _ {x} (t) = \frac {1}{2} [ f (x + t) - f (x - t) ],
$$

so that $f(x + t) = e_{x}(t) + o_{x}(t)$ . If $f$ is integrable, $S[f](t) = \sum_{n = -\infty}^{\infty}c_ne^{int}$ and $\tilde{S}[f](t) = -i\sum_{n = -\infty}^{\infty}sgn(n)c_ne^{int}$ ; thus $\tilde{S}[f]$ is the complex conjugate of $S[f]$ . The behaviors of $S[f]$ and the conjugate series $\widetilde{S}[f]$ are crucially dependent on the properties of $e_{x}$ and $o_{x}$ at $t = 0$ . In fact, if $\intop_{0}^{0}|o_{x}(t)|\frac{1}{t} dt < \infty$ , then one can show that the series $\widetilde{S}[f]$ converges at $x$ to $\widetilde{f}(x)$ , the conjugate of $f(x)$ (see Wheeden and Zygmund [1, Ch.12] for a quick view of these results). Thus if $f\in L^{1}(\Omega)$ , so that $S[f]$ exists and the conjugate series $\widetilde{S}[f]$ is the Fourier series of some function $g\in L^{1}(\Omega)$ , then $g(= \widetilde{f})$ is the conjugate function of $f$ . The following theorem, due to M. Riesz (cf., Zygmund [1, Vol.I, p.253]), which will be used in the following, explains the concept further.

so that the mapping $T: f \mapsto \tilde{f}$ is a bounded linear operator on $L^p(\Omega)$ .

Definition 3. For each measurable function $f: \Omega \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$ , let

$$
m _ {f} (\lambda) = \mu \{x \in \Omega : | f (x) | > \lambda \}.

Then $m_{f}(\cdot)$ is termed the measure function of $f$ determined by $\mu$ .

If $\mu(\Omega) = 1$ and $F_{f}(\lambda) = 1 - m_{f}(\lambda)$ , then $F_{f}(\cdot)$ is called the distribution function of $f$ in Probability Theory. Unfortunately some times in the literature, especially in trigonometric series, $m_{f}(\cdot)$ is also termed a distribution, and we shall avoid this conflict by calling it just a measure function. text: VI. Fourier Analysis in Orlicz Spaces

We now conclude this section by the following observation on pointwise convergence, which is a more delicate problem. Indeed, in 1876 du Bois-Reymond constructed a continuous function with a divergent Fourier series at a point, and extension of his work shows that the same is true for a dense set of points in $[0,2\pi)$ , which however has Lebesgue measure zero. Then in 1915 Luzin conjectured that the Fourier series converges a.e. for each $f \in L^2[0,2\pi)$ . In 1926 Kolmogorov showed that there exist $f \in L^1[0,2\pi)$ whose Fourier series diverge a.e.. Then in 1966 Carleson [1] finally proved Luzin's conjecture, namely that the Fourier series of every $f \in L^2[0,2\pi)$ converges a.e. His proof was extended by Hunt [1] to cover all functions $f \in L^p[0,2\pi), 1 < p < \infty$ . Thus we have the final result as:
Theorem 14. The Fourier series of $f \in L^{p}[0,2\pi), 1 < p < \infty$ , converges a.e. (and this is known to be false both for $p = 1$ (Kolmogorov) and $p = \infty$ ).
Corollary 15. If $\Phi \in \nabla_2(\infty)$ is an $N$ -function, then the Fourier series of $f \in L^{\Phi}[0,2\pi)$ converges a.e., and also in mean if further $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ .
Proof. By definition, $\Phi \in \nabla_2(\infty)$ implies the existence of $a > 1$ and $u_0 > 0$ such that

$$
\left(\frac {u}{u _ {0}}\right) ^ {a} \leq \frac {\Phi (u)}{\Phi (u _ {0})}, u \geq u _ {0},
$$

(cf.(9)). Then it follows that $L^{\Phi}[0,2\pi) \subset L^{a}[0,2\pi)$ , the inclusion being continuous, i.e., $\| f\|_{a} \leq C\| f\|_{\Phi}$ for some $C > 0$ . Hence Theorem 14 implies the a.e. convergence assertion. The last statement in mean convergence follows from Corollary 9.

The purpose of this section is to present a few results on Fourier series, based on the conjugate functions for certain elements in Orlicz spaces, continuing the discussion of the preceding section. We give the next result as a motivation for (possible) further work in this area.

Proposition 1. Let $\Phi_0(u) = c_1u^2$ and $\Phi_2 = c_2|u|^p, 1 < p < \infty$ . Pick now an $N$ -function $\Phi$ satisfying $\Phi_1 \prec \Phi \prec \Phi_0$ or $\Phi_0 \prec \Phi \prec \Phi_1$ . If $\mathcal{M}^\Phi$ is the closed subspace generated by the simple functions (i.e., the Morse-Transue subspace) of $L^\Phi(\mu)$ , then for each $f \in \mathcal{M}^\Phi$ , its conjugate $\tilde{f}$ exists and $\tilde{f} \in \mathcal{M}^\Phi$ . Moreover, there is a constant $K_\Phi$ depending only on $\Phi$ such that

We use this result only when $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ so that $\mathcal{M}^{\Phi} = L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ and it is reflexive. In this event (4) and the proposition itself is a special case of Theorems 1.3 and 2.7, due to Ryan[1]. The case when $\Phi$ does not satisfy this growth condition, which is possible (cf. Krasnoselskii and Rutickii[1], p.28, the example given there shows that there exist such $\Phi$ ), then the argument is long and tedious, and we shall omit it here. A possible method is to use Green's formula and extending the argument of M. Riesz's. An outline of it has been included in Rao [12], which is already long and not revealing. The result is stated here with the hope that some simple proof

We now consider some results on the strong convergence of the partial sums of Fourier series of $f$ , which is related to conjugation $\tilde{f}$ of $f$ . Here we should like to unify and include the $n$ -torus case, i.e., $\Omega = [0,2\pi)^n$ . Since $f \in L^{\Phi}(\mu) \subset L^{1}(\mu)$ , $f$ has a Fourier expansion, namely, if $\hat{f}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}f(t)e^{-int}d\mu(t)$ , let
We then have:
Proposition 2. Let $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ and $f \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . Then $S_n[f] \to f$ strongly (i.e., in norm) as $n \to \infty$ .

Proof. We recall that a Banach space $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)$ contained, as a set, in $L^1 (\mu)$ , admits conjugation by definition if each $f\in \mathcal{X}$ has a conjugate $\tilde{f}$ again in $\mathcal{X}$ . By Ryan's theorem (or Prop. 1 above) $\mathcal{X} = L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ admits conjugation. Moreover, the norm $\| \cdot \| (= \| \cdot \|_{\Phi})$ has the following additional properties since it is also absolutely continuous, i.e., $\| g\chi_{A_n}\|_{\Phi}\to 0$ for any $A_{n}\downarrow \emptyset :(\mathrm{i})$ $\| fe_n\|_{\Phi} = \| f\|_{\Phi}$ for any $e_n:t\mapsto e^{int},t\in [0,2\pi) = \Omega$ , called the character of $\Omega$ ; (ii) $(\tau_hf)(t) = f(t - h)$ , $h\in \Omega$ (addition mod $(2\pi)$ ), then $\| \tau_hf\|_{\Phi} = \| f\|_{\Phi}$ ; and (iii) $\| (\tau_{h_1} - \tau_{h_2})f\|_{\Phi}\to 0$ as $h_1\rightarrow h_2$ . Any Banach space $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)$ contained in $L^1 (\mu)$ with the properties (i)-(iii) is called homogeneous, and by a general result on the structure of such spaces (cf. e.g., Katznelson [1], p.49) it admits conjugation iff it admits strong convergence for the partial sums $S_{n}[f]$ to $f$ , i.e., $\| S_n[f] - f\|_{\Phi}\to 0$ as $n\to \infty$ . [see below in Prop.3 for details of this result.] Thus the assertion for reflexive $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ spaces is a consequence of these facts.

This result in the case of $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , $1 < p < \infty$ , has been known when $\Omega$ is replaced by a compact abelian group, using methods of complex analysis (cf. Rudin [1]). We now present a few extensions to Orlicz spaces in the manner of Proposition 2 above. Let us set up the problem, in general terms, to be specialized later by adding further restrictions. Thus let $\Omega$ be a compact group with $\mu$ as its normalized Haar measure. [If $\Omega = [0,2\pi)^n$ ,
6.3 Conjugate series and convergence in subsets of Orlicz spaces
group operation being addition (mod(2π)), then $d\mu = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^n} d\omega$ .] When $\Omega$ is not necessarily abelian there is no dual group, and we proceed differently.

Let $G$ be a compact group and $\mu$ be its normalized Haar measure, i.e., $\mu(G) = 1$ . Since there are no nontrivial characters of $G$ , we define the Fourier transform on $G$ by using its unitary representations on a Hilbert space as follows. Let $U(\alpha) = \{u_{ij}^{\alpha}, i,j = 1,2,\dots,d_{\alpha}\}$ , $\alpha \in A$ , be a complete set of unitary irreducible inequivalent representations of $G$ into $L^{2}(G,\mu)$ , where $A$ is an index set and $1 \leq d_{\alpha} < \infty$ are integers ( $d_{\alpha} = 1$ iff $G$ is abelian). Also $\bar{u}_{ij}^{\alpha} = u_{ji}^{\alpha}$ and

$$
\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {d _ {\alpha}} \int_ {G} \left| u _ {i j} ^ {\alpha} (x) \right| ^ {2} d \mu (x) = 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots , d _ {\alpha}, x \in G.
$$
This is a consequence of the classical Peter-Weyl theorem. For details of unitary representations and the famous Peter-Weyl theorem, we refer the reader to Hewitt and Ross ([1], Sec. 27). Then

$$
\left\{\sqrt {d _ {\alpha}} u _ {i j} ^ {\alpha}, i, j = 1, 2, \dots , d _ {\alpha}, \alpha \in A \right\} \subset L ^ {2} (G, \mu)
$$
is a complete orthonormal set. For $f \in L^{2}(G, \mu)$ , one defines the Fourier transform $\hat{f}$ as

here the factor $\sqrt{d_{\alpha}}$ is put in for convenience, to normalize $u_{ij}^{\alpha}$ 's, and will be omitted later on. we can define the conjugate function $\tilde{f}$ of $f\in L^{p}(G,\mu)$ or $L^{\Phi}(G,\mu)$ , using the fact that $G$ has the dual group $\Gamma$ which is discrete and, what is more decisive, $\Gamma$ admits a linear ordering compatible with its topology. Namely, there is a closed semi-group $P\subset \Gamma$ such that (i) $P\cup P^{-1} = \Gamma$ , (ii) $P\cap P^{-1} = \{0\}$ , $P^{-1} = \{p^{-1}:p\in P\}$ , so that for $x,y\in \Gamma$ one can define $x\leq y$ iff $yx^{-1}(= y - x)\in P$ , (cf., e.g., Rudin ([1], p.197, 8.1.8). Using “+” for group operation and “-” for inversion (set $u_{ij}^{\alpha} = u_{\alpha}$ since $d_{\alpha} = 1$ for $\alpha \in A = \Gamma$ here), one defines a trigonometric polynomial as
and its conjugate function (also a similar one) as
Let $(\tau_y f)(x) = f(xy^{-1})$ , so $\tau_y$ is the translation operator. The desired extension of the result used in Proposition 2 above, on homogeneous Banach spaces, can be presented as:

Proposition 3. Let $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)\subset L^{1}(G,\mu)$ be a homogeneous Banach space, i.e., (i) $\| \cdot \| _1\leq \| \cdot \|$ , (ii) $\| fu\| = \| f\bar{u}\|$ $u\in \Gamma$ , the dual of $G$ (the compact abelian group), $f\in \mathcal{X}$ , and (iii) $\| \tau_yf - \tau_{y_0}f\| \to 0$ as $y\rightarrow y_0,f\in \mathcal{X}$ . Then $\mathcal{X}$ admits conjugation iff it admits convergence in norm.
Proof. For convenience, the argument is given in steps.

1. For each $f \in \mathcal{X}, \gamma \in \Gamma$ , let $\hat{f}_{\gamma} = \int_{G} f(t) \bar{\gamma}(t) d\mu(t)$ , where $\gamma \mapsto \gamma(t) = \langle t, \gamma \rangle$ is a duality mapping of $G \to C$ , so that $\langle 0, \gamma \rangle = 1 = \langle t, 0 \rangle$ , $\langle -t, \gamma \rangle = \langle t, \gamma \rangle^{-1} = \overline{\langle t, \gamma \rangle}$ , and $\langle t_1 t_2, \gamma \rangle = \langle t_1, \gamma \rangle \langle t_2, \gamma \rangle$ , $\langle t, \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 \rangle = \langle t, \gamma_1 \rangle \langle t, \gamma_2 \rangle$ for $t_1, t_2, t \in G$ and $\gamma_1, \gamma_2, \gamma \in \Gamma$ . Here the same operations "+" and "-" in both $G$ and $\Gamma$ are used. Let $f$ be a trigonometric polynomial and $\tilde{f}$ be its conjugate given by (7) and (8), so $a_{\gamma} = \tilde{f}_{\alpha}$ in their definitions. Set $F_f = \sum_{\gamma > 0} f_{\gamma}(\cdot, \gamma)$ in the above chosen ordering, so that $F_f = \frac{1}{2} (\hat{f}_0 + f + i\tilde{f})$ . We claim that the mapping $T: f \mapsto F_f$ is linear and bounded iff $\tilde{f} \in \mathcal{X}$ . To see this, note that if $\tilde{f} \in \mathcal{X}$ , then $\| \tilde{f} \| \leq C \| f \|$ by definition, whence

$$
\left\| F _ {f} \right\| \leq \frac {1}{2} \left(\left\| f \right\| + \left\| f \right\lvert + C \left\| f \right\|\right) = \frac {C + 2}{2} \| f \|
$$

and $T$ is bounded (and clearly linear) on the set of trigonometric polynomials. These are dense in $\mathcal{X}$ by condition (iii) of the definition of a homogeneous space, so that $T$ is bounded on $\mathcal{X}$ . Conversely, if $T$ is bounded, since $\tilde{f} = -i(2F_f - f - \tilde{f}_0)$ , by definition, $\| \tilde{f} \| \leq 2(\| T \| + 1) \| f \|$ , with (1) of the definition of the space $\mathcal{X}$ . Consequently, $\tilde{f} \in \mathcal{X}$ . The general case follows again by the density of trigonometric polynomials in $\mathcal{X}$ using (iii).

2. We next assert that $\mathcal{X}$ admits norm convergence iff the set of mappings $S_I: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{X}, I \in \mathcal{I}$ , is uniformly bounded, i.e., $\| S_I[f] \| \leq K_0 \| f \|$ , $f \in \mathcal{X}, I \in \mathcal{I}$ , for an absolute constant $K_0 > 0$ . In fact, by the density of trigonometric polynomials in $\mathcal{X}$ , for each $\varepsilon > 0$ and each $f \in \mathcal{X}$ there is such a polynomial $h_\varepsilon \in \mathcal{X}$ for which $\| f - h_\varepsilon \| < \varepsilon / (K_0 + 1)$ . Now $S_I[f_\varepsilon] = h_\varepsilon$ for large enough $I \in \mathcal{I}$ . Then for this $f \in \mathcal{X}, S_I[f] \to f$ in norm as $I \to \infty$ , since

$$
\left\| S _ {I} [ f ] - f \right\| \leq \left\| S _ {I} [ f ] - h _ {\varepsilon} \right\| + \left\| h _ {\varepsilon} - f \right\| \leq \left(K _ {0} + 1\right) \left\| h _ {\varepsilon} - f \right\| <   \varepsilon .
$$

Conversely, if $S_I[f] \to f$ in norm as $I \to \infty$ , then the convergence set being (pointwise) bounded, we have $\| S_I[f] \| \leq K_f$ for each $f \in \mathcal{X}$ and $I \in \mathcal{J}$ . Since $\mathcal{X}$ is a Banach space, it follows by the uniform boundedness theorem that $K_f \leq K_0 \| f \|$ for some absolute constant $K_0 > 0$ . Thus our statement is established.

3. It is now asserted that for each $f \in \mathcal{X}$ , the function $F_{f} \in \mathcal{X}$ iff $\mathcal{X}$ admits norm convergence and this will establish the proposition in one direction. Indeed, let $f \in \mathcal{X}$ and $f_{n} \in \mathcal{X}$ be a trigonometric polynomial such that $f_{n} \to f$ in norm. Since $F_{f_{n}} \in \mathcal{X}$ , it suffices to prove that $\{F_{f_n}, n \geq 1\}$ is Cauchy. Let $J_{n} = (\gamma_{1}, \dots, \gamma_{n}) \in \mathcal{J}$ , with $\gamma_{i} \geq 0$ . If $u \in \Gamma$ is arbitrary, let $H_{u}: \Gamma \to \Gamma$ be a shift defined as $H_{u}(\gamma) = \gamma + u$ . Then $H_{u}$ is a homeomorphism of $\Gamma$ onto $\Gamma$ , and if $I_{n} = H_{u}(J_{n}) \in \mathcal{J}$ , consider

For any $f \in \mathcal{X}$ and $\varepsilon > 0$ there is a trigonometric polynomial $h_\varepsilon \in \mathcal{X}$ such that $\| f - h_\varepsilon \| < \varepsilon$ and $F_n(h_\varepsilon) = h_\varepsilon$ for large enough $n$ . Thus

We record the following consequence.
6.3 Conjugate series and convergence in subsets of Orlicz spaces

Theorem 4. Let $G$ be a compact group and $f \in L^{2}(G, \mu)$ , where $\mu$ is the normalized Haar measure on $G$ . Then the partial Fourier sums $S_{I}[f]$ of $f$ converge in $L^{2}(G)$ as $I \nearrow$ under the inclusion order of the directed set $\mathcal{J}$ of all finite subsets of $A$ , the index set introduced above. If moreover $G$ is connected and abelian so that the dual $\Gamma$ has an order (relative to a certain closed maximal subgroup, used above and proved in Rudin [1], p.195) and if $S_{I}[f]$ is the (finite) partial sum of the Fourier series relative to this order, then $S_{I}[f] \to f$ for each $f \in L^{p}(G) (= L^{p}(G, \mu))$ , $1 < p < \infty$ .

Proof. The $L^2 (G,\mu)$ case is simply a consequence of the classical Peter-Weyl theorem, already noted above. In the general $L^p (G),1 < p < \infty$ case, while $G$ is now also a connected compact abelian group, it is known under this hypothesis that $\mathcal{X} = L^{p}(G)$ admits conjugation (cf. Rudin [1], Thm. 8.7.2). It is trivial to see that $\mathcal{X}$ is a homogeneous Banach space contained in $L^1 (G)$ . Since the same ordering is employed in Proposition 3, the result follows from it. $\square$

Remark. An obvious question is to extend this to reflexive Orlicz spaces $L^{\Phi}(G)$ . This needs an extension of Ryan's theorem to $L^{\Phi}(G)$ . This is the same as extending Theorem 8.7.2 from Rudin [1] to this case. However, the proof there does not easily generalize to Orlicz spaces, and a different attack is desirable.

We now include a result on the existence of an unconditional basis in an Orlicz space, due to Gaposkin ([1],[2]). Recall that a sequence $\{x_{n}, n \geq 1\}$ of elements of a separable Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is a (Schauder) basis if every $x \in \mathcal{X}$ can be expanded as $x = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} a_{i}(x) x_{i}$ for a unique coefficient sequence $a_{i}(x)$ , and the series converges (i.e., the partial sums converge) to $x$ in the norm of $\mathcal{X}$ . If the above series is unconditionally convergent to $x$ (i.e., if the indices can be permuted), then it is called an unconditional basis of $\mathcal{X}$ . If $\mathcal{X}$ has a basis, then the space is necessarily separable! For a long time it was an open problem about the existence of a (Schauder) basis for each separable Banach space, but P. Enflo in 1973 has constructed a separable $\mathcal{X}$ which does not admit a basis. It is known before that the space $C[0,1]$ of continuous functions of the closed interval $[0,1]$ with the uniform norm does not have an unconditional basis (by a result of S. Karlin's, cf., Day [1], p.77). Consequently the following result is of interest in applications.
Theorem 5.(Gaposkin [1],[2]) The Haar system of functions in $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ , with
VI. Fourier Analysis in Orlicz Spaces
Lebesgue measure, forms an unconditional basis iff $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ , so that $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ must be reflexive.

We present a short proof of this result utilizing the indices of $N$ -functions and a general result on symmetric function spaces due to Semenov [1]. The argument is from Ren [1], and illustrates a nice application of the properties of the quantitative indices of Chapter I. First we need to recall the definition of a symmetric space, which is related to the concept of decreasing rearrangement of functions.
For a real or complex measurable function $f$ on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ , if $\lambda_f(y) = \mu \{ \omega \in \Omega : |f(\omega)| > y \}$ , which is nonincreasing, its inverse $f^*$ defined by

with $\inf \{\emptyset\} = +\infty$ , is called the decreasing rearrangement of $f$ (cf. e.g., the companion volume, Rao and Ren [1], p.406 for some of its properties). Then $f_1^* \leq f_2^*$ if $|f_1| \leq |f_2|$ , and $f_1, f_2$ are equi-measurable when $\lambda_{f_1}(y) = \lambda_{f_2}(y), y > 0$ . If $(\mathcal{X}, \| \cdot \|)$ is a Banach space of measurable functions on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ , then it is symmetric whenever:
(i) $f_{2}\in \mathcal{X},|f_{1}|\leq |f_{2}|,\Rightarrow f_{1}\in \mathcal{X}$ and $\| f_1\| \leq \| f_2\|$

(ii) $|f_1|$ and $|f_2|$ are equi-measurable, $f_1 \in \mathcal{X} \Rightarrow f_2 \in \mathcal{X}$ and $\| f_1 \| = \| f_2 \|$ . Also $\mathcal{X}$ is called rearrangement invariant function space if $f \in \mathcal{X}$ , $g$ is measurable and $f^* = g^*$ , then $g \in \mathcal{X}$ and $\| f \| = \| g \|$ . It is verified that $\mathcal{X}$ is a symmetric space iff it is rearrangement invariant. Thus one sees that an Orlicz space $L^\Phi(\mu)$ is a symmetric space.
The general result that we would use is:

Theorem 6. (Semenov [1]) The Haar system is an unconditional basis in a separable symmetric function space $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)$ on $(\Omega ,\Sigma ,\mu) = ([0,1],\mathcal{B},\mu)$ with $\mu$ as the Lebesgue measure iff
where $F(t) = \|\chi_{[0,t]}\|, 0 < t \leq 1$ , is the fundamental function of $(\mathcal{X},\|\cdot\|)$ .

Proof of Theorem 5. Let $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ be reflexive, which is equivalent to
$\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ . Now the norm $F(t)$ is given by

$$
F (t) = \left\| \chi_ {[ 0, t ]} \right\| _ {(\Phi)} = \left[ \Phi^ {- 1} (\frac {1}{t}) \right] ^ {- 1}, 0 <   t \leq 1.
$$
Letting $u = (2t)^{-1}$ , we have

$$
\lim  _ {t \rightarrow 0} \inf  _ {\Phi (2 t)} \frac {F (2 t)}{F (t)} = \left[ \lim  _ {u \rightarrow 0} \sup  _ {\Phi^ {- 1} (2 u)} \frac {\Phi^ {- 1} (u)}{\Phi^ {- 1} (2 u)} \right] ^ {- 1} = \frac {1}{\beta_ {\Phi}},
$$
