But by the relations between these indices in Chapter I, we find that $1 < \beta_{\Phi}^{-1} \leq \alpha_{\Phi}^{-1} < 2$ so that (14) holds. Consequently the sufficiency part of this result follows from that of Semenov's) Theorem 6.

Conversely, suppose that the Haar system is an unconditional basis of $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ . Hence this space is separable, which implies that $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ (cf. Rao and Ren [1], Prop.III.5.2 on p.91), since the Lebesgue closed interval $[0,1]$ is a separable measure space. To see that $\Phi \in \nabla_2(\infty)$ as well, in the contrary case we have $\alpha_{\Phi} = \frac{1}{2}$ so that the second half of (14) fails, since $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ is a separable symmetric space. Thus by the corresponding part of Theorem 6, the Haar system fails to be unconditional in $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ , contradicting the hypothesis. Hence the result holds as stated. $\square$

Remark. We observe that the same result holds with Orlicz norm, since it is equivalent to the gauge norm and the unconditionality is a topological invariant. This also can be proved by a similar computation, on noting that $(L^{\Phi}[0,1],\| \cdot \|_{\Phi})$ is a symmetric space.
It is natural to ask whether the basis problem can be considered for
VI. Fourier Analysis in Orlicz Spaces

subspaces of $L^{\Phi}[0,1]$ , such as $\mathcal{M}^{\Phi}$ which is not an Orlicz space in general (it is just the Morse-Transue space). The question can be answered for certain spaces if $\Phi$ is restricted as in Proposition 1, but the method of proof is different. It depends on the Hausdorff-Young inequality. The latter has independent interest for many other applications. Hence we consider this in the context of a general compact (or locally compact abelian) group as in Proposition 3 above. The elegant interpolation method, first noted by A. Weil [1], in the $L^{\Phi}(G)$ context does not give anything new unless one finds an independent proof for one $L^{\Phi}(G)$ with $\Phi$ not a power. The classical method of Hardy and Littlewood fortunately extends, and we present a result in this way which may be of interest in other contexts.
6.4 A Hausdorff-Young theorem for Orlicz spaces

To motivate the subject, we recall that if $L^p(\mathbb{R})$ denotes the classical Lebesgue space on $\mathbb{R}$ and $f \in L^p(\mathbb{R})$ , and its Fourier transform $\hat{f}$ exists so that $\hat{f} = Tf$ , then the Hausdorff-Young theorem asserts that, for $1 \leq p \leq 2$ , $\hat{f}$ exists and satisfies the inequality

It is known that this result is false if $p > 2$ , i.e., $\hat{f}$ does not exist for some $f \in L^{p}(\Omega)$ if $p > 2$ . A quick proof of this result is obtained from the fact that $\| \hat{f} \|_{\infty} \leq \| f \|_{1}$ and (by the Plancherel theorem) $\| \hat{f} \|_{2} = \| f \|_{2}$ so that, using the Riesz convexity theorem (cf., e.g., the companion volume, Rao and Ren [1], Thm VI.3.5, suitably specialized), $\| \hat{f} \|_{q_{s}} \leq \| f \|_{p_{s}}$ where $\frac{1}{p_{s}} = \frac{1 - s}{p_{0}} + \frac{s}{p_{1}}$ , $0 \leq s \leq 1$ and here $p_{0} = 1$ , $p_{1} = 2$ and $\frac{1}{q_{s}} = \frac{1 - s}{q_{0}} + \frac{s}{q_{1}}$ with $q_{0} = +\infty$ , $q_{1} = 2$ . But this procedure does not bring in any Orlicz space that is not a Lebesgue space. So it is necessary to go back to one of the methods, not depending on interpolation theory. We extend a result of Hardy and Littlewood [1] and obtain an inequality for a genuine Orlicz space, and then present some applications. We use the partial ordering $\prec'$ between the Young functions $\Phi_{1}, \Phi_{2}$ i.e., $\Phi_{1} \prec' \Phi_{2}$ iff $\Phi_{1}(x) \leq \Phi_{2}(kx)$ , $x \geq x_{0} > 0$ and some $k > 0$ , discussed in Section 3 above, and they also demand that $\Phi_{2}(c_{1}x) \leq c_{2} \Phi_{1}(x)$ for $|x| \leq x_{1}$ with positive constants $c_{1}, c_{2}$ and $x_{1}$ .
Lemma 1. Let $\Phi_1, \Phi_2$ be continuous Young functions vanishing only at the origin, and $\Phi_1 \prec' \Phi_2$ . Then for their complementary functions $(\Psi_1, \Psi_2)$ one

The corresponding result for nonabelian $G$ involves difficulties even for a definition of the Fourier transform. We include some discussion on this point latter. The extension of Theorem 2 for LCA groups is as follows.

Remarks. (a) The Hausdorff-Young inequality of Theorem 2 plays a key role in all that follows and the conditions on $(\Phi, \Psi)$ are fully utilized in the LCA case also. Since an application of the Riesz-Thorin convexity theorem applied to $L^1$ and $L^2$ gives only the $L^p$ , $1 < p < 2$ , via Plancherel's theorem and since between any two $L^{p_i}$ -spaces, $1 < p_i < 2$ , there exist general Orlicz spaces $L^{\Phi_i}$ ( $|u|^{p_1} \prec \Phi_i(u) \prec |u|^{p_2})$ , the result of Theorem 2 demands a different technique. The method of Hardy and Littlewood for Theorem 2 and a special trick of Titchmarsh's for its extension to LCA groups (via the structure theory) seem necessary.

(b) The normalization of $N$ -functions in the preceding result is done for convenience only. Because of the topological equivalence for the normalized and general definitions, the same results (of Theorems 2 and 4) hold with different finite absolute constants depending only on the $\Phi$ and $\Phi_0$ .

(c) In the case of the Lebesgue spaces $L^p(G)$ with $1 \leq p \leq 2$ , conditions for equality in (7) have been investigated by Hirschman [1]. The corresponding study for $\mathcal{M}^\Phi(G)$ will be interesting but seems to be quite involved.

Since in the previous chapters we have used interpolation results to extend the work from a pair of Orlicz spaces, we can similarly present a corresponding statement on Fourier transforms for LCA groups. We devote the next result for this purpose.

Proof. (a) Let $\mathcal{P} \subset L^{\infty}(G)$ be the set of positive definite functions and $f \in \mathcal{P} \cap \mathcal{M}^{\Phi}(G)$ , be of compact support. Then it follows from the classical inversion theorem that $\hat{f}$ is bounded and integrable so that $\hat{f} \in \mathcal{M}^{\Psi}(\hat{G})$ , (cf., e.g., Loomis [1], p.143), and moreover that every step function can be approximated by elements of $\mathcal{P} \cap \mathcal{M}^{\Phi}(G)$ . Indeed, $L^{1} \cap L^{\infty}$ is dense in $\mathcal{M}^{\Phi}(G)$ and if $f \in L^{1} \cap L^{\infty}$ and $u$ is an approximate identity chosen from the same set (i.e., given $V$ , a neighborhood of identity, there is a $u \geq 0$ , $\operatorname{supp}(u) \subset V$ and $\int_{G} u d\mu = 1$ , and moreover the convolution $f * u$ approximates $f$ arbitrarily closely), then $\| f * u - f \|_{\Phi}$ is arbitrarily small. The only crucial difference is that $T$ is a sublinear operator, and so one has to use the corresponding interpolation theorem for such operators. Fortunately the necessary result is available. It is due to Kraynek ([1], Theorem 2.1.1) and it is also included in the companion volume (cf. Rao and Ren [1], p.367). We omit the repetition of the detail.

It is of interest to observe that examples of $N$ -functions $\Phi$ of the type studied in Theorem 2, include those considered by Riordan in his thesis. He treated $\Phi(x) = \frac{|x|^p}{p} L(x)$ , $1 < p < 2$ , where $L(\cdot)$ is a (finite number of) product of iterated logarithms and $\Psi(x) = \frac{|x|^q}{q} L_1(x)$ , $x > 0$ , where $L_1(x) = [L(x)]^{-\frac{q}{p}}$ for large $x$ and $q = \frac{p}{p - 1}$ . Although $\Psi$ is not necessarily complementary to $\Phi$ , it is equivalent to the complementary function. Actually, these are the principal parts of a pair of complementary $N$ -functions (cf., e.g., Krasnoselskii and Rutickii [1], Theorems 7.1 and 7.2 on pp.58-59). The particular form allows a more detailed analysis involving complicated calculations. Some of J. Riordan's unpublished results, related to an extension of Marcinkiewicz's interpolation theorem, have been included in our companion volume (cf. Rao and Ren [1], p.242). Applications of his results to Fourier analysis are possible, but we shall not consider this further here.

A basic motivation of the above work is to study the conjugate function $\tilde{f}$ of $f\in L^{p}(G)$ , if $p = 1$ . Then $\tilde{f}\notin L^{1}(G)$ , $G = [0,2\pi)$ , but will be in $L^p (G)$ , $0 < p < 1$ . Consequently, we need to consider the Fourier transforms on the $F$ -normed spaces, and we devote the next section for this topic in the context of Orlicz spaces, and continue an aspect in Chapter X.

In connection with the study of groups, we should note that the Fourier transforms on locally compact, noncompact, nonabelian cases are quite involved. If $G$ is a locally compact unimodular group, an extension of the Hausdorff-Young theorem for $L^p(G)$ spaces has been obtained by Kunze [1]. This was a motivation for our extension of the result for $\mathcal{M}^\Phi(G)$ spaces in Theorem 2 and Proposition 6 above for compact groups. In the general case, the definition of Fourier transform itself presents difficulties. Something akin to the above considerations can be studied if $G$ is a second countable unimodular group, following the work of Mautner [1], and for a general study the very detailed structure theory of Tatsuyauma [1] seems essential. We therefore

omit the abstract study of this subject, and turn briefly to the generalized Orlicz spaces with $F$ -norms (cf. Rao and Ren [1], Chapter $X$ ) and some results on conjugate functions based on $[0,2\pi)$ spaces.
6.5 Fourier analysis on generalized Orlicz spaces

The spaces to be considered here include $L^p, 0 < p < \infty$ , and we recall these definitions for convenience and immediate use. A function vanishing only at the origin, increasing and continuous is called a $\phi$ -function. In other words, a continuous function $\phi: \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ , such that $\phi(-u) = \phi(u), \phi(\cdot) \nearrow \infty$ and $\phi(u) = 0$ iff $u = 0$ is a $\phi$ -function. We then have a generalized Orlicz space $L^\phi(\Omega)$ on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ as before, i.e., $L^\phi(\Omega) = \{f: \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R} \text{ measurable}, \rho_\phi(\alpha f) < \infty \text{ for some } \alpha (= \alpha_f) > 0\}$ where $\rho_\phi(f) = \int_{\Omega} \phi(f) d\mu$ . The metric or F-norm $\| \cdot \|_\phi$ is then given by

$$
\left\| f \right\| _ {\phi} = \operatorname * {i n f} \left\{k > 0: \rho_ {\phi} (\frac {f}{k}) \leq k \right\}.
$$

We defined $M^{\phi}(\Omega) = \{f\in L^{\phi}(\Omega):\rho_{\phi}(\alpha f) < \infty \text{ for all } \alpha >0\}$ , so one finds $M^{\phi}(\Omega) = L^{\phi}(\Omega)$ iff $\phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ when $\mu (\Omega) < \infty$ . A few of the basic facts for such spaces may be found in the companion volume (Rao and Ren [1], Chapter X). We restate the following pair of results for use below.
Proposition 1. (a) $(L^{\phi}(\Omega),\| \cdot \|_{\phi})$ is a Fréchet space, i.e., a complete metric space under the metric $d(f,g) = \| f - g\|_{\phi}$ ;

list_items: (b) $\| f\|_{\phi}\leq k$ iff $\rho_{\phi}\left(\frac{f}{k}\right)\leq k;$
(c) $|f|\leq |g|$ , a.e. $\Rightarrow \| f\|_{\phi}\leq \| g\|_{\phi};$
(d) $\| f_n\|_{\phi}\longrightarrow 0$ as $n\longrightarrow \infty \Longrightarrow f_n\longrightarrow 0$ in $\mu$ -measure;
(e) $\lim_{\rho_{\phi}(f)\to 0}\| f\|_{\phi} = 0$ iff $\phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ , when $\mu (\Omega) < \infty$ .

text: The following order relations hold between pairs of $\phi$ -functions.
Proposition 2. If $\phi_i, i = 1,2$ are $\phi$ -functions and $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ , then the following statement are mutually equivalent.

list_items: (a) There are constants $c, k, u_0 > 0$ such that $\phi_2(u) \leq c\phi_1(ku)$ for $u \geq u_0$ (in symbol $\phi_1 \succ \phi_2$ );
(b) $L^{\phi_1}(\Omega)\subset L^{\phi_2}(\Omega);$
(c) $M^{\phi_1}(\Omega)\subset M^{\phi_2}(\Omega);$
(d) $\| f_n\|_{\phi_1}\longrightarrow 0\Longrightarrow \| f_n\|_{\phi_2}\longrightarrow 0$ , for $f_{n}\in L^{\phi_{1}}(\Omega)$

Hereafter we consider $f:[0,2\pi) \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$ as one defined on $\mathbb{R}$ by periodicity, i.e., $f(x + 2k\pi) = f(x), 0 \leq x < 2\pi, k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$ . As noted at the end of the last section, our aim here is to develop the generalized $L^{\phi}(\Omega)$ -spaces to establish that the mapping $T: f \mapsto \tilde{f}$ of functions into their conjugates, satisfies $T(L^{1}(\Omega)) \subset L^{\phi}(\Omega)$ for a $\phi$ -function and that $T$ is continuous when (and only when) $\phi$ satisfies an integral growth condition. The treatment mainly follows the work of Lesniewicz [1].

Remarks 10. 1. Condition (3), called Lesniewicz condition by Zhizhiaashvili [1] (p.71), is related to certain ordering relations and growths of $\phi$ -functions. Bibliographical notes. This chapter concentrates on certain results from Fourier series, in the context of Orlicz spaces. The basic result contained in Sections 1 and 2 is an extension due to R. Ryan [1] of the classical result, first given by M. Riesz [1], on conjugate functions and Fourier series. This is an important characterization and an extension to Orlicz spaces based on the torus group identified as $[0,2\pi)$ . Some of the results (independently worked out of Ryan's work) for certain subspaces of $L^{\Phi}$ -spaces based on compact groups, and locally compact abelian groups are in Rao ([6] and [12]). Ryan's result shows that $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ if we consider the M. Riesz theorem for $L^{\Phi}$ -spaces. If these are not assumed, then $\Phi$ and $\Psi$ (the complementary $N$ -functions) must satisfy a different set of conditions. This
VI. Fourier Analysis in Orlicz Spaces

work, extending the classical Hausdorff-Young theorem, is given in Sections 3 and 4. Also conjugate series are considered there. It is interesting that an extension of Hardy and Littlewood method is needed in obtaining a proper Orlicz (non Lebesgue $L^p$ ) space result. In the final section we have presented the conjugate function result for $L^1[0,2\pi)$ into generalized Orlicz spaces due to Lesniewicz [1], who discovered a key condition for the $\phi$ -function to admit conjugate mapping from $L^1$ into $L^\phi$ . Thus we have included some hard analysis in the context of Orlicz spaces related to the classical harmonic analysis. Many other results can be extended with similar methods. More on generalized Orlicz spaces will be found in Chapter X when $\phi$ is slightly specialized. Some other applications are considered in the following chapters.
6.5 Fourier analysis on generalized Orlicz spaces
Chapter VII
Applications to Prediction Analysis

The classical least squares prediction, originated by Gauss, finds a natural home in Orlicz spaces when the optimality criterion is considered as a nonnegative symmetric convex function vanishing at the origin, the quadratic function being a special case. This is also closely related to aspects of approximation theory. Here we present a brief account of the existence, uniqueness of best predictors for convex (loss) functions, and consider a method of construction of them for a subclass, using the available Orlicz space theory. Then we discuss conditions under which the prediction operators become linear, since they are typically nonlinear in the non least squares problems. This chapter attempts to show how Orlicz space theory forms a crucial part of this analysis which is important in probability theory as well as abstract approximation and other areas.
7.1 Best predictions with convex loss

Let $X_{T} = \{X_{t}, t \in T \subset \mathbb{R}\}$ be a random process on a probability space $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ such that $X_{t} \in L^{\Phi}(P), t \in T$ , where $\Phi : \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{+}$ is a Young function. The family $X_{T_{1}}, T_{1} \subset T$ is given (i.e., is observed in an experiment). If $t_{0} \in T - T_{1}$ and $X_{t_{0}}$ is the unobserved random variable, it is desired to predict or approximate $X_{t_{0}}$ by using a function $g$ of $X_{T_{1}}$ , such that $g$ is closest to $X_{t_{0}}$ in the sense of the norm of $L^{\Phi}(P)$ [or the modular defined by $\Phi$ ]. It is called the prediction problem relative to the convex loss function $\Phi$ . More precisely, this is stated as follows.

Definition 1. Let $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ be a closed subspace determined by $X_{T_1} = \{X_t, t \in T_1 \subset T, T_1 \neq T\}$ . This means either (i) $\mathcal{M}$ is a closed linear span of the $X_t, t \in T_1$ or (ii) $\mathcal{M} = L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_{T_1})$ where $\mathcal{B}_{T_1} = \sigma(X_t, t \in T_1)$ , the $\sigma$ -algebra generated by the $X_t, t \in T_1$ . [So the first space is a proper subspace

of $L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_{T_1})$ , if at least one $X_{t}$ is nonconstant.] If $X_{t_0} \in L^{\Phi}(\Sigma) - \mathcal{M}$ , then a unique $\hat{X} \in \mathcal{M}$ , if it exists, is termed the best predictor of $X_{t_0}$ relative to the convex (loss) function $\Phi$ , if $\| X_{t_0} - \hat{X}\|_{\Phi} = \inf \{\| X_{t_0} - Y\|_{\Phi}:Y \in \mathcal{M}\}$ . [Alternatively, we can also understand it as:

assuming that these integrals are finite. In particular this holds if $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ . Here problem (i) is called linear prediction, and (ii) nonlinear prediction. We consider only (ii) hereafter since (i) uses different tools.]

It must be noted that the best predictor, using the distance (or norm) functional and the modular in the alternative definition, are not necessarily the same in contrast to the Lebesgue spaces. This is not unexpected since in the more inclusive Orlicz context, the norm and modular convergence need not be the same. One can better utilize the geometry of $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ -spaces with the norm than with the modular concept in (1). We present first an auxiliary result, using the norm topology, to understand the problem better.

Remarks. 1. As noted in the proof, if $\{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$ is a finite collection of elements of $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ , and $\{Y_1, Y_2, \dots, Y_n\}$ are the corresponding closest elements of $\mathcal{M} = L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B})$ , then nonuniqueness of quasi-complements allows one to choose the $\widetilde{P_{\mathcal{B}}}$ that works for $X_1, \dots, X_n$ , such that $\widetilde{P_{\mathcal{B}}}X_i = Y_i, i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ . This may even be done for a countable collection, but not for all the elements of $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ . The dependence of the closed (usually unbounded) $\widetilde{P_{\mathcal{B}}}$ on such a collection of $X_n'$ s may be exploited in some problems.

2. The existence of quasi-complements is a nontrivial result in Banach space geometry. It was shown by Lindenstrauss [1] that in every reflexive Banach space (not necessary separable), each closed subspace admits a
VII. Applications to Prediction Analysis

quasi-complement, and that for nonreflexive nonseparable spaces (in contrast to Mackey's result) this is no longer true, and constructed spaces $\mathcal{X}$ that contain $m(\Gamma)$ , the space of bounded functions on an uncountable set $\Gamma$ , having subspaces that include isometrically $c_0(\Gamma)$ , the space of bounded functions that vanish at "infinity", which do not admit quasi-complements. [This was shown by him in [2].] Thus the separability used as hypothesis is needed.

3. The operator $\widetilde{P}_{\mathcal{B}}$ has a dense domain in $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ so its adjoint $(\widetilde{P}_{\mathcal{B}})^{*}$ exists as a closed projection, whose domain is weakly\*-dense in $(L^{\Phi}(\Sigma))^{*}$ . If $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ is reflexive, and either the range or the null space of $\widetilde{P}_{\mathcal{B}}$ [or of its adjoint] is finite dimensional, then $\widetilde{P}_{\mathcal{B}}$ is bounded. However, even then $\widetilde{P}_{\mathcal{B}}$ is not necessarily a contraction and if $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ is not a Hilbert space, the above operator, in Proposition 2, need not coincide with the conditional expectation, although the properties noted as (i)-(v) are characteristic of a conditional expectation operator.

For a given $X$ , the unique minimal element $Y_0$ assumed in the above proposition will now be established in the following result. The existence question for the modular functionals was solved in De Groot and Rao ([1], [2]), and for the $L^1(\mu)$ case by Shintani and Andô [1]. Uniformly convex Orlicz spaces will be treated later as the techniques are different in all these cases. We start with convex loss functions satisfying some smoothness (i.e., differentiability) conditions. The concept of best predictor relative to a convex loss function, as introduced in Definition 1, will be used. Also, define the mapping $U: f \longmapsto \int_{\Omega} \Phi(f_0 - f) d\mu, f \in L^\Phi(\mu)$ , where $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ is the basic probability space.

Suppose $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ and the derivative $\Phi'$ exists and satisfies the following condition: for each $a > 1$ there is a $K_a > 1$ such that $\Phi'(ax) \geq K_a \Phi'(x), x \geq 0$ . Then for each $\sigma$ -subalgebra $\mathcal{B}$ of $\Sigma$ and $f_0 \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , there is a unique $\tilde{f}$ (depending on $f_0$ ) in $L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}) = \{f \in L^{\Phi}(\mu), f \text{ is } \mathcal{B} - \text{measurable}\}$ such that

[Here, as usual, $\operatorname{sgn}(x)$ is the signum function.]

Proof. First observe that $U(\tilde{f}) < \infty$ because $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ . Let $\alpha_0 = \inf \{U(g): g \in L^\Phi(\mathcal{B})\}$ . Since for nontriviality we can assume that $f_0 \notin L^\Phi(\mathcal{B})$ , it follows that $0 < \alpha_0 < \infty$ . The conditions on $\Phi'$ imply that $\Phi$ is uniformly convex, and (with $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ ) that the space $L^\Phi(\mu)$ is uniformly convex. (Cf. the companion volume, Rao and Ren [1], p.288.) By definition of infimum, there exist $f_n \in L^\Phi(\mathcal{B})$ such that $U(f_n) \longrightarrow \alpha_0$ . Hence the sequence $\{f_n, n \geq 1\}$ is norm bounded, i.e., is contained in a ball. Since $L^\Phi(\mu)$ is also reflexive, its subspace $L^\Phi(\mathcal{B})$ is reflexive. Then $\{f_n, n \geq 1\}$ is relatively weakly sequentially compact; whence there is a weakly convergent subsequence $f_{n_k} \longrightarrow f^1$ with $f^1 \in L^\Phi(\mathcal{B})$ . Since $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ , the norm convergence and the modular (or mean) convergence in $L^\Phi(\mu)$ are well-known to be equivalent. Hence $f_{n_k} \longrightarrow f^1$ in norm by a classical result (due to F. Riesz) in any uniformly convex Banach space. [See Theorem 4.2 below on a generalized version.] It follows now that $U(f_n) \longrightarrow U(f^1) = \alpha_0$ . Since the space is strictly convex and the functional $U(\cdot): L^\Phi(\mathcal{B}) \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ is convex, it follows that the minimum is attained at a unique point. So $f^1 = \tilde{f}$ of (4) and the first part of the theorem is proved.

To derive (5), we now differentiate the functional $U(\cdot)$ . Let $h(t,g) = \frac{d}{dt} U(\widetilde{f} +tg),g\in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . Since $U(\widetilde{f})\leq U(\widetilde{f} +tg)$ for all $t\in \mathbb{R}$ and $g\in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , with the unique minimum at $\widetilde{f}$ , it follows from elementary calculus considerations that $h(0,g) = 0$ for all $g\in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . From known results (cf. Dunford and Schwartz [1], V.9.1) that $|\overline{f} + tg| / t$ is nondecreasing in $t$ as $t \searrow 0$ , and $\frac{d}{dt}(|\overline{f} + tg|) \leq |\widetilde{f}| + |g|$ . Hence the known results in Orlicz space theory imply that the right side of (7) is integrable. Consequently, by the dominated convergence theorem the integral and derivative can be interchanged in (7). This gives

Since $g \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is arbitrary, setting it to be a nonzero constant $g = a \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , and cancelling, we get (5), and then (6) is an immediate consequence of the definition of the signum function. $\square$

This $Q(\cdot, \cdot)$ is called a regular conditional measure iff (a) $Q(\cdot, \omega)$ is a probability measure for each $\omega \in \Omega$ and (b) $Q(A, \cdot)$ is $\mathcal{B}$ -measurable for each $A \in \Sigma$ . Discussion. It is useful to note the relation between the existence of regular conditional measures related to a given probability space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ and another concept called disintegration of measures in mathematics.
VII. Applications to Prediction Analysis

Consider again the identity $E(g) = E(E^{\mathcal{B}}(g))$ for all $g \in L^{1}(\mu)$ , given in (8). This is written explicitly, if a regular conditional measure $Q(\cdot, \cdot)$ of $\mu^{\mathcal{B}}(\cdot)(\cdot)$ exists, as:
where $\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \mu |\mathcal{B}$ and $|Q(\cdot ,\cdot)| =$ variation of $Q(\cdot ,\cdot) = \mu (\cdot)$ . Taking $g = \chi_A,A\in \Sigma$ , this becomes

or symbolically $\mu(d\omega) = Q(d\omega', \omega)\mu_{\mathcal{B}}(d\omega)$ , and thus $\mu$ is "broken up or disintegrated" relative to $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ into a family $\{Q(\cdot, \omega), \omega \in \Omega\}$ of probability measures. Such a disintegration for a general measure is not always possible. In fact, there is a close relation between regularity of conditional probability measures, disintegration of a probability measure and the existence of a "(strong) lifting operation" on $L^{\infty}(\mathcal{B})$ . A detailed discussion of this may be found in the book (Rao [21], Ch.5). If, for instance, $\mathcal{B}$ is generated by a vector $X = (f_1, f_2, \dots)$ of random variables $(\mathcal{B} \subset \Sigma)$ on $\Omega$ , and if the range $X(\Omega) \subset \mathbb{R}^{N}$ , is a Borel set, then the regular conditioning $\mu^{\mathcal{B}}(\cdot)(\cdot)$ exists. This is always possible if the $f_i$ are "coordinate functions" $f_i = \pi_i : \Omega = \mathbb{R}^{N} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$ . These questions are discussed in the above reference, and we shall omit them here. Finally, it may be shown that the best predictor given by (13), when regular conditional measures exist, is valid for a convex "loss function" $\Phi$ which need not satisfy a $\triangle_2$ -condition and hence $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ need not be reflexive. This is established in De Groot and Rao [1]. We shall discuss this and a more specialized case of $\Phi(x) = |x|$ later to appreciate the nonreflexive (and non strictly convex) cases clearly.

If $\{f_n, n \geq 1\}$ is a sequence of observations, let $\mathcal{B}_n = \sigma(f_i, i \leq n)$ and $\mathcal{B}_{\infty} = \sigma(f_n, n \geq 1)$ . Then by the preceding theorem, for each $n \geq 1$ , there is a (unique) best predictor $\tilde{f}_n$ based on $f_1, \dots, f_n$ in the sense that $U(\tilde{f}_n) = \min\{U(g) : g \in L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_n)\}$ . Similarly, $\tilde{f}_{\infty}$ is the best predictor of $f_0$ based on all the observations so that $\tilde{f}_{\infty} \in L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_{\infty})$ and is the closest to $f_0$ . The relation between $\{\tilde{f}_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ and $f_0$ is described by the following:

Theorem 5. Let $\Phi : \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ be the $N$ -function and $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , $L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_n)$ be as in Theorem 3. If $U: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ is the modular functional and $\{\tilde{f}_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ is the best predictor sequence obtained above, then $\tilde{f}_n \longrightarrow \tilde{f}_{\infty}$ as $n \longrightarrow \infty$ in $U$ -(modular) mean, so that $U(\tilde{f}_{\infty} - \tilde{f}_n) \longrightarrow 0$ as $n \longrightarrow \infty$ . [It can also be shown, under the same additional hypothesis that $\tilde{f}_n \longrightarrow \tilde{f}_{\infty}$ , a.e.]
7.1 Best prediction with convex loss

Proof. Here we establish the mean convergence, but omit the pointwise part which is better considered by using a detailed probabilistic analysis. Now under the present hypothesis, $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is uniformly convex (hence reflexive). By Theorem 3, for each $n$ , there is a unique $\tilde{f}_n \in L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_n)$ such that $U(\tilde{f}_n) = \min \{U(f): f \in L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_n)\}$ . Since $\mathcal{B}_n \subset \mathcal{B}_{n+1}$ implies $L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_n) \subset L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_{n+1})$ , it follows that $U(\tilde{f}_n) \geq U(\tilde{f}_{n+1})$ , so that the set $\{\tilde{f}_n, n \geq 1\} \subset \bigcup_{n \geq 1} L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_n) \subset L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_{\infty})$ , is norm bounded, and hence is weakly sequentially relatively compact. The argument of the proof of Theorem 3 shows that there is a weakly convergent subsequence $\tilde{f}_{n_k} \longrightarrow \tilde{f}_0$ (say), and that the convergence (by Riesz's theorem used there) is in fact strong. The strict convexity of the functional $U(\cdot)$ also implies that every convergent subsequence of $\{\tilde{f}_n, n \geq 1\}$ has the same limit so that the whole sequence $\tilde{f}_n \longrightarrow \tilde{f}_0$ in norm and $\tilde{f}_0 \in L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_{\infty})$ , where $\mathcal{B}_{\infty} = \sigma(\bigcup_{n \geq 1} \mathcal{B}_n)$ and that $L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_{\infty}) = \text{closed linear span}(\bigcup_{n \geq 1} L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}_n))$ . Note that the equivalence of the norm and modular convergences is utilized here, and so the assertion holds.

Remark. The pointwise convergence, using a probabilistic argument is established even for a more general class of "Riesz-Köthe" function spaces whose norm is absolutely continuous, strictly convex and has the "Kadec property" as well as obeying a uniform monotonicity condition. These are all automatic for the Orlicz spaces considered above. This general result, due to Bru and Heinich [1], is detailed in a recent book (cf. Rao [24], p.475), and will be omitted here. Instead, we shall present a characterization of linearity of the prediction operator $P_{\mathcal{B}}^{x}(\cdot)$ , for all $x$ , (cf. Proposition 2 above) in the next section, extending a basic result due to Andô [3].
7.2 When is a prediction operator linear?

It was seen in the last section that in a separable $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ , if $\mathcal{M} = L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}) \subset L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ , then for each $X \in L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ , the existence of $Y_0 \in \mathcal{M}$ such that $\|X - Y_0\|_{(\Phi)} = \inf \left\{\|X - Y\|_{(\Phi)} : Y \in \mathcal{M}\right\}$ , implies $P_{\mathcal{B}}^x : X \longmapsto Y_0$ defines a linear operator depending on $X$ . Here we wish to consider a subclass of spaces $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ , not assumed separable, for which unique minimal elements exist in $\mathcal{M} = L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B})$ , so that $P_{\mathcal{B}} : X \longmapsto P_{\mathcal{B}}X$ is defined for all $X \in L^{\Phi}(\Sigma)$ but is also linear. If $L^{\Phi}(\Sigma) = L^{p}(\Sigma)$ , $1 \leq p \neq 2 < \infty$ , such a characterization was obtained by Ando [3], and its extension to Orlicz spaces was considered by Rao [11]. We shall detail the salient part of the latter for the problem here, as it is interesting and nontrivial, revealing a new aspect of Orlicz spaces.
One says that a subspace $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is a Chebyshev set iff for each $f \in$
VII. Applications to Prediction Analysis

$L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ there is a unique $g_{f}\in \mathcal{M}$ such that $\| f - g_f\|_{(\Phi)} = \inf \{\| f - h\|_{(\Phi)}:h\in$ $\mathcal{M}\}$ , i.e., $g_{f}$ is the best predictor of $f$ . A pair of subspaces $\{\mathcal{M},\mathcal{N}\} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is complementary if $\mathcal{M}\cap \mathcal{N} = \{0\}$ and the direct sum $\mathcal{M}\oplus \mathcal{N} = L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ (not merely dense!). The following simple but crucial observation is basic for the characterization problem of linearity of the prediction operator on $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ into $\mathcal{M}$ , denoted by $P_{\mathcal{M}}$

Proposition 1. Let $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ be an Orlicz space on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ and $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ be a Chebyshev subspace so that $P_{\mathcal{M}}: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}$ is the prediction operator which exists. Then $P_{\mathcal{M}}$ is linear iff there is a complementary subspace $\mathcal{N}$ of $\mathcal{M}$ which is the range of a contractive projection on $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ .

Proof. Since $\mathcal{M}$ is a Chebyshev subspace the mapping $P_{\mathcal{M}}: f \longmapsto g_f \in \mathcal{M}$ exists, but generally it is nonlinear. If it is linear, then $Q = I - P_{\mathcal{M}}$ is a (linear) projection with range $\mathcal{N}$ which is complementary to $\mathcal{M}$ . Moreover, for $f \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ and $h \in \mathcal{M}$
Taking $h = 0$ , it follows that $\| Qf\|_{(\Phi)}\leq \| f\|_{(\Phi)}$ so that $Q$ is also a contraction.

Conversely, let $\mathcal{N}$ be a complementary subspace of $\mathcal{M}$ , that is the range of a contractive (linear) projection $Q$ . Then $P_{\mathcal{M}} = I - Q: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}$ is a (linear) projection with range $\mathcal{M}$ and null space $\mathcal{N}$ . Also, for $h \in \mathcal{M}$

This follows from the fact that $\mathcal{M}$ is the null space of $Q$ and $Q$ is a linear contraction. Since $P_{\mathcal{M}}f \in \mathcal{M}$ , it is clear from (2) that $P_{\mathcal{M}}f$ is the best predictor of $f$ in $\mathcal{M}$ which is a Chebyshev subspace, so that $P_{\mathcal{M}}$ is a linear predictor on $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . $\square$

Note that for this statement, we did not use any property of the Orlicz space, and thus it is valid for any Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ in place of $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . However, the structure of $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ will be used for a characterization of the pair $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$ in order that $P_{\mathcal{M}}$ is linear. To solve our problem, we restate the above result in a different but equivalent form which uses the Orlicz space structure in a crucial manner. We assume hereafter that $\Phi$ is normalized, i.e., $\Phi(1) + \Psi(1) = 1$ , [cf. the companion volume, Rao and Ren [1], p.35].

It is classical that in a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ with $\mathcal{M}$ as a closed subspace and $\mathcal{M}^{\perp} = \{x^{*} \in \mathcal{X}^{*} : x^{*}(\mathcal{M}) = 0\}$ , the annihilator, then the quotient space $\mathcal{X} / \mathcal{M}$ with the quotient norm, i.e., if $\hat{x} = x + \mathcal{M} \in \mathcal{X} / \mathcal{M}, \| \hat{x} \| = \inf \{\| x + y \| : y \in \mathcal{M}\}$ , is isometrically isomorphic to $\mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ whose norm is that

of $\mathcal{X}^*$ , the adjoint space (cf. e.g., Dunford and Schwartz [1], p.72). Moreover, if $P: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \mathcal{X}$ is a bounded projection with range $\mathcal{M}$ , and null space $\mathcal{N}$ , so that $\mathcal{X} = \mathcal{M} \oplus \mathcal{N}$ , then $\mathcal{X}^* = \mathcal{M}^\perp \oplus \mathcal{N}^\perp$ and the adjoint operator $P^*: \mathcal{X}^* \longrightarrow \mathcal{X}^*$ is also a projection with range $\mathcal{N}^\perp$ and null space $\mathcal{M}^\perp$ . In our case, $\mathcal{X} = L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , $\mathcal{M}$ is a Chebyshev set, and $P_{\mathcal{M}}: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}$ is linear (hence also a contractive projection) iff $I - P_{\mathcal{M}} = Q: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow \mathcal{N}$ is a contractive projection by the preceding proposition. Then the adjoint operator $Q^*$ is a contractive projection on $(L^{\Phi}(\mu))^*$ with range $\mathcal{M}^\perp (\cong L^{\Phi}(\mu) / \mathcal{M})$ . Assuming $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ to be a real space, so that it is actually a Banach lattice under the pointwise a.e. ordering, the quotient space $L^{\Phi}(\mu) / \mathcal{M}$ inherits a partial ordering, or $\mathcal{M}^\perp \subset (L^{\Phi}(\mu))^*$ is also a Banach lattice. Since in general this adjoint space is not a real function space (it has set functions), we now restrict $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ so that $(L^{\Phi}(\mu))^*$ is identifiable with $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ , (cf. Rao and Ren [1], Sec.4.1). For simplicity, we further assume that $\Psi \in \triangle_2$ (or $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ ) so that $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is reflexive. In this case $Q^{**} = Q$ [and $\mathcal{M}^\perp = \mathcal{M}$ , $(L^{\Phi}(\mu))^{**} = L^{\Phi}(\mu)]$ which is a contractive projection. Moreover, $\tilde{P} = I - Q$ is a linear prediction operator onto the Chebyshev subspace $\mathcal{M}$ , since $Q(\mathcal{M}) = 0$ and for $f \in L^{\Phi}(\mu), g \in \mathcal{M}$ ,

Thus $\tilde{P} f$ is a minimal element and then it is unique so that $\tilde{P}:f\mapsto \tilde{P} f$ is a (linear) prediction operator. But now $\mathcal{M}^{\perp}\subset L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ is a Banach lattice. However $\mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ and $\mathcal{N}^*$ (as well as $\mathcal{M}^*$ and $\mathcal{N}^{\perp}$ ) can be isometrically identified, preserving lattice operations. Hence $\mathcal{N}$ is a Banach lattice as well, and is a subspace of $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . Thus the preceding result can be restated as:

Proposition 2. Let $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ be a Chebyshev subspace with $P_{\mathcal{M}}: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \to \mathcal{M}$ as its prediction operator and $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ . Then $P_{\mathcal{M}}$ is linear iff $L^{\Phi}(\mu) / \mathcal{M}$ (identified as a complementary subspace of $\mathcal{M}$ ) is a Banach lattice (because $P_{\mathcal{M}}f \geq 0$ for $f \geq 0$ always).

This result demands that we characterize Banach sublattices of the (Banach) lattice $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . In general, this is an involved problem. A complete solution was given by Ando [3] when $\Phi(x) = |x|^p, p > 1$ , and it was extended by Rao [11] when $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ . Since these details are numerous, we shall present the general form completing the last proposition and then discuss some consequences.

A set $S \in \Sigma$ is called the support of a subspace $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ on a $\sigma$ -finite measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ if $S$ is the supremum of $\{S_f : f \in \mathcal{M}\}$ where $S_f = \{\omega : f(\omega) \neq 0\}$ is the support of $f$ . It can be verified that such

a supremum exists in any complete $\sigma$ -finite measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ . [It is even true for all "localizable" measures, but here we restrict to finite $\mu$ for simplicity.] Then one has the following with $\Phi \in \Delta_2$ , an $N$ -function:
Proposition 3. Let $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ be an Orlicz space with $\mu$ complete and finite. If $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is a Banach lattice, then there exist

(ii) a $\sigma$ -algebra $\mathcal{B} \subset \Sigma$ such that $\mathcal{M}$ is representable as $\mathcal{M} = g_0 \cdot L^\Phi(\Omega, \mathcal{B}, \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) = \{g_0 f : f \in L^\Phi(\Omega, \mathcal{B}, \mu_{\mathcal{B}})\}$ and $\|g_0 f\|_{(\Phi)} = \|f\|_{(\Phi)}, f \in L^\Phi(\mu_{\mathcal{B}})$ where $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ is $\mu$ restricted to $\mathcal{B}$ .

With this structural result, $\mathcal{M}$ can be related to a projection operator on the space $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ as follows (it was already noted at the end of Chapter V).
Proposition 4. A subspace $\mathcal{N} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is the range of a contractive projection iff it is a Banach lattice.

Proof. If $\mathcal{N} = P(L^{\Phi}(\mu))$ where $P$ is a positive contractive projection, it is a complete subspace. Also if $f\in \mathcal{N}$ , then $f = Pf$ since $P|\mathcal{N} =$ identity, and $|f| = |Pf|\leq P(|f|)$ a.e. But $\| f\|_{(\Phi)}\leq \| P(|f|)\|_{(\Phi)}\leq \| f\|_{(\Phi)}$ so that by the monotonicity of the norm, $P(|f|) = |f|$ a.e. and hence $|f|\in \mathcal{N}$ . Thus $\mathcal{N}$ is a Banach lattice.

Conversely, if $\mathcal{N}$ is a Banach lattice, then by the preceding result, $\mathcal{N} = g_0 \cdot L^{\Phi}(\mu_{\mathcal{B}})$ , where $0 \leq g_0 \in \mathcal{N}$ is an element with support $S \in \mathcal{B}$ which is also the support of $\mathcal{N}$ . But the conditional expectation $E^{\mathcal{B}}$ on $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ has range $L^{\Phi}(\mu_{\mathcal{B}})$ and is a positive contractive projection. Let $P: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow \mathcal{N}$ be defined as $P(g) = g_0 E^{\mathcal{B}}(g)$ , $g \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , $g_0 \geq 0$ . Then it is asserted that $P$ is a positive contractive projection on $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ with range $\mathcal{N}$ . Indeed, by the isometry of the mapping (cf. Prop.3) and using the fact that $E^{\mathcal{B}}$ is a positive contraction, we have

It remains to show that $P^2 = P$ . By construction $S = \text{supp}(g_0) \in \mathcal{B}$ , hence also $E^{\mathcal{B}}(g_0) = \chi_S$ . From this one has with the "averaging property" of $E^{\mathcal{B}}$ , the following relation: if $g \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , then
7.2 When is a prediction operator linear?
Hence $P$ is a contractive positive projection with range $\mathcal{N}$ .

It should be observed that when $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is not a Hilbert space, a bounded projection with range a given subspace $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ need not exist. The above result shows that such a projection (even a contractive one) exists if $\mathcal{M}$ is a Banach lattice. When it exists, the projection need not be unique. However, if $\mathcal{M}$ is a Chebyshev subspace in addition, or if $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is strictly convex and some further conditions hold, then again the uniqueness property obtains.
Based on the preceding propositions, we can present the following general statement on the linearity problem.

Theorem 5. Let $L^{\Phi}(\mu), \Phi \in \Delta_{2} \cap \nabla_{2}$ be a (reflexive) Orlicz space and $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ be a Chebyshev subspace. Then the prediction $P_{\mathcal{M}}: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}$ is linear iff the quotient space $L^{\Phi}(\mu) / \mathcal{M}$ is isometrically isomorphic to an $L^{\Phi}(\widetilde{\Omega}, \widetilde{B}, \widetilde{\mu})$ where $\Phi$ is as before and $(\widetilde{\Omega}, \widetilde{B}, \widetilde{\mu})$ is some (finite) measure space. Moreover, the isomorphism preserves the maximal supports of spaces.

Sketch of proof. If $P_{\mathcal{M}}$ is linear, then $L^{\Phi}(\mu) = \mathcal{M} \oplus \mathcal{N}$ , where $\mathcal{N}$ is a complementary subspace of $\mathcal{M}$ which is isometrically isomorphic to $L^{\Phi}(\mu) / \mathcal{M}$ and is the range of a positive contractive projection. It is then equivalent (≡isometrically isomorphic) to an $L^{\Phi}(\tilde{\mathcal{B}}) = L^{\Phi}(\tilde{\Omega},\tilde{\mathcal{B}},\tilde{\mu})$ by the preceding proposition.

Conversely, if $L^{\Phi}(\mu) / \mathcal{M}$ can be isometrically identified with an $L^{\Phi}(\widetilde{B})$ , then $(L^{\Phi}(\mu) / \mathcal{M})^{*} \cong \mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ , the annihilator of $\mathcal{M}$ . This implies, since $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ , $\mathcal{M}^{\perp} \cong L^{\Psi}(\widetilde{B})$ . But $\mathcal{M}^{\perp}$ can be identified with a closed subspace of $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ and the above equivalence implies that there is a unitary operator $U$ such that $U(\mathcal{M}^{\perp})$ is a Banach lattice so that it is the range of a positive contractive projection $T$ on $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ . Consequently, $T^{*}: (L^{\Psi}(\mu))^{*} = L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is a contractive projection with a similar property (after some work). Thus $P = T^{*}$ is a projection on $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ with range $\mathcal{N}$ and null space $\mathcal{M}$ , whence $\mathcal{N} \oplus \mathcal{M} = L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . Since $\mathcal{M}$ is a Chebyshev space, it follows that $P_{\mathcal{M}} = I - P: L^{\Phi}(\mu) \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}$ is a linear prediction operator, as in (3), completing the sketch.

Some remarks on the solution are in order. From the point of view of applications, it is hard to verify the conditions of the characterization given in the theorem. If $\mathcal{M}$ itself is a subspace $L^{\Phi}(\mu_{\mathcal{B}})$ , based on a $\sigma$ -subalgebra $\mathcal{B} \subset \Sigma$ , [i.e., $\mathcal{M} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ ], then $\mathcal{B}$ has to be "small" so that $L^{\Phi}(\mu_{\mathcal{B}})$ is a small space, and the direct form of such a space on which $P_{\mathcal{M}}$ is a linear projection is really uninteresting for applications. The next best things are the isomorphism results. This can be appreciated if we recall that any reflexive Orlicz
VII. Applications to Prediction Analysis

space $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , which itself is not smooth, is isometrically isomorphic to a uniformly convex and uniformly smooth Orlicz space $L^{\Phi_1}(\mu)$ where $\Phi \sim \Phi_1$ (cf. e.g., Rao and Ren [1], p.297). This fact exemplifies the reasons behind the preceding considerations. If one does not require the linearity of prediction operators, Theorems 1.3 and 1.4 present integral equations for finding the best predictors for any given element of $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , under reasonable conditions.
We consider some complements to the results of the preceding sections.
7.3 Nonlinear prediction for nonreflexive spaces

Most of the geometric properties of spaces used in the preceding sections are not available for general nonreflexive spaces. So here we indicate some methods for such spaces by considering the special case of real $L^1(\mu)$ and a class of real $L^\Phi(\mu)$ both on a probability triple $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ . For this analysis, we need to consider regular conditional probability measures as recalled in Section 1 above. A somewhat more general problem will be presented, and the following preliminary result will be useful. It follows De Groot and Rao [1].

We finally obtain an analog of Theorem 1.5 for the $L^1$ -case. Because of the nonuniqueness of best predictors, the argument there does not apply here. If the observations are taken for $t \leq t_k, t_k < t_{k+1}$ , and $\mathcal{B}_k = \sigma(X_t, t \leq t_k)$ , then $\mathcal{B}_k \subset \mathcal{B}_{k+1}$ and if $g_k \in L^1(\mathcal{B}_k)$ is a best predictor of $X_\tau$ where $\tau > t_k$ for all $k$ , and $\lim_{k \to \infty} t_k = t_\infty < \tau$ , then the problem is to discuss the convergence of the sequence $\{g_k, k \geq 1\}$ , and its relation to $g_\infty$ if $\mathcal{B}_\infty = \sigma(\bigcup_{k \geq 1} \mathcal{B}_k)$ . This is outlined here. A sequence of (not necessarily monotone) $\sigma$ -algebras $\mathcal{B}_n \subset \Sigma$ is termed strongly (or a.e.) convergent to a $\sigma$ -algebra $\mathcal{B}_\infty$ if the sequence $E^{\mathcal{B}_n}(f) \to E^{\mathcal{B}_\infty}(f)$ in norm (or a.e.) as $n \to \infty$ for each $f \in L^1(\Sigma)$ where $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ is a probability space. If $\mathcal{B}_n \subset \mathcal{B}_{n+1}$ , then this is an immediate consequence of a classical martingale convergence theorem. Here we are assuming just its conclusion to gain some needed generality.

The following important result on strong convergence of best predictors of a given $X_{\tau} \in L^{1}(\Sigma)$ , relative to $\mathcal{B}_n$ , is due to Shintani and Andô [1], and the proof uses the structure of $L^{1}(\Sigma)$ .

III. We observe that the $U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau)$ (and $V_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau)$ ) converge in $L^1(\mathcal{B}_{\infty})$ to $U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ (and $V_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ ). Since $V_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau) \leq h_n \leq U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau)$ , and the $h_n$ -sequence was shown to be uniformly integrable, it follows in particular that the end point sequences, i.e. $\{U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau), n \geq 1\}$ and $\{V_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau), n \geq 1\}$ , have the same property. Then the desired $L^1$ -convergence will follow from Vitali's theorem if the concerned sequence is shown to converge in measure which will now be established.

For this, by standard results in Real Analysis, it is sufficient to show that each subsequence has a further subsequence that converges a.e. to $U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_{\tau})$ . Now by hypothesis $\mathcal{B}_n \to \mathcal{B}_{\infty}$ strongly. [The same argument below works for a.e. convergence also.] By the regularity of $P^{\mathcal{B}_n}$ , consider their conditional distributions, denoted $Q(\cdot, x) = P^{\mathcal{B}_n} \circ X_\tau^{-1}(\cdot)(x)$ . The assumption on $\mathcal{B}_n$ 's implies that $Q(\cdot, x) \to Q_{\infty}(\cdot, x)$ as $n \to \infty$ , for any arbitrarily fixed $x$ . Then using a diagonal procedure (as in the classical Helly selection principle) one can find a subsequence $Q_{n_k}(\cdot, x) \to Q_{\infty}(\cdot, x)$ at all rational (and then at all) points $x \in \mathbb{R}$ . Now the Borel $\sigma$ -algebra $\Re$ and hence $U_{\mathcal{B}_{n_k}}(X_\tau)$ , may be chosen to satisfy $\lim_{k \to \infty} U_{\mathcal{B}_{n_k}}(X_\tau) \leq U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ . On the other hand $U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau) \in L^1(\mathcal{B}_{\infty}), n \geq 1$ so that by definition of best predictor, $U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ being such a one, $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau) \geq U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ always holds. Hence $U_{\mathcal{B}_{n_k}}(X_\tau) \to U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ , in norm. Thus from the earlier reduction it follows that $U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau) \to U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ in $P$ -measure. So $h_n \vee U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau) \to U_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ in measure, and then in norm. Similarly $h_n \wedge V_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau) \to V_{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(X_\tau)$ in norm (or the result can also be obtainable from the above).

IV. Since $\{h_n, n \geq 1\}$ is uniformly integrable (hence relatively weakly compact), there is a convergent subsequence $h_{n_k} \to h_\infty$ weakly, and $h_\infty \in L^1(\Sigma)$ . However, for each $n$ , $V_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau) \leq h_n \leq U_{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_\tau)$ , a.e. Consequently $V_{\mathcal{B}_\infty}(X_\tau) \leq h_\infty \leq U_{\mathcal{B}_\infty}(X_\tau)$ a.e. must also hold. If it is shown that $h_\infty$ is $\mathcal{B}_\infty$ -measurable, then $h_\infty \in L^1(\mathcal{B}_\infty)$ and then $h_\infty \in \mathcal{D}$ so that $h_{n_k} \to h_\infty$ in norm. Every subsequence will have the same property, and this proves the desired statement. Thus to establish the $\mathcal{B}_\infty$ -measurability, let $g \in L^\infty(\Sigma)$ and consider $E^{\mathcal{B}_n}(g)$ . By the strong convergence of the $\mathcal{B}_n$ , $E^{\mathcal{B}_n}(g) \to E^{\mathcal{B}_\infty}(g)$ , in $L^1$ -norm. Also by the boundedness of $g$ , $h_{n_k} g \to h_\infty g$ in $L^1(\Sigma)$ , as well as a.e., and the sequence is uniformly integrable. So using Vitali's theorem,

We now discuss briefly on alternative approaches to the existence of best predictors in $L^1(\mathcal{B})$ , for a dense set of elements of $L^1(\Sigma)$ , that may "simplify" the analysis of the preceding work. Thus for $X \in L^p(\Sigma)$ , $1 < p < \infty$ , let $Y \in L^p(\mathcal{B})$ , $\mathcal{B} \subset \Sigma$ a $\sigma$ -algebra, be the unique closest element $(Y = Y_X, \|X - Y_X\|_p = \inf \{\|X - Z\|_p : Z \in L^p(\mathcal{B})\})$ , and $\Pi_p(X|\mathcal{B}) = Y_X, V_p(X|\mathcal{B}) = \|X - Y_X\|_p$ . It is seen that $\Pi_p(\cdot|\mathcal{B}) : L^p(\Sigma) \to L^p(\mathcal{B})$ is continuous (but not necessary linear), so that $V_p(\cdot|\mathcal{B}) : L^p(\Sigma) \to IR^+$ is also continuous. One can verify that for $X \in L^p(\Sigma)$ , and $1 < p_0 < p_n < p$ , $\lim_{p_n \searrow p_0} \Pi_{p_n}(X|\mathcal{B}) = \Pi_{p_0}(X|\mathcal{B})$ a.e., and $V_{p_n}(X|\mathcal{B}) \to V_{p_0}(X|\mathcal{B})$ . It was shown by J.A.Cuesta and C. Matrán [1] that the above limit operation implies for any sequence $1 < p_n \leq p, p_n \to 1$ ,

$$
\lim  _ {p _ {n} \rightarrow 1 ^ {+}} \Pi_ {p _ {n}} (X | \mathcal {B}) = \Pi_ {1} (X | \mathcal {B}), \text {a . e .} (\mathrm {P})
$$

and $\Pi_1(X|\mathcal{B})$ is a best predictor of such an $X$ in $L^1 (\mathcal{B})$ . However, it is known that $\bigcup_{p > 1}L^{p}(\Sigma)\stackrel {\subset}{\neq}L^{1}(\Sigma)$ , and for an equality it is necessary that Orlicz spaces
7.3 Nonlinear prediction for nonreflexive spaces

be allowed in the union (cf. e.g., Rao and Ren [1], p.6 after Cor.3). Thus the above argument does not cover all of $L^1(\Sigma)$ , and moreover, there are many other elements in $L^1(\mathcal{B})$ that are best predictors of $X$ . Hence the problem has to be approached using different methods that do not depend on the $L^p(\Sigma), p > 1$ , spaces. This is an important reason for our special study, and a complete analysis involves lattice properties which were crucially utilized by Shintani and Andô [1].
In the next section we indicate how the prediction analysis proceeds for vector valued functions, which has some novelty and complements the preceding work. It is also useful for other applications.
7.4. Some extensions to vector valued functions

In this section, we present some extensions of the preceding results if the functions (i.e., processes) take values in (infinite dimensional) Banach spaces. When once the proper formulation is set forth, the results generalize, and such statements will be useful for applications. Since conditional measures are important in this work, they depend on the Radon-Nikodym theorem which is not always valid in a Banach space. So we first state the concept.
The Orlicz space $L^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ , of $\mathcal{X}$ -valued strongly measurable $f: \Omega \to \mathcal{X}$ , is defined as those elements $f$ for which the gauge norm is finite:

It can be verified that $\left\{L^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}), \| \cdot \|_{(\Phi)}\right\}$ is a Banach space and several properties of the scalar valued case extend. Also, it is known that Banach spaces $\mathcal{X}$ with separable duals $\mathcal{X}^*$ have the RN-property. In particular all reflexive spaces have the RN-property. A set $S \subset L^{1}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is uniformly integrable if for each $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a $\delta_{\varepsilon} > 0$ such that $\mu(A) < \delta_{\varepsilon}$ implies $\int_{A} \|f\| d\mu < \varepsilon$ for all $f \in S$ , which is a direct extension of the scalar concept. Analogous to the scalar case, $M^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}) \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ denotes the closed subspace determined by the simple functions of $L^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ in the norm

A Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is said to have property (H) if $\mathcal{X}$ is strictly convex and $\{x_{n}, n \geq 1\} \subset \mathcal{X}$ converges weakly to $x \in \mathcal{X}$ , and $\| x_{n} \| \to \| x \|$ as $n \to \infty$ , then $\| x_{n} - x \| \to 0$ . If in the definition of (H) above weak convergence is replaced by $\sigma(\mathcal{X}, M)$ -convergence and the conclusion still holds, then it will be termed the $(H_M)$ condition.

3. We assert that $\{x_{n}, n \geq 1\}$ of the above step converges in the $\sigma(\mathcal{X}, M)$ -topology.

For, since the sequence is bounded, it is relatively $\sigma(\mathcal{X}, M)$ compact. Hence by 1., there is a convergent subsequence $\{x_{n_i}, i \geq 1\}$ of $\{x_n, n \geq 1\}$ such that $x^*(x_{n_i}) \to x^*(y_\infty)$ , for all $x^* \in M$ and some $y_\infty \in C_\infty$ . Then

By the strict convexity of $C_{\infty}, y_{\infty} = x_{\infty}$ . Repeating this argument for each subsequence, which necessarily has a convergent subsequence, and has the same limit $x_{\infty}$ , so that the whole sequence must converge to $x_{\infty}$ in the $\sigma(\mathcal{X}, M)$ -topology.

4. We now observe that $\{x_{n}, n \geq 1\}$ is Cauchy. This is so since $x_{n} \to x_{\infty}$ in $\sigma(\mathcal{X}, M)$ -topology, and $\|x_{n}\| \to \|x_{\infty}\|$ so that by (ii) and the hypothesis that $\mathcal{X}$ has $(H_{M})$ property. Thus $\|x_{n} - x_{\infty}\| \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ .
5. Finally consider the decreasing part, which is simpler. Since $C_{-\infty} = \bigcap_{n\geq 1}C_n$ , the set $\{x_{-n}, - 1\geq n\geq -\infty \} \subset C_1$ is bounded, because for $n\leq -1$

But then there is a subsequence $\{x_{-n_i}, i \geq 1\}$ of the $\sigma(\mathcal{X}, M)$ -compact sequence $\{x_{-n}, n \geq 1\}$ having a limit $y_{-\infty}$ . Since for each $n > m$ , $C_{-m} \supset C_{-n}$ , it follows that $y_{-\infty} \in C_{-m}$ for every $m$ , and hence $y_{-\infty} \in C_{-\infty}$ . By the strict convexity of $\mathcal{X}$ , we conclude that $x_{-\infty} = y_{-\infty}$ as above, and then $\| x_{-n} - x_{-\infty} \| \to 0$ , by the same argument. This proves the main part.
6. Let us now verify that the special conditions (I) or (II) imply the main hypothesis.

(I) Indeed, if $\mathcal{X}^*$ has property (F), then by using several geometric properties of Banach spaces (cf., e.g., Day [1], pp.111-114), one can deduce that $\mathcal{X}$ is reflexive and has property (H) with $M = \mathcal{X}^*$ here. This is a consequence of some classical results of V. L. Smulian [1]. It follows that $\mathcal{X}$ is both strictly convex and each of its closed bounded sets is weakly sequentially compact. Thus the main hypothesis is satisfied in this case.

(II) The fact that $\mathcal{X}^*$ is separable implies the same for $\mathcal{X}$ , and the closed unit sphere $S(\mathcal{X})$ in its weak topology is then a metric topology (see Dunford and Schwartz [1], Sec. V.5 here and for the following consequences). Also $\mathcal{X}$ is weakly sequentially complete so that $S(\mathcal{X})$ is a complete metric space. By the same argument, since $\mathcal{X}^*$ is separable, the closed unit sphere $S(\mathcal{X}^{**})$ is a complete metric space in its weak-star topology, and $S(\mathcal{X}^{**})$ is compact in the latter topology (Alaoglu-Birkhoff-Kakutani or ABK-theorem). Also $S(\mathcal{X}) \subset S(\mathcal{X}^{**})$ is weak-star dense. But since both $S(\mathcal{X})$ and $S(\mathcal{X}^{**})$ are complete metric spaces in the same topology, it follows that $S(\mathcal{X}) = S(\mathcal{X}^{**})$ ,
7.4. Some extensions to vector valued functions

where $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathcal{X}^{**}$ under a natural embedding is used. Hence $\mathcal{X}$ is reflexive (by the ABK-theorem again). Since $\mathcal{X}$ has property (H), the main hypothesis is implied, and the result follows as before. $\square$

Remark. It is possible to prove 6., of the above theorem using a diagonal method and the Eberlein-Smulian theorem, in a more elementary fashion, to show the reflexivity of $\mathcal{X}$ . The above short argument is due to C. Foias, and both are given together with the theorem and some complements in Rao [9]. However, it should be observed that the weak sequential completeness is essential for this result.

We now translate the conditions of the above abstract result for Orlicz spaces, which is our main concern here. Let us recall that if $\mathcal{X}$ is a Banach space and $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is the Orlicz space of strongly measurable functions $f$ for which $\| f\|_{(\Phi)} < \infty$ , given by (1), then $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is reflexive iff $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ and $\mathcal{X}$ are both reflexive. Moreover, by Theorems 7.2.3 and 7.5.1 of our companion volume (cf. Rao and Ren [1], p.280 and p.304) $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ has a weakly (or Gâteaux) differentiable norm if $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ , $\Phi'$ is continuous and $\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}^*$ have also weakly differentiable norms. If $\mathcal{X}^*$ has property (F), and $\Psi'$ is also continuous [it is already true that the complementary function $\Psi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ is also], then since every countable set of elements of $L^{\Psi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}^*)$ has a separable range in $\mathcal{X}^*$ , it follows that $L^{\Psi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}^*)$ has property (F). Hence these facts and Theorem 2 imply the following result.

Theorem 3. Let $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ with its derivative $\Phi'$ continuous and strictly increasing. If $\mathcal{X}$ is a reflexive Banach space with property $(F)$ , then $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ [on a probability space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ ] is reflexive. Suppose $\mathcal{B}_n \subset \mathcal{B}_{n+1} \subset \Sigma$ , $\sigma$ -algebras, $-\infty < n < \infty$ and $C_n = L^{(\Phi)}(\mathcal{B}_n, \mu, \mathcal{X})$ . Then the $C_n$ satisfy conditions (i), (ii) of Theorem 2 [actually $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ has property $(F)]$ , and if $f_0 \in L^{(\Phi)}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ then there exist (uniquely) best predictors $f_n \in C_n$ of $f_0$ such that $f_n \to f_{\infty}(f_{-\infty})$ in norm as $n \to \infty (-\infty)$ .

Somewhat strengthened conditions on $\Phi$ can be presented to obtain pointwise convergence of the best predictors in the above theorem. However, we omit that discussion. [For related results and further discussion on this problem, see the recent book, Rao [24], pp.470-478.]
We discuss briefly the smoothness [or $F$ -differentiability] properties of $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ -spaces to explain how "close" one is from a Hilbertian structure.
Recall that $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)$ has twice $F$ -differentiable norm iff
VII. Applications to Prediction Analysis

for all $x_0, y \in S(\mathcal{X})$ , the unit sphere, where $G(x_0, \cdot)$ and $T_{x_0}(\cdot, \cdot)$ are the first and second $F$ -derivatives at $x_0$ of the norm, as $\alpha \to 0$ , the limit existing uniformly in $y \in S(\mathcal{X})$ . Also $G(x_0, y)$ is linear and $T_{x_0}(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a positive definite quadratic form. The following simple characterization of a Hilbert space can be stated for comparison.

Proposition 4. Let $\mathcal{X}$ be a Banach space with $\mathcal{X}^*$ as its dual space. If either $\mathcal{X}$ or $\mathcal{X}^*$ has a twice $F$ -differentiable norm, then $\mathcal{X}$ is a Hilbert space iff one of the following conditions holds:

list_items: (i) $\sup \left\{\frac{1}{\alpha^2} (\| x + \alpha y\| - \| y + \alpha x\|):\alpha >0\right\} <  \infty ,\quad x,y\in S(\mathcal{X}),$
(ii) $\sup \left\{\frac{1}{\alpha^2} (\| x^* + \alpha y^* \| - \| y^* + \alpha x^* \|): \alpha > 0\right\} < \infty, \quad x^*, y^* \in S(\mathcal{X}^*)$

text: Proof. Suppose (i) holds. Then by (9) for $x, y \in S(\mathcal{X})$

$$
\left\| x + \alpha y \right\| = 1 + \alpha G (x, y) + \frac {\alpha^ {2}}{2} T _ {x} (y, y) + o (\alpha^ {2})
$$
and

$$
\left\| y + \alpha x \right\| = 1 + \alpha G (y, x) + \frac {\alpha^ {2}}{2} T _ {y} (x, x) + o (\alpha^ {2}).
$$
Subtracting and dividing by $\alpha^2$ one has

$$
\operatorname * {l i m} _ {\alpha \to 0} \left| \frac {\| x + \alpha y \| - \| y + \alpha x \|}{\alpha^ {2}} - \frac {G (x , y) - G (y , x)}{\alpha} \right| = \frac {1}{2} \left[ T _ {x} (y, y) - T _ {y} (x, x) \right].
$$

Since the first term is bounded, by (i), and the limit exists, the second term in $|\cdot|$ must also be bounded as $\alpha \to 0$ . This holds only if $G(x,y) = G(y,x)$ . But $G(x,x) = \|x\|^2$ and $G(x,\cdot) \in \mathcal{X}^*$ . Hence $G(x,y) = [x,y]$ is an inner product and the norm of $\mathcal{X}$ is given by it. So $\mathcal{X}$ is a Hilbert space.
The case that (ii) holds is similar. The converse is trivial. $\square$

For the Orlicz spaces, we give some results of interest in applications. The study on the $RN$ -property and property $(H)$ of the Orlicz spaces seems to have first appeared in Rao [8], Propositions 6.3 and 3.7), which is now completed in Chen [1], Theorems 3.32 and 3.14). The Gâteaux and Fréchet differentiabilities of the gauge and Orlicz norms are also detailed in Chen ([1], Theorems 2.50, 2.52 and 2.57). See also our volume ([1], pp. 278-283).
Proposition 5. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a pair of complementary $N$ -functions, $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ be a $\sigma$ -finite measure space with $\mu$ being nonatomic. We have the following assertions.
$(RN)$ : $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ (hence $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ ) has $RN$ -property iff $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ ( $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ for $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ ).
7.4. Some extensions to vector valued functions
$(H)$ : $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ (or $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ ) has $H$ -property iff $\Phi$ is strictly convex and $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ ( $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ for $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ ).
$(G)$ : $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ (or $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ ) has $G$ -differentiable norm iff $\Phi'$ is continuous and $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ ( $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ for $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ ).

$(F)$ : $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ (or $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ ) has $F$ differentiable norm iff $\Phi'$ is continuous and $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ ( $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ for $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ ).

From the above results, we assert that if $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ and $\Phi'$ is continuous and strictly increasing (hence $\Psi$ and $\Psi'$ satisfy the same conditions), then $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu), L^{(\Psi)}(\mu)$ (with the gauge norm) and $L^{\Phi}(\mu), L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ (with the Orlicz norm) have properties $(RN), (H), (G)$ and $(F)$ . We note that the nonatomicity is needed only for the necessity proofs.

Proposition 6. If $\Phi$ is an $N$ -function, $\Phi'$ is strictly increasing and $\mathcal{B} \subset \Sigma$ is a $\sigma$ -subalgebra with $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ , then $L^{\Phi}(\mathcal{B}) \subset L^{\Phi}(\Sigma, \mu)$ is a Chebyshev set, (although not every closed convex subset of $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ has this property).

Proposition 7. Let $\mathcal{X}$ be a reflexive Banach space and $L^1 (\mu ,\mathcal{X})$ be the Lebesgue space of $\mathcal{X}$ -valued Bochner (or strongly) integrable functions on a probability (or just a finite measure) space $(\Omega ,\Sigma ,\mu)$ . Then the following statements are mutually equivalent for a subset $\mathcal{H}\subset L^1 (\mu ,\mathcal{X})$ :

list_items: 1. $\mathcal{H}$ is uniformly integrable.
2. $\mathcal{H} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is a bounded set for some $N$ -function $\Phi$ with $\Phi'$ continuous.
3. $\mathcal{H} \subset L^{1}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is relatively weakly sequentially compact.

4. $\mathcal{H} \subset L^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is relatively sequentially compact in the $\sigma(L^{\Phi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}), M^{\Psi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}^{*}))$ topology, where as usual $M^{\Psi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}^{*})$ is the subspace of $\mathcal{X}^{*}$ -valued functions in $L^{\Psi}(\mu, \mathcal{X}^{*})$ determined by its simple functions, $\Psi$ being complementary to the $N$ -function $\Phi$ .

If $\mathcal{X}$ is not reflexive, but has the $RN$ -property, the preceding work gives the implications: 1. $\Leftrightarrow$ 2. $\Leftarrow$ 3. $\Rightarrow$ 4. $\Leftrightarrow$ 6. $\Rightarrow$ 2. and 3. $\Leftrightarrow$ 5.. The following example clarifies the relations between differentiability of norms and of the $N$ -functions. The $F$ -differentiability of norms is much more stringent, as examples show where $\Phi, \Psi$ are infinitely differentiable, but $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu), L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ do not have $F$ -differentiable norms.

Example 8. Let $\Phi(x) = (1 + x)\log(1 + x) - x, x \geq 0$ , so $\Psi(y) = e^y - y - 1, y \geq 0$ . Then $\Phi, \Psi$ are infinitely differentiable and $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ . Let $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ (with the gauge norm) and $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ (with the Orlicz norm) be (scalar) Orlicz spaces on a finite measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ . Then $M^{(\Phi)} = L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ but $M^{\Psi} \neq L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ and the set $M^{\Psi}$ is nowhere dense in $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ . By Proposition 5, $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ has properties $(RN)$ , $(H)$ , and $(G)$ . But $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ and $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ do not have $F$ -differentiable norms. Note that $M^{\Psi}$ has $F$ -differentiable norm, since $(M^{\Psi})^{*} = L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ and $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ is locally uniformly convex (see Chen[1], Theorem 2.28(ii)). Both $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ and $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ are strictly convex. [Recall that $M^{\Psi}$ is not an adjoint space if $\mu$ is nontrivial, we analyze this space further in Example 11 below.]

Consider $\Phi(x) = a\left(\frac{x^3}{3} + \frac{x^2}{2}\right)$ so $\Psi(y) = -\frac{y}{2} + \frac{a}{12}\left[(1 + \frac{4y}{a})^{\frac{3}{2}} - 1\right]$ , $x, y \geq 0$ and $0 < a \leq 1$ . These spaces $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ and $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ are uniformly convex and uniformly smooth. Banach spaces whose norms are seen to be twice $F$ -differentiable. Since $\Phi(x) \neq \frac{cx^2}{2}$ , $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ $\left[= (L^{\Psi}(\mu))^*\right]$ is not a Hilbert space. It can be given an equivalent norm in terms of which it becomes a Hilbert space. Thus $L^{(\Phi)}(\mu)$ is isomorphic and topologically equivalent to a Hilbert space but itself is not one.

So $\Psi$ is uniformly convex on $[0, u_{\varepsilon}]$ . Since $\Psi$ is strictly convex, it is uniformly convex on $[u_{\varepsilon}, 1]$ . Hence $\Psi$ is uniformly convex on $\mathbb{R}^{+}$ . But $\Phi(v)$ , although is uniformly convex for small $v$ , does not have the same property for large $v$ , since $\Phi \notin \nabla_2(\infty)$ . Thus $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ and $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ are not reflexive. It should be observed that the sequence spaces $l^{\Phi}$ and $l^{\Psi}$ are uniformly convex and uniformly smooth.

Bibliographical notes. A prediction [or approximation, also termed extrapolation] problem relative to a convex loss function can be studied either using a norm functional or a modular in the context of Orlicz spaces. One gets equivalent but functionally distinct forms of the solution. We discussed both in Section 1 to clarify the situation. For subspaces $L^{(\Phi)}(B)$ , we can obtain two types of prediction operators. One is a "closed conditional expectation" or prediction operator which depends on the function $x_0$ to be predicted and typically it is a closed unbounded linear operator. The other type is a nonlinear (if the loss is not a quadratic function) best continuous operator, that does not depend on $x_0$ . Thus these are the two distinct possibilities.

Next if we have a sequence of subspaces, and hence a sequence of best predictors, we discussed the existence and mean (or norm) convergence of these sequences in some detail. The work is largely based on Andô [3], Rao [11], and De Groot and Rao [1]. The construction of a best predictor is a difficult problem as it depends, among others, on regular conditional measures, and no algorithms for a computation of these are available. Some generalizations are included in Section 2, and Bru and Heinich [1] is also utilized here. The conditions for linearity in the second formulation depend on several results in functional analysis, the structure of contractive projections in $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , and the existence of complementary subspaces. This is discussed in Andô [3] for the $L^p$ -case and in Rao [11] for the $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ . We included only an abbreviated version of it to save space for other applications.

If the spaces are nonreflexive, such as the (extreme case of) $L^1(\mu)$ space, the work depends on the lattice properties. This was considered by Shintani and Ando [1]. We included an extended treatment of the convergence problem to illuminate the key role played by the existence and regularity of conditional measures, as well as the nonuniqueness of best predictors. We
7.4. Some extensions to vector valued functions

hope that our treatment of Section 3 focuses better on the real mathematical problems lying underneath the analysis of the subject. To make it better appreciated, we have devoted Section 4 for vector valued functions. Here Theorem 4.2 is the abstract version of results in a general Banach space and the others are useful specializations of it in the context of Orlicz spaces. These are based on papers by Rao [9], [10], and other references given in the text. In the $L^p(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ context, the result of Proposition 4.6 was treated by Cuesta and Matrán [1].

It is interesting to observe that the whole (nonlinear) prediction problem depends on important aspects of the geometry and other structure theory of the $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ -spaces. In the next chapters we consider other applications, related to stochastic analysis and PDE, which also use the Orlicz spaces as essential components in obtaining new perspectives.
VII. Applications to Prediction Analysis
Chapter VIII
Applications to Stochastic Analysis

This chapter is devoted to certain classes of stochastic processes and fields that take values in Orlicz spaces. First we show how Orlicz spaces appear quite naturally in important parts of stochastic analysis, by discussing an aspect of large deviations for independent random variables and then for other processes such as Brownian motion. These lead to vector Orlicz spaces and they are also considered. Then we include, in some detail, the regularity properties of stochastic processes and fields that take values in Orlicz spaces where the $N$ -functions appearing there are typically of exponential type. Here the sample path behavior of processes that are so important in many applications is analyzed. Proceeding further, we study a subclass of processes, namely martingales. Also presented include some vector valued extensions of these processes. There are elaborate theories on each of these subjects. We restrict to those parts where Orlicz space theory contributes for a deeper understanding of the subject. The work shows the extent that Orlicz spaces with Young functions growing exponentially fast have special roles to play in this analysis, as distinct from those based on growth restrictions (such as $\triangle_2$ type conditions) in applications of the preceding two chapters as elsewhere. This exemplifies the fact that all Orlicz spaces are useful in applications.
8.1 Large deviations and Young functions

Let us motivate the subject by a simple result in probability theory. Thus consider a sequence of independent (Bernoulli) random variables $\{X_{n}, n = 1,2,\dots\}$ , where $X_{n} = 1$ if the $n$ -th toss of a coin is head and 0 if it is tail each with the same distribution, $P[X_{n} = 1] = p$ and $P[X_{n} = 0] = 1 - p = q, 0 < p < 1, n \geq 1$ . Let $S_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{i}$ and $Y_{n} = S_{n}/n$ . So $Y_{n}$ denotes the number of heads in $n$ independent tosses. Then $Y_{n} \longrightarrow p$ in measure, i.e., for $\varepsilon > 0$ , $P[|Y_{n} - p| > \varepsilon] \longrightarrow 0$ as $n \longrightarrow \infty$ , by the weak law of large numbers. In the above, the convex function $I(\cdot)$ is termed a rate function of the large deviations problem. We generalize this simple but basic result. It can be considered for the multinomial case, the multidimensional $X_{n}$ , empirical measures for $Y_{n}$ , and also more general sequences or processes (or multidimensional parameters, termed random fields). All these are of interest, and one can refer e.g., to Ellis [1] for some of these extensions. Here we discuss the role of Orlicz spaces which appear in such applications.

The result contains that of Proposition 1, where $\tilde{\Lambda}(x) = I(x)$ given there. We included all the tedious details for the result, since it is the basic proposition of large deviations theory, and the key functions for it are the cumulant generating function $\Lambda(\cdot)$ which is convex increasing, and its complementary Young function $\tilde{\Lambda}(\cdot)$ which is always convex and nonnegative. Neither of these is symmetric. Thus Orlicz space theory is an ingredient but simple adjustments (such as symmetrization) are necessary. Returning to Cramér's theorem and Laplace's method, one can abstract the following facts. Let $X_{n}, n \geq 1$ , be a sequence of random variables on a probability space $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ with values in a topological space $\mathcal{X}$ , usually taken as a separable complete metric (=Polish) space, with its Borel $\sigma$ -algebra $\mathcal{B}$ . If $X_{n} \longrightarrow X_{0} \in \mathcal{X}$ , in probability, then the sequence is said to obey the large deviations principle (LDP) if the convergence is exponentially fast. More precisely, if there is a mapping $I: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow [0, \infty)$ , continuous (or only lower semi-continuous), called the rate function, with the property that $\{x: I(x) \leq k\}$ is compact for each $0 < k < \infty$ , and for each $A \in \mathcal{B}$
where $A^0 = int(A)$ and $\overline{A} = cl(A)$ .

Thus Cramér's theorem shows that the large deviations principle is valid if the $X_{n}$ are independent, identically distributed with the cumulant generating function $\Lambda$ of $X_{1}$ existing and $\mathcal{X} = \mathbb{R}, I = \tilde{\Lambda}$ , the complementary Young function of the convex $\Lambda$ , as the rate function. where $\langle x^{*},X_{1}\rangle$ on $\mathcal{X}^*\times \mathcal{X}$ , is the duality pairing. Then $\tilde{\Lambda}$ can be defined as

VIII. Applications to Stochastic Analysis

New problems arise in the multidimensional case. To proceed further, it is necessary to generalize (i) the Laplace's principle to the topological set $\mathcal{X}$ [usually for $\mathcal{X}$ a separable Banach space], and then (ii) establish, for the large deviations principle, the inequalities in (36). For the latter some aspect of independence (or conditional one at least) is needed, in addition to the variational calculus for (38). The desired result was first obtained by Varadhan [1], on which the following developments are based. Then the second part for a number of different types of Markov processes was also established by Donsker and Varadhan in a series of papers (one can refer to the survey by Varadhan [2] for references). We state the basic results on both aspects without details and then will indicate the underlying Orlicz (or more exactly Fenchel-Orlicz) space analysis.
A generalized Laplace method, i.e., of (30), can be obtained as a consequence of the large deviations principle. This is the content of the following theorem, due to Varadhan [1].

Theorem 3. (a) Let $\{X_{n}, n \geq 1\}$ be a sequence of random variables on $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ with values in a separable complete metric (=Polish) measurable space $(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{B})$ , whose distributions [or image probability measures] $P_{n} = P \circ X_{n}^{-1}$ obey the large deviations principle with a rate function $I: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow [0, \infty)$ , so that $I(\cdot)$ is lower semicontinuous, convex and $\{x : I(x) \leq k\} \subset \mathcal{X}$ is compact for all $k \geq 0$ . Then for any continuous function $f: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$ which is bounded below, one has
Moreover, the rate function $I$ of the sequence is unique.

Conversely, if $I: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow [0, \infty)$ is a rate function satisfying (39) for all bounded continuous $f: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$ , then the sequence $\{X_n, n \geq 1\}$ obeys the large deviations principle on $\mathcal{X}$ with rate function $I$ .

(b) If $\mathcal{Y}$ is another Polish space, $h: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ is a continuous mapping and $I: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow [0, \infty)$ is a rate function, then $J: \mathcal{Y} \longrightarrow [0, \infty)$ , given by $J(y) = \inf \{I(x): x \in h^{-1}(y)\}, y \in f(\mathcal{X})$ , and $= \infty$ otherwise, is a rate function on $\mathcal{Y}$ . Further if $\{X_n, n \geq 1\}$ obeys the large deviations or the Laplace principle on $\mathcal{X}$ with rate $I$ , then $\{f(X_n), n \geq 1\}$ obeys the large deviations principle on $\mathcal{Y}$ with rate function $J$ .
Since generally $J$ takes fewer values than $I$ , the result of (b) above is termed the contraction principle. We omit the proof of this result and refer

Since for all this work $I(\cdot)$ plays a key role and $I: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow [0, \infty)$ is defined, we discuss its properties for special vector spaces $\mathcal{X}$ . Here again convex function analysis plays a significant role. That $\mathcal{X}$ may be infinite dimensional is seen from the next result on the Brownian motion, which extends the preceding theorem.
