Let $\{X_{t}, 0 \leq t \leq b\}$ be a Gaussian process with mean $E(X_{t}) = 0$ , and continuous covariance given by $r(s, t) = E(X_{s}X_{t}) = u(s \wedge t)v(s \vee t)$ , $u(\cdot), v(\cdot) \geq 0$ and $(\frac{u}{v})(t)$ strictly increasing. If $v(t) \equiv 1$ and $u(t) = t$ , $0 < t < \infty$ , the resulting process is called the (standard) Brownian motion (or BM, or Wiener) process. It has independent increments, since with Gaussian hypothesis this is equivalent to showing for $0 < s < t < a < b$ , $E[(X_{t} - X_{s})(X_{a} - X_{b})] = 0$

which is clearly true. In case $u(s) = \sigma^2 e^{\beta s}$ and $v(s) = e^{-\beta s}$ , so that $r(s, t) = \sigma^2 e^{-\beta |s - t|}, \sigma^2 > 0, \beta > 0$ , it is called the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (or O. U.) process. It may be shown that both have continuous sample paths, and the BM $X_t$ takes values in $\mathcal{X} = C_0[0, b]$ , the space of continuous functions vanishing at $t = 0$ and with the uniform norm. If $f \in \mathcal{X}$ , then $X_t + f(t)$ is also a BM (or O. U. process) with mean function $f$ and covariance $r(s, t)$ . Let $P_0$ and $P_f$ be the image measures of, or induced by, the processes $[P_0 = P \circ X^{-1}$ and $P_f = P \circ (X + f)^{-1}]$ which are Gaussian on the $\sigma$ -algebra $\mathcal{B}$ of $\mathcal{X}$ , with means 0 and $f$ , but having the same covariance $r(\cdot, \cdot)$ . Now by the fundamental Gaussian dichotomy [special case of the classical Hajek-Feldman] theorem, $P_f$ and $P_0$ are either mutually absolutely continuous or singular. If $P_f < P_0$ [hence $P_f \sim P_0$ ] then $f$ is called an admissible mean of $P_0$ . It is known that the set of such admissible means is a vector space $H \subset \Omega$ which can be endowed with a Hilbert space metric [or norm], and then the RN-derivative $\frac{dP_f}{dP_0}$ can be calculated. It is given, even for general $u, v$ above, in the following.

Remark. The computations for the lower bound use only the sample path continuity of the process $\{X_{\varepsilon}(t), t > 0\}$ and the fact that the covariance function is "triangular", i.e., of the form $u(\cdot, \cdot)v(\cdot)$ . The work appears to extend to the O. U. process. But the upper bound uses the symmetry and reflection principle for the BM, and hence the computations do not easily go over to the O. U. process. So these properties are not available for the latter. It is known that a centered Gaussian process with a triangular covariance such as $r = uv$ above (including the O. U. process), is Markovian. Using other methods a great many of the large deviations results are extended by Donsker and Varadhan in a series of papers of fundamental significance. Another important method due to Freidlin [1] will now be described briefly.

It is appropriate here to indicate how Schilder's theorem admits a extension to a large class of (not necessarily Markovian) Gaussian processes, following Freidlin (cf. Freidlin and Wentzell [1], Sec. 3.4), based on the crucial observation that centered Gaussian processes with continuous covariances are linear integral transforms of BM processes, so that the LDP theory of the latter may be extended. Here is a more precise description.

Let $X = \{X_{t}, t \in T = [a, b]\}$ be a centered second order process with a continuous covariance function $r: T \times T \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$ . Then the associated integral operator $R: f \longmapsto (Rf)(t) = \int_{T} r(s, t) f(s) ds$ , is compact (actually Hilbert-Schmidt) on the Hilbert space $L^{2}(T, dt)$ , nonnegative definite, and hence has a unique (positive) square root $R^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . [This is true if only $r$ is square integrable.] The subspace $R^{\frac{1}{2}}(L^{2}(T, dt))$ has the following remarkable property, noted by T. S. Pitcher [1]: Take $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ as a canonical space (i.e., $\Omega = \mathbb{R}^{T}$ , $\Sigma =$ cylinder $\sigma$ -algebra, $P: \Sigma \longrightarrow [0, 1]$ a probability) as described prior to Theorem 5 above, let $f: \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be an admissible mean of $P$ so that $P_{f} < < P$ where $P_{f}$ is the measure of $X(t, \cdot) + f(t)$ . If $M_{p}$ is the set of all admissible means, then $M_{p} \subset R^{\frac{1}{2}}(L^{2}(T, dt))$ and there is equality if moreover $X$ is Gaussian (and not otherwise, cf., Rao [24], Sec. V.1 on this
VIII. Applications to Stochastic Analysis

topic and for details). Further $M_p$ is a Hilbert space under a norm given as follows: $f_i \in M_p \Rightarrow f_i = R^{\frac{1}{2}}h_i$ for some $h_i \in L^2(T, dt), i = 1, 2$ and the inner product of $M_p$ is obtained by $(f_1, f_2) = (R^{\frac{1}{2}}h_1, R^{\frac{1}{2}}h_2) = (Rh_1, h_2)$ , the latter is the scalar product of $L^2(T, dt)$ . Using the spectral theorem (cf., also Dunford-Schwartz [1], VI.9.59) one finds that $R^{\frac{1}{2}}$ is given by a kernel $G$ as:

$$
(R ^ {\frac {1}{2}} f) (t) = \int_ {T} G (s, t) f (s) d s, f \in L ^ {2} (T, d t)
$$

and one has $r(s, t) = \int_{T} G(s, u) G^{*}(u, t) du$ ( $G^{*}(u, t) = G(t, u)$ ). If we define a new process $\widetilde{X}(t) = \int_{T} G(s, t) dB_{s}$ , where $\{B_{s}, s \in T\}$ is the standard BM, then $\{\widetilde{X}(t), t \in T\}$ is a Gaussian process with mean zero and covariance $r$ , so that $X$ and $\widetilde{X}$ can be identified. This means that $\widetilde{X} = X(= \int_{T} G(s, \cdot) dB_{s})$ is a linearly transformed process of $B$ , observed earlier.

Thus $I(f) = \frac{1}{2} (R^{-1}f, f)$ , in the above notation of inner product of the space $R^{\frac{1}{2}}(L^{2}(T, dt))$ . Then Freidlin's extension is given by:

Theorem 7. Let $X^{\varepsilon}(t) = \varepsilon X(t), \varepsilon > 0$ , be a centered Gaussian process for $t \in T = [a, b]$ , with a continuous covariance function. Then $X^{\varepsilon}$ obeys the LDP with the rate functional $I: L^{2}(T, dt) \longrightarrow [0, \infty)$ , defined in (60).

The proof is given in the above monograph, even for a process with values in $\mathbb{R}^d$ without much difficulty, and we omit it here. It has alternative procedures, excluding the representation through the BM, and many other developments are possible. We leave this discussion for specialized treatments, and observe that $I(\cdot)$ is again a convex functional on $L^2(T, dt)$ . So we need consider vector Young functions and the corresponding Orlicz spaces on $\mathcal{X}$ , possibly of infinite dimension, which is the topic of the next section.
8.2 Infinite dimensional extensions and vector Orlicz spaces

The finite dimensional Cramér theorem, i.e. Theorem 1.4, admits an extension to infinite dimensional spaces containing all the finite dimensional sets as subspaces with a suitable (and natural) compatibility condition on overlaps. The collection of spaces (of different dimensions) are then termed a "projective system" for which we can extend the above result. Let us make
8.2 Infinite dimensional extensions and vector Orlicz spaces
these terms precise and establish an abstract version which also shows some new features leading to general Fenchel-Orlicz spaces.

We recall the concept. A family of probability spaces $\{(\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}), \alpha \in J\}$ , where $J$ is an index set, is called a projective system if (i) $J$ is directed (i.e., there is an order relation $<$ such that for $\alpha, \beta \in J, \alpha < \beta$ is defined for at least some pairs, and for any $\alpha, \beta \in J$ , there is a $\gamma \in J$ such that $\alpha < \gamma$ and $\beta < \gamma$ ), and (ii) there are connecting mappings and $g_{\alpha \beta}: \Omega_{\beta} \longrightarrow \Omega_{\alpha}$ for each $\alpha < \beta$ in $J$ , satisfying $g_{\alpha \alpha} = \text{id}$ identity, and for $\alpha < \beta < \gamma$ , $g_{\alpha \beta} \circ g_{\beta \gamma} = g_{\alpha \gamma}$ . An example of such a system, which actually will be of interest below, is: $\Omega_{\alpha} = \mathbb{R}^{\alpha}$ and $g_{\alpha \beta}: \mathbb{R}^{\beta} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{\alpha}$ for $\alpha < \beta$ with $\alpha = (1, 2, \dots, m)$ and $\beta = (1, 2, \dots, m, m + 1, \dots, n)$ , “ $<$ ” is inclusion (in other cases a lexicographic) ordering, so $\mathbb{R}^{\alpha}$ and $\mathbb{R}^{\beta}$ can be identified as the $m$ and $n$ -dimensional spaces ( $m \leq n$ ), with $g_{\alpha \beta}$ as coordinate projection mappings, $g_{\alpha \beta}(x_1, \dots, x_n) = (x_1, \dots, x_m)$ . We may denote such $g_{\alpha \beta}$ as $\prod_{mn}: \mathbb{R}^n \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^m$ , $m < n$ , to specify this case.

Consider a set $\Omega \subset \times_{\alpha \in J} \Omega_{\alpha}$ such that the "thread", $\omega = (\omega_{\alpha}, \alpha \in J)$ is in $\Omega$ , iff for each $\alpha < \beta$ , $g_{\alpha \beta}(\omega_{\beta}) = \omega_{\alpha}$ . In the above example $g_{\alpha \beta} = \prod_{\alpha \beta}, \Omega_{\alpha} = IR^{\alpha}$ , then $\Omega$ can be identified with $\times_{\alpha \in J} IR^{\alpha}$ , the cartesian product. In the general case $\Omega$ can be empty (e.g., let $\Omega_{n} = (0, \frac{1}{n})$ , $n \geq 1$ and for $n > m$ let $g_{mn}: \Omega_{n} \to \Omega_{m}$ where $g_{mn}$ is an inclusion, then $\{(\Omega_{n}, g_{mn}), m < n\}$ is a projective system, but $\Omega = \emptyset$ ). We need to restrict the spaces so that $\Omega$ is nonempty. A good sufficient condition for this is sequential maximality (s.m.) introduced by Bochner in his basic formulation of this subject. Thus the family is said to obey the s.m. condition if for each sequence $\alpha_{1} < \alpha_{2} < \dots$ from $J$ and any $\omega_{\alpha_{i}} \in \Omega_{\alpha_{i}}$ satisfying $g_{\alpha_{i} \alpha_{i+1}}(\omega_{\alpha_{i+1}}) = \omega_{\alpha_{i}}, i \geq 1$ , there exists an element $\omega = (\omega_{\alpha}, \alpha \in J) \in \Omega$ such that $g_{\alpha}(\omega) = \omega_{\alpha}$ where $g_{\alpha \infty}: \times_{\alpha \in J} \Omega_{\alpha} \to \Omega_{\alpha}$ . When this holds, $g_{\alpha} = g_{\alpha \infty}$ is onto, i.e., $g_{\alpha}(\Omega) = \Omega_{\alpha}$ for all $\alpha \in J$ . The s.m. condition is automatic if $g_{\alpha \beta} (= \prod_{\alpha \beta}), g_{\alpha} = \prod_{\alpha}(: IR^{J} \longrightarrow IR^{\alpha})$ are coordinate projections. Moreover, we can also show it to be automatic if each $\Omega_{\alpha}$ is a compact space. The measure spaces $(\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha})$ are said to form a projective system, if $g_{\alpha \beta}: \Omega_{\beta} \longrightarrow \Omega_{\alpha}$ is measurable, i.e., $g_{\alpha \beta}^{-1}(\Sigma_{\alpha}^{-1}) \subset \Sigma_{\beta}$ for $\alpha < \beta$ , and $P_{\alpha} = P_{\beta} \circ g_{\alpha \beta}^{-1}$ so that $P_{\alpha}$ is the image probability measure of $P_{\beta}$ . Thus $\{(\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha \beta}): \alpha < \beta$ in $J\}$ is termed a projective system of probability spaces, which generalizes the Fubini-Jessen concept of infinite product measures. The relation that $P_{\alpha} = P_{\beta} \circ g_{\alpha \beta}^{-1}$ is just the consistency requirement for these measures and if $\Omega_{\alpha} = IR^{\alpha}, \Omega_{\beta} = IR^{\beta}$ , then this simply means that $P_{\alpha}$ is the marginal probability of $P_{\beta}$ . But the general case is also of interest since the random variables can take values in different spaces. Thus if $\Omega = \varprojlim (\Omega_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ , called the projective limit of the
VIII. Applications to Stochastic Analysis

system, with $g_{\alpha} : \Omega \longrightarrow \Omega_{\alpha}$ , one defines $\Sigma_0 = \bigcup_{\alpha \in J} g_{\alpha}^{-1}(\Sigma_{\alpha})$ and observes that $\Sigma_0$ is an algebra and let $\Sigma = \sigma(\Sigma_0)$ , called the cylinder $\sigma$ -algebra. Then a set function $P_0$ can be defined as $P_0(A) = P_0(g_{\alpha}^{-1}(A_\alpha)) = P_\alpha(A_\alpha)$ , $A \in \Sigma_0$ , where $A = g_{\alpha}^{-1}(A_\alpha)$ for some $\alpha \in J$ by definition. It is not hard to see that $P_0$ is uniquely defined (does not depend on $\alpha$ ) and that $P_0$ is finitely additive on $\Sigma_0$ . In general one can not assert more. It is here we need to assume that each $\Omega_\alpha$ is a topological space, $g_{\alpha \beta} : \Omega_\beta \longrightarrow \Omega_\alpha$ is continuous (it is clearly onto and $g_\alpha : \Omega \longrightarrow \Omega_\alpha$ is required to be continuous), each $\Sigma_\alpha$ is Borel, and what is decisive, each $P_\alpha$ is a regular probability measure. Under these conditions $P_0$ is $\sigma$ -additive (but still need not be regular!), and if $P$ is its (unique) extension to $\Sigma$ , then we set $P = \varprojlim P_\alpha$ and $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ is called the projective limit measure space. It satisfies immediately $P \circ g_{\alpha}^{-1} = P_\alpha$ for all $\alpha \in J$ . [For a proof of these statements, see e.g., Rao [20], p.354, and we shall not reproduce the proof here.] In order to conclude that $P$ is regular, one has to demand the Prokhorov $(\varepsilon, K_\varepsilon)$ - condition, namely for each $\varepsilon > 0$ , there exists a compact set $K_\varepsilon$ such that

so $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ will be a regular projective limit space. [For a detailed proof, see the above reference pp.359-364.] This condition is automatic if each $\Omega_{\alpha}$ is compact, and then $\Omega = \varprojlim (\Omega_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ is also compact as well as $P$ regular.

With the above setup we can present an abstract large deviations result from $(\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha})$ to their limit. It is essentially due to Dawson and Gartner [1]. The result depends on the contraction principle discussed after the statement of Theorem 1.3.

Theorem 1. Then as $\varepsilon \to 0$ , the system obeys the large deviations principle (LDP), iff the limit triple does. When this happens, if $I_{\alpha}$ and $I$ are the corresponding rate functions, then

Proof. By hypothesis $g_{\alpha} : \Omega \longrightarrow \Omega_{\alpha}$ is onto (a consequence of the s.m. condition) and $g_{\alpha}$ and $g_{\alpha \beta}$ are continuous. Suppose that the limit measure $P^{\varepsilon}$ of $(\Omega, \Sigma, P^{\varepsilon}) = \varprojlim (\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}^{\varepsilon}, g_{\alpha \beta})_{\alpha < \beta}$ , obeys the LDP. Then by Theorem 1.3 (the contraction part), one deduces that (3) holds and $I_{\alpha}$ is a rate function on $(\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}^{\varepsilon})$ . Since $g_{\alpha}$ is continuous, the set $g_{\alpha}^{-1}(\{x\})$ is compact so we can replace "inf" by "min" in (3).

For the reverse direction, involving more work, let $(\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}^{\varepsilon})$ admit the LDP for each $\alpha$ with a rate function $I_{\alpha}(\cdot)$ . If $I(\cdot)$ is given by (2), we claim that $I$ is a rate function for $(\Omega, \Sigma, P^{\varepsilon})$ satisfying the LDP. Since for each $\alpha \in J, I_{\alpha}(\cdot)$ is a rate function, the level set $A_{\alpha}^{y} = \{x : I_{\alpha}(x) \leq y\}$ for $y \geq 0$ is compact and by the compatibility condition, $P_{\alpha}^{\varepsilon} = P_{\alpha \beta}^{\varepsilon} \circ g_{\alpha \beta}^{-1}$ and $I_{\beta}(\cdot)$ is a rate function, so that for $\alpha < \beta, A_{\beta}^{y}$ which is compact satisfies $g_{\alpha \beta}(A_{\beta}^{y}) = A_{\alpha}^{y}$ . Thus $\{(A_{\alpha}^{y}, g_{\alpha \beta}) : \alpha < \beta \text{ in } J\}$ is a projective system of compact spaces for each $y \geq 0$ and so $A^{y} = \lim_{\leftarrow}(A_{\alpha}^{y}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ exists, nonempty and is compact by the earlier result. To conclude that $I(\cdot)$ is a rate function, one has to verify that it satisfies the appropriate upper and lower bounds of the LDP as $\varepsilon \searrow 0$ .

Thus let $O \subset \Omega$ be a nonempty open set and $\omega \in O$ . From the fact that $\Omega = \lim_{\leftarrow} (\Omega_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ and the definition of the projective limit topology, there is an open set $\mathcal{U}_{\alpha} \subset \Omega_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha}^{-1}(\mathcal{U}_{\alpha}) \subset O$ and $g_{\alpha}(\omega) \in \mathcal{U}_{\alpha}$ . So from $P^{\varepsilon}(O) \geq P^{\varepsilon}(g_{\alpha}^{-1}(\mathcal{U}_{\alpha}))$ and $I = \sup_{\alpha \in J} I_{\alpha} \circ g_{\alpha}$ , we have

$$
\liminf_{\varepsilon \searrow 0}\log P^{\varepsilon}(O)\geq \liminf_{\varepsilon \searrow 0}\log P^{\varepsilon}_{\alpha}(\mathcal{U}_{\alpha})\geq -I_{\alpha}(g_{\alpha}(\omega)).
$$
Taking $sup$ over $\alpha \in J$ and since $\omega \in O$ is arbitrary, the lower bound for $I$ is obtained.

Regarding the upper bound, let $F \subset O$ be any closed set, consider a compact level set $A_{\alpha}^{y}, y \geq 0$ for $I_{\alpha}$ . On $A_{\alpha}^{y}, I_{\alpha}(x) \leq y$ , and the upper bound for $I$ is obtained immediately to be $y$ on $F \cap A^{y}$ , where $A^{y} = \varprojlim (A_{\alpha}^{y}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ , a compact nonempty set, if this is nonempty. So let $F \cap A^{y} = \emptyset$ and suppose $\inf \{I(x): x \in F\} > y_{0}$ for some $y_{0} \geq 0$ . We obtain the upper bound for the LDP in this case also to conclude the argument, since the result follows if there is no such $y_{0}$ . Now the projective limit theory implies that if $F_{\alpha} = g_{\alpha}(F)$ , then $F = \varprojlim (\overline{F}_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ where $\overline{F}_{\alpha}$ is the closure of $F_{\alpha}$ , although $F_{\alpha}$ may not be closed. To see this, for $\alpha < \beta, g_{\alpha \beta}(\overline{F}_{\beta}) \subset \overline{F}_{\alpha}$ since the $g_{\alpha \beta}$ are continuous. Thus $F \subset \varprojlim (\overline{F}_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ . On the other hand if $\omega \in F^{\mathrm{c}}$ , the complement of $F$ , there is an open set $U_{\beta} \subset \Omega_{\beta}, \beta \in J$ such that $\omega \in g_{\alpha \beta}^{-1}(U_{\beta}) \subset F^{\mathrm{c}}$ . Hence $g_{\beta}(\omega) \in U_{\beta} \subset F_{\beta}^{\mathrm{c}}$ implying that $g_{\beta}(\omega) \notin \overline{F}_{\beta}$ and hence $\omega \notin \varprojlim (\overline{F}_{\alpha}, g_{\alpha \beta})$ . This implies that the above asserted equality

holds. Now $\overline{F}_{\alpha} \cap A_{\alpha}^{y_0}$ is compact for each $\alpha$ , since $I_{\alpha}$ is a rate function, and $F \cap A^{y_0} = \varprojlim (\overline{F}_{\alpha}) \cap \varprojlim A_{\alpha}^{y_0} = \varprojlim (\overline{F}_{\alpha} \cap A_{\alpha}^{y_0})$ . But each $\overline{F}_{\alpha} \cap A_{\alpha}^{y_0}$ is compact and $F \cap A^{y_0} = \emptyset$ so that $\overline{F}_{\alpha} \cap A_{\alpha}^{y_0} = \emptyset$ for some $\alpha = \alpha_0$ essentially because $F \cap A^{y_0}$ is an intersection of compact sets. But $(\Omega_{\alpha_0}, \Sigma_{\alpha_0}, P_{\alpha_0}^{\varepsilon})$ obeys the LDP and so $\lim_{\varepsilon \searrow 0} \sup_{\varepsilon > 0} \log P_{\alpha_0}^{\varepsilon}(F) \leq -y_0$ . This shows the upper bound holds in this case also and $(\Omega, \Sigma, P^{\varepsilon})$ satisfies the LDP.

To apply this result, it is useful to get some classes of spaces $(\Omega_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}^{\varepsilon})$ for which an LDP holds. If each $\Omega_{\alpha}$ is a finite dimensional vector space, then Theorem 1.4 applies to each of them. However in a locally convex topological space $\mathcal{X}$ , the projective limit of all finite dimensional subspaces, whose collection $J$ is ordered by inclusion, is known to be larger than $\mathcal{X}$ . More precisely, let $J$ be the collection of all subspaces, of a topological vector space $\mathcal{X}$ , of finite codimension, so that $F \in J$ iff $\mathcal{X} / F$ , the quotient space, is finite dimensional. Then $J$ becomes directed if for $\alpha, \beta \in J, \alpha < \beta$ iff $\alpha \supset \beta$ . Let $\prod_{\alpha \beta}: \mathcal{X}_{\beta} \longrightarrow \mathcal{X}_{\alpha}$ where $\mathcal{X}_{\alpha} = \mathcal{X} / \alpha$ and $\prod_{\alpha}: \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \mathcal{X}_{\alpha}$ is the coordinate projection, $\alpha \in J$ . With this understanding, $\{(\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}, \prod_{\alpha \beta}): \alpha < \beta$ in $J\}$ becomes a projective system of vector spaces, and each is finite dimensional. If $\mathcal{X}$ is assumed to be a locally convex metric space (e.g., a Banach space), then $\Omega = \varprojlim (\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}, \prod_{\alpha \beta})$ exists, but usually larger than $\mathcal{X}$ . If $\mathcal{X}^{*}$ is the adjoint space of $\mathcal{X}$ and $(\mathcal{X}^{*})'(\supset \mathcal{X}^{**})$ is the algebraic dual of $\mathcal{X}^{*}$ , the projective limit topology of $\Omega$ is weaker than the topology of $\mathcal{X}$ , and if it is endowed with $\sigma((\mathcal{X}^{*})', \mathcal{X}^{*})$ -topology, then $\Omega$ and $(\mathcal{X}^{*})'$ can be identified in the sense that there is a linear bijection between these two spaces. [See, for instance, (Rao [22], Prop.1, on p.34) on this problem, and references therein.] It can also be shown that, with $\Sigma_{\alpha}$ denoting the Borel $\sigma$ -algebra of $\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}$ , the limit of the projective system $\{(\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}, \prod_{\alpha \beta}): \alpha < \beta$ in $J\}$ exists and equals $(\mathcal{X}, \Sigma, P)$ iff it satisfies the $(\varepsilon, K_{\varepsilon})$ -condition (1) of Prokhorov's, as discussed in the above reference. Since Theorem 1.4 implies that, for $\varepsilon > 0$ , the $(\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}^{\varepsilon})$ satisfies the LDP where $\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}$ is finite dimensional, the following is a consequence of the above theorem and discussion which we record for a convenient reference.

Corollary 2. Let $\mathcal{X}$ be a locally convex topological vector space and $\{(\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}, \prod_{\alpha \beta}):\alpha < \beta$ in $J\}$ be a projective system of finite dimensional subspaces of $\mathcal{X}$ . Consider the projective system of probability spaces $\{(\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}^{*}, \Sigma_{\alpha}, P_{\alpha}^{\varepsilon}, \prod_{\alpha \beta}):\alpha < \beta$ in $J\}$ such that $(\mathcal{X}_{\alpha}^{*}$ is the adjoint space of $\mathcal{X}_{\alpha})$ :

One of the important points raised by this as well as the previous result is to find ways of evaluating the rate function $\tilde{\Lambda}_p(\cdot)$ , which is the (Young) complementary function of $\Lambda_p(\cdot)$ , the "cumulant" generating function of the process (or the measure $P$ ). A class of processes were considered by Pinsky [1] for an evaluation of $\tilde{\Lambda}_p(\cdot)$ which the reader may consult for an appreciation of the problem. Here to explain this further, we include some functional analysis properties, by discussing Orlicz spaces based on the pair $\Lambda_p(\cdot)$ and $\tilde{\Lambda}_p(\cdot)$ and present a few results in that direction.

If $\Lambda_p(\cdot)$ (and $\widetilde{\Lambda}_p(\cdot)$ ), considered as a convex function on $\mathbb{R}^n$ (and on $(\mathbb{R}^n)^* = \mathbb{R}^n$ ), is desired, then one may turn to Rockafellar [1] for an incisive analysis, and we refer to this work when needed. Definition 4. Let $\mathcal{X}$ be a Banach space, $\Lambda : \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb{R}}^{+}$ a Young function with $\{x : \Lambda(tx) < \infty \text{ for some } t > 0\} = \mathcal{X}$ , $\Lambda(-x) = \Lambda(x)$ and $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ a measure space. Then the Fenchel-Orlicz space $\mathrm{L}^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is the set of (strongly) measurable $f : \Omega \longrightarrow \mathcal{X}$ such that the (Bochner) integral $\int_{\Omega} \Lambda(kf) d\mu < \infty$ for some $k > 0$ . The gauge functional (essentially norm) $\| \cdot \|_{(\Lambda)} : L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X}) \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{+}$ is given as
Using the standard arguments, extending the classical Orlicz spaces $L^{\Lambda}(\mu)$ as in Rao and Ren [1], one can establish the following:

Proposition 5. The space $(L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X}), \| \cdot \|_{(\Lambda)})$ is a normed linear space, when equivalent classes under the norm functional $\| \cdot \|_{(\Lambda)}$ are identified. If either $\mathcal{X}$ is finite dimensional or $\Lambda(\cdot)$ is such that $\Lambda(x) \geq \alpha \| x\| + \beta$ for fixed $\alpha, \beta > 0$ , $(x \neq 0)$ then $L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is also complete, i.e., it is a Banach space. The last condition is equivalent to the continuity of $\tilde{\Lambda}(\cdot)$ at $0$ , or alternatively equivalent to the assertion that $\| f_n - f\|_{(\Lambda)} \to 0$ implies convergence in measure, i.e., that $\mu\{\omega \in \Omega : \| f_n - f\| \geq \varepsilon\} \longrightarrow 0$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$ .

The condition $\Lambda(x) \geq \alpha \|x\| + \beta$ is of "support line" type, and it is equivalent to saying that $\lim_{n \to \infty} \inf \{\Lambda(x) : \|x\| = n\} = +\infty$ , termed uniformly large at infinity. This is always satisfied if $\mathcal{X}$ is finite dimensional. Without such a growth condition $L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ need not be complete; but it is always satisfied for the cumulant generating functions. This is also equivalent to saying that the complementary function $\tilde{\Lambda}(\cdot)$ is continuous at 0. With these observations the completeness proof is a modification of that given usually (cf., e.g., Rao and Ren [1], p.67). It will be omitted, and a detailed account can be found in Turett [1] who extended the standard Orlicz space analysis to $L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ and characterized $(L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X}))^{*}$ when $\Lambda$ satisfies a $\triangle_2$ -condition, defined below. Indeed much of the theory of the corresponding scalar Orlicz theory extends (nontrivially). We present a few analogs of the classical analysis (cf., Rao and Ren [1], Sections 4.1 and 4.2) for our applications.

A Young function $\Lambda : \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb{R}}^{+}$ is said to satisfy a $\triangle_{2}-$ condition for large values (for all values) if $\Lambda(\cdot)$ is bounded on balls of $\mathcal{X}$ and there exist $K_{1} > 0$ and $K_{2} > 0$ such that $\Lambda(2x) \leq K_{2}\Lambda(x)$ for $\|x\| \geq K_{1}$ (for $K_{1} = 0$ ). Theorem 7. Let $\Lambda : \mathcal{X} \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb{R}}^{+}$ be a Young function as in Definition 4 and $\tilde{\Lambda} : \mathcal{X}^{*} \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb{R}}^{+}$ be its complementary function. The space $L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ of strongly measurable and “ $\Lambda$ -integrable” functions is defined as before. Then $L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is reflexive if both $\Lambda, \tilde{\Lambda}$ satisfy $\triangle_{2}$ -conditions for all values and $\mathcal{X}$ is reflexive. On the other hand, $L^{\Lambda}(\mu, \mathcal{X})$ is strictly convex (in that the sphere has no line segments) if $\Lambda$ is strictly convex and satisfies the $\triangle_{2}$ -condition.

We omit the details (cf., Turett [1]) and turn to the classical spaces in which the Young function $\Lambda(\cdot)$ has exponential growth (as in, e.g., Rao and Ren [1], Chapter II). We then can immediately apply the analysis to stochastic functions.
8.3 Regularity of stochastic functions and Orlicz spaces

We now consider more general process than $\{Z(t), t \geq 0\}$ to be in $L^{\Lambda}(P)$ , using Theorem 1. It is interesting to note that, when the exponential $N$ -function defined by $\Lambda_p(u) = \exp \{|u|^p\} - 1, p > 1$ , the corresponding Orlicz spaces $L^{\Lambda_p}(P)$ are closely related to the classical Lebesgue spaces. This fact allows us to discuss the regularity of stochastic functions with values in such spaces using some sharper tools through Besov-Orlicz spaces which are extensions of Orlicz-Sobolev spaces. We recall these classes and then present the desired results on stochastic processes.

To analyze the smoothness of functions in $L^{\Lambda_p}(P)$ , where $\Lambda_p(\cdot)$ is as above, one considers the modulus of continuity of a function and adds it with the norm condition to isolate subsets of Orlicz spaces having these additional properties. The above class of function spaces is of interest here, since the Brownian motion and, more generally, a large class of diffusion processes (i.e., those that are solutions of Ito-type stochastic differential equations), take their values in these spaces as seen below. Actually their values will be in separable subspaces of Besov-Orlicz spaces. The following conditions "nearly characterize" the latter subspaces. These are obtained in terms of series expansions of $f$ in $B(\Lambda_p, \omega_{\alpha,\beta}, \infty)$ using a Schauder basis of the space. They are the indefinite integrals of Haar functions which are orthonormal, but the Schauder functions, although not orthogonal, are continuous and linearly independent which are convenient in this context. The idea is to expand each element $f \in B(\Lambda_p, \omega_{\alpha,\beta}, \infty)$ in a (Fourier-Haar) series and consider the linear span of those whose $n^{th}$ term (or the "tail" part) tends to zero at a suitable rate, so that this space will be separable. The details follow.
Recall that the Haar functions on $[0,1]$ are given as: $h_1 = 1$ and for $j\geq 1$ and $1\leq k < 2^{j}$

8.4 Martingales and Orlicz spaces
Let us introduce the martingale concept for vector and operator functions. Thus if $\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y}$ are Banach spaces, then $B(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y})$ denotes the Banach space of

bounded linear mappings from $\mathcal{X}$ into $\mathcal{Y}$ under the operator (i.e., uniform) norm. If $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ is a probability space and $f: \Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y})$ is a mapping, we wish to define the conditional expectation of $f$ suitably, then introduce the martingale concept, and present some (convergence) results explaining the role of Orlicz space techniques in the analysis.

To discuss the convergence of vector valued martingales, it is necessary to recall conditional expectations of these general objects. Thus, if $f: \Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y})$ is a function, it is termed strongly measurable if the vector function $fx: \Omega \longrightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ has the (strong) measurability property for each $x \in \mathcal{X}$ , i.e., there exists a sequence $g_n (= g_n^x)$ of simple functions $g_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \chi_{A_i}, a_i \in \mathcal{Y}, A_i \in \Sigma$ , disjoint, satisfying $\| fx - g_n \| \longrightarrow 0$ as $n \longrightarrow \infty$ . Then $\int_{\Omega} fxdP$ can be defined as a Bochner integral if $\int_{\Omega} \| fx \| dP < \infty$ . The only new property is (5). This follows from the classical fact that, when $\mathcal{X}$ is separable, there is a denumerable norm-determining set of elements $\Gamma$ so that $\sup \{\|f_x\|_{\mathcal{Y}} : x \in \Gamma, \|x\| \leq 1\} = \|f\|$ and the left side being countable and each $\|f_x\|$ measurable, $\|f\|$ is a measurable $(\Sigma)$ function (even though $f$ itself need not be measurable for $\Sigma$ ). Then $\|f_x\|_{\mathcal{Y}} \leq \|f\|\|x\|_{\mathcal{X}}$ which gives (5) on taking supremum on $\Gamma$ . With these observations, the concept of an operator or vector martingale can be introduced as follows.

Definition 3. Let $\mathcal{B}_n\subset \mathcal{B}_{n + 1}\subset \Sigma$ be a sequence of $\sigma$ -algebras from $(\Omega ,\Sigma ,P)$ , a probability space, and $f_{n}:\Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y})$ be a strongly $\mathcal{B}_n$ -measurable strongly integrable operator sequence, denoted by $\{f_n,\mathcal{B}_n,n\geq 1\} (\subset B(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y}))$ . It is called a strong operator martingale if $\mathcal{E}^{\mathcal{B}_m}(f_n) = f_m$ , a.e., in that for each $x\in \mathcal{X},\mathcal{E}^{\mathcal{B}_n}(f_{n + 1}x) = f_nx$ , a.e., setting $x = 1$ if $\mathcal{X} = \mathbb{R}$ . When $\mathcal{X} = \mathbb{R}$ , it is a (strong) vector martingale sequence in $\mathcal{V}$ . If $\mathcal{X} = \mathcal{Y} = \mathbb{R}$ , and $E^{\mathcal{B}_m}(f_n)\geq (\leq)f_m$ , it is a (scalar) sub (super)martingale.
VIII. Applications to Stochastic Analysis

We recall that a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is said to have the Radon-Nikodym property, if each vector measure $\nu : \Sigma \longrightarrow \mathcal{X}$ , with a finite variation measure $\mu$ , has the RN-derivative $\frac{d\nu}{d\mu}$ which is Bochner integrable on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ . It is known that all reflexive Banach spaces and those that are separable adjoint spaces have the RN-property. On the other hand, $L^1[0,1]$ and $C_0$ , or any Banach space which contains an isomorphic copy of either of these spaces do not have the RN-property. The following result can now be presented.

Using a Stone representation theorem of $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ onto a nice topological space, the proof can be reduced to the case where $\nu$ is $\sigma$ -additive. Corollary 6. Let $\{f_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ be given by $f_n = \mathcal{E}^{\mathcal{B}_n}(f)$ for some Bochner integrable $f: \Omega \to \mathcal{X}$ , an arbitrary Banach space. The preceding result for vector martingales extends to strong operator martingales, which are useful for applications. As usual, the second adjoint of $\mathcal{V}$ is denoted $\mathcal{V}^{**}$ .

Theorem 7. Let $\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y}$ be Banach spaces of which $\mathcal{V}$ has the RN-property. If $(\Omega ,\Sigma ,P)$ is a probability space, $f_{n}:\Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y})$ is strongly measurable for $\mathcal{B}_n$ where $\mathcal{B}_n\subset \mathcal{B}_{n + 1}$ and $\{f_n,\mathcal{B}_n,n\geq 1\}$ is a strong martingale, suppose that $\sup_{n\geq 1}\int_{\Omega}\| f_nx\| dP = K_x < \infty$ for each $x\in \mathcal{X}$ . Then there is a strongly measurable and strongly integrable $f_{\infty}:\Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y}^{**})$ such that $\lim_{n\to \infty}\| f_nx - f_\infty x\| _y = 0$ , a.e., for all $x\in \mathcal{X}$ . If moreover $\mathcal{X}$ is separable and $\sup \{K_x:||x||\leq 1\} < \infty$ , then $\| f_n - f_\infty \|$ is uniformly measurable [i.e., strongly measurable in the Banach space $\mathcal{Z} = B(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y}^{**})$ ], $\lim_{n\to \infty}\| f_n - f_\infty \| _Z = 0$ a.e., even though $f_{n},f_{\infty}$ are not necessary uniformly measurable.
If $\mathcal{X}$ is separable, for the strong martingale $\{f_n,\mathcal{B}_n,n\geq 1\}$ in $B(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{Y})$ the seven statements below are equivalent.

A consequence, corresponding to Corollary 6, can be stated as follows, which is recorded for some applications.

Corollary 8. Let $\mathcal{X}$ be a separable and $\mathcal{Y}$ be any Banach space. If $\mathcal{B}_n \subset \mathcal{B}_{n+1} \subset \Sigma$ , of a probability space $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ , and $f: \Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y})$ is a strongly measurable and strongly integrable function, let $f_n = \mathcal{E}^{\mathcal{B}_n}(f)$ be a strong operator, defined in Corollary 2. Then there is a strongly measurable $f_\infty: \Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}^{**})$ such that $f_n \longrightarrow f_\infty$ strongly a.e. Also $\|f_n - f_\infty\|$ is (uniformly) measurable and tends to zero a.e., as $n \to \infty$ . Moreover, there is an $N$ -function $\Phi$ and $\alpha_0 \geq 1$ such that

$$
\lim  _ {n \rightarrow \infty} E \left(\Phi \left(\frac {\| f _ {n} - f _ {\infty} \|}{\alpha_ {0}}\right)\right) = 0.
$$

Here one does not need to assume the RN-property of $\mathcal{V}$ , since the strong martingale $\{f_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ is uniformly integrable and $f_{\infty} = \mathcal{E}^{\mathcal{B}_{\infty}}(f)$ is the desired limit as in Corollary 6 with values in the larger space $B(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}^{**})$ . For decreasing martingales, i.e., $\mathcal{B}_n \supset \mathcal{B}_{n+1}$ and $\{f_n = \mathcal{E}^{\mathcal{B}_n}(f_1), n \geq 1\}$ , the uniform integrability is built in. So the preceding work slightly simplifies. However, the limit $\nu(A) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \int_A f_n dP, A \in \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} \mathcal{B}_n$ is $\sigma$ -additive, but its density is not guaranteed unless $\mathcal{V}$ has the RN-property. Otherwise the procedure is the same, and we can make the following assertion.

Corollary 9. Let $\{f_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ be a strong operator martingale on $\Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y})$ , where $\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}$ are Banach spaces with $\mathcal{Y}$ having the RN-property. Then there is an $f_{\infty} : \Omega \longrightarrow B(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}^{**})$ such that $f_n \longrightarrow f_{\infty}$ strongly a.e., the limit $f_{\infty}$ being strongly measurable and $\{f_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ uniformly strongly integrable.
8.4 Martingales and Orlicz spaces

We now give two applications of these results. The first one characterizes the strong convergence of a sequence of partial sums of independent stochastic processes, and the second establishes the structure of a sequence which is a martingale in both increasing and decreasing directions at the same time. The first result is related to the work by Walsh [1], and is as follows.

Proposition 10. Let $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ and $(T, \mathcal{I}, \mu)$ be probability spaces with $(T \times \Omega, \mathcal{I} \otimes \Sigma, \mu \otimes P)$ as their product. Suppose $X_{n}: T \times \Omega \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}, n \geq 1$ are measurable functions such that $\{X_{n}(t, \cdot), n \geq 1\}$ are mutually independent, $t \in T$ , $X_{n}(\cdot, \omega) \in L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ for a Young function $\Phi \in \Delta_2 \cap \nabla_2$ , for almost all $\omega \in \Omega$ , and $E(X_{n}(t, \cdot)) = 0$ . Let $X_{0}$ be in the unit ball of $L^{\Phi}(\mu \otimes P)$ and $S_{n}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} X_{k}(t)$ . Then the following statements are equivalent:
(i) $\lim_{n\to \infty}\| S_n(\cdot ,\omega) - X_0(\cdot ,\omega)\|_{(\Phi)} = 0,a.a.(\omega).$
(ii) For each $g \in \Gamma \subset L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ , where $\Gamma$ is norm determining for $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , $\Psi$ being the complementary function of $\Phi$ , one has

$$
\lim  _ {n \rightarrow \infty} \left\langle g, (X _ {0} - S _ {n}) (\cdot , \omega) \right\rangle = 0, \quad a. a. (\omega),
$$
where $\langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle$ denotes the duality pairing of (the reflexive) $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ and $L^{\Psi}(\mu)$ .
When either of these equivalent conditions holds, then there is an $N$ -function $\Phi_1$ such that $S_n(\cdot, \omega) \longrightarrow X_0(\cdot, \omega)$ in $\Phi_1$ -mean for a.a. $(\omega)$ .
Proof. Since by the Hölder inequality

$$
| \langle g, (X _ {0} - S _ {n}) (\cdot , \omega) \rangle | \leq 2 \| g \| _ {(\Psi)} \cdot \| (X _ {0} - S _ {n}) (\cdot , \omega) \| _ {(\Phi)} \longrightarrow 0, a. a. (\omega),
$$
by (i), we obtain (ii) for all $g\in L^{\Psi}(\mu)$

Conversely, let (ii) hold. By hypothesis $E(S_{n}(t,\cdot)) = 0$ and hence by Fubini's theorem (and $L^{\Phi}(\mu)\subset L^{1}(\mu),E(\langle g,S_{n}(\cdot ,\cdot)\rangle) = \langle g,E(S_{n}(\cdot ,\cdot))\rangle = 0$ ). But by (ii), $S_{n}\longrightarrow X_{0}$ in $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ , and all quantities are integrable. Since $L^{\Phi}(\mu)\subset L^{1}(\mu)$ , again by Fubini's theorem $S_{n}(t)\longrightarrow X_{0}(t)$ a.e., and since $S_{n}(t)$ is a partial sum of independent centered random variables (so is a martingale) as well as the hypothesis that $X_0(t,\cdot)\in L^\Phi (P)\subset L^1 (P)$ , it follows from a classical scalar martingale convergence theorem (cf., e.g., Doob [1], p.337), that $E(X_0(t)) = 0 = E(S_n(t))$ .
Next let $\mathcal{B}_n = \sigma \{X_k(t), t \in T, 1 \leq k \leq n\}$ . Then $\{\langle g, S_n \rangle, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ is a martingale, since $g(\cdot)$ is non-stochastic, and one has

Now $E^{\mathcal{B}_n}$ commutes with the $\mu$ -integral, $\mathcal{B}_n$ is independent of $X_k, k > n$ and so $E(X_k) = 0$ for all $k$ . It follows that $E^{\mathcal{B}_n}(X_0) = S_n$ , a.e. Thus $\{S_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ is a uniformly integrable martingale with values in $L^1(P, L^\Phi(\mu))$ . Then by Corollary 8, in which $\mathcal{X} = IR$ , $S_n \longrightarrow S_\infty$ a.e., and hence $S_\infty = X_0$ a.e., from the preceding work. Moreover, under either of the conditions (i) or (ii), $\{S_n, \mathcal{B}_n, n \geq 1\}$ is a uniformly integrable martingale with values in the Orlicz space $L^\Phi(\mu)$ . So by Corollary 8 again, there is an $N$ -function $\Phi_1$ and an $\alpha_0 \geq 1$ such that $\lim_{n \to \infty} E(\Phi_1(\|S_n - X_0\|_{(\Phi)}) / \alpha_0)) = 0$ . This is the last statement. As already observed, here $\Phi_1$ (the de la Vallée Poussin's function) can be chosen to satisfy a $\triangle_2$ -condition (cf., e.g., Rao [16], p.21) so that $\alpha_0 = 1$ is possible. Thus all the statements are established.

In the above result, if one can weaken the hypothesis to allow that $\Phi$ jumps to infinity at a finite point, so that $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ may be identified with $L^{\infty}(\mu)$ and (i) of the proposition becomes uniform convergence of $S_{n}(t) \longrightarrow X_{0}(t)$ outside of a P-null set. Since $L^{\infty}(\mu)$ is isometrically isomorphic to the continuous function space $C(\tilde{T})$ on a compact (Stone) space, replacing $T$ by $\tilde{T}$ one may eliminate the P-null set. This stronger result with $T$ as a compact metric space was established by Walsh [1] using direct methods. An interesting consequence of the latter is the following. If $\{\xi_{n}, n \geq 1\}$ is a sequence of independent standard normal random variables and $\{F_{n}(\cdot), n \geq 1\}$ are Schauder functions in $L^{2}[0,1]$ , then taking $S_{n}(t) = \xi_{n}F_{n}(t)$ , one has $X_{0}(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} S_{n}(t)$ , converging with probability 1, defining the Brownian motion, the convergence is uniform as well as in $L^{\Phi_{1}}(P)$ -norm where $\Phi_{1}$ is an $N$ -function satisfying the $\Delta_{2}$ -condition. This implies, on taking for the derivatives, $F_{n}'(t) = \sin 2\pi nt$ and $\cos 2\pi nt$ , the uniform convergence of the series

where $\{\xi_n, \tilde{\xi}_n, n \geq 1\}$ are mutually independent standard normal random variables. It includes a classical theorem of Paley and Wiener [1] who established it for a subsequence of the partial sums. Our proposition above does not apply to this situation, but may be extended to cover it.

If the martingale of the proposition is square integrable, at least when $\mathcal{X} = \mathcal{Y} =$ scalars, then (12) shows that the assertion of the proposition is immediate, but the general case is clearly more involved.

Bibliographical notes. Applications in many parts of mathematics, some times not only use important results from Orlicz space analysis, they also show the essential need for extensions of the latter theory. This is well exemplified by the applications in analysis of large deviations treated in Section 1. They show the necessity of the use of Young (and $N$ -) functions on multidimensional spaces. The complementary $N$ -function is called a rate (or an action) function in this theory. One appreciates the subject better, if the work on large deviations is seen from the underlying Orlicz space structure as well. A detailed account of large deviations is included to explain the importance of this application, in our context. We have already given references in the text. Here one has to reiterate that the important work of Cramér [1] and its deep generalization and expansion in the hands of Donsker and Varadhan in a series of fundamental papers have shaped this subject. Several monographs, after Varadhan's [2], have detailed the work. These include Dembo and Zeitouni [1], Deuschel and Stroock [1], Dupuis and Ellis [1], Ellis [1], and equally for the continuous parameter processes Freidlin and Wentzell [1]. The convex analysis of Rockefeller [1] is an indispensable companion in the study of Young functions on $\mathbb{R}^n$ , $n > 1$ . Since we need to consider this multidimensional case as a natural problem, and thus the section has grown longer.

An application of large deviations to processes, as in the work of Schilder [1] included already, shows that one needs Young functions defined actually on infinite dimensional vector spaces. Some of it can be reduced to finite dimensions, using the projective limit theory of measure spaces on spaces such as $\mathbb{R}^n$ . This and the direct analysis of Orlicz, or what are properly called Fenchel-Orlicz, spaces is discussed in Section 2. The work of Turett's [1], and the earlier work of Uhl's [1] are relevant in this situation. But much
8.4 Martingales and Orlicz spaces
remains to be done from a functional analysis point of view. We thus see several problems that are natural in this context, motivating further research.

From another angle, notice that the less frequently used classical Orlicz space $L^{\Lambda}(\mu)$ when $\Lambda$ grows exponentially has a deep interest in analyzing the regularity of stochastic functions. We showed some of this in Section 3. A detailed account of many more such regularity problems for processes was included in Buldygin and Kozachenko [1]. An interesting aspect of these Orlicz spaces, defined by exponential $N$ -functions, is that they have a close relation to Besov-Orlicz spaces, and we indicated it in Section 3. The work gives a good motivation why one should study these, somewhat special spaces. It was noted there that the boundedness of sample paths and the large deviation of random processes and fields as solution of diffusion equations actually need Besov-Orlicz spaces. They are related to Orlicz-Sobolev spaces (cf., also Adams [1]), p.218 ff.) of interest in PDE analysis, to be discussed in the next chapter.

From another view the results of Orlicz spaces related to uniformly integrable sets, or equivalently bounded sets in $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ when $\Phi$ is an $N$ -function, are of interest in martingale analysis. We presented it for a general operator valued case, since it reveals the structure of these processes better without invoking too many special results. The basic vector measure theory, as found in Diestel and Uhl [1], and Dinculeanu ([1], [2]), is used at some places. The introduction of conditional expectation of operators is not difficult, and we included the discussion in its natural generality. The treatment in Section 4 follows a streamlined account from Rao [8].

Our aim in this chapter has been to show how substantive problems in various parts of analysis (here stochastic processes) lead to a better appreciation of Orlicz space theory. We continue this point of view with PDE and related nonlinear problems in considerable detail in the following chapter.
VIII. Applications to Stochastic Analysis
Chapter IX
Nonlinear PDEs and Orlicz Spaces

This chapter contains an outline of applications to PDEs with the ideas and flexibility afforded by Orlicz spaces as well as their differential structure. Several classical results admit satisfactory extensions, often giving optimal results, in the context of Orlicz spaces. These include removable singularities, comparison principles, embedding theorems utilizing the structure of Orlicz-Sobolev spaces as well as some inequalities of the Hardy-Littlewood type useful in those spaces. We also consider continuity of certain nonlinear operators on Orlicz spaces that are of interest in composition and Nemitsky type operations. Although explicit solutions of PDEs are difficult, we indicate such a possibility through stochastic differential equations. The areas opened up for this treatment are many, and we show how these function spaces expand possibilities in applications.

The desired space then is denoted $W^{m,p}(G) = \{f: G \to \mathbb{R}, \| f\|_{m,p} < \infty \}$ . One can verify that $\{W^{m,p}(G), \| \cdot \|_{m,p}\}$ is a Banach space and search for functions $f \in W^{m,p}(G)$ that solve the corresponding equation (1). Such spaces, incorporating the smoothness (or differentiability) properties for norm (3), were constructed and analyzed by S. L. Sobolev in [1], now called Sobolev spaces, and they play a key role in this analysis. For a finer analysis, it is necessary to embed these spaces (continuously) into $L^p(G)$ with Lebesgue measure, denoted $W^{m,p}(G) \hookrightarrow L^p(G)$ . The following comprehensive result was established early by Sobolev for a suitable class of domains $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ .

Let $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ have the following: the cone property, i.e., at each $x \in G$ there is a finite cone $C_x \subset G$ congruent to a fixed right spherical cone $C$ so that each $C_x$ is obtained by a rigid motion of $C$ . The existence of such a solution in the Sobolev space is the content of the following classical theorem due to Friedrichs.

Theorem 2. A solution of the equation, $L(f) = g$ , has square integrable distribution (or weak) derivatives of order $2n + p$ in a relatively compact open subset $G_{1}$ of $G$ (written $G_{1} \subset \subset G$ ) if $g$ has similar weak derivatives of order $p$ . This means $f \in W^{p + 2n}(G_1)$ . If $p = +\infty$ , then there exists an $f_{0} \in C^{\infty}(\overline{G}_{1})$ such that $f = f_{0}$ a.e. on $G_{1}$ .

Taking $C_{\alpha \beta}$ as constants, one sees the importance of Sobolev spaces for the PDE analysis from the start. This result also explains why Sobolev spaces are so important in PDE studies.

Recall that a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is embedded in another Banach space $\mathcal{V}$ , written $\mathcal{X} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ , if there is an absolute constant $C > 0$ such that $\| \hat{x} \|_{\mathcal{Y}} \leq C \| x \|_{\mathcal{X}}$ , $x \in \mathcal{X}$ , where $\hat{x}$ is the image of $x$ in $\mathcal{Y}$ . Thus (b) and hence (20) follow. By the fact that $\Phi_{*}^{-1}(1) < \infty$ and $\Phi_{*}^{-1}(\infty) = \infty$ , it is immediate that $\Phi_{*}(0) = 0$ and $\Phi_{*}(\infty) = \infty$ . This shows that $\Phi_{*}$ is a strictly convex N-function. $\square$

Thus using the notation of Definition 4, let $W_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G)$ be the closed linear span of $C_c^\infty(G)$ in the norm (14) and let $H_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G)$ be the similar span of $C^{m}(G)$ in the same norm, where $C^{m}(G)$ is the space of $f: G \to \mathbb{R}$ with $D^{\alpha}f$ continuous for $|\alpha| \leq m$ . The following structural result for Orlicz-Sobolev spaces was established in our earlier volume and will be stated for use in proofs below (cf. Rao and Ren [1], Theorem 3, p.382) and it extends an earlier result of N.G. Meyers and J. Serrin for the classical Sobolev spaces.

Theorem 6. The set $(W_{\theta, \beta}^{m, \Phi}(G), N_{\theta, \beta}^{m, \Phi}(\cdot))$ is a Banach space and $H_{\theta, \beta}^{m, \Phi}(G)$ is a closed subspace of $W_{\theta, \beta}^{m, \Phi}(G)$ . Moreover if $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ (globally if $G$ is noncompact, and at infinity if $G$ is compact), then $H_{\theta, \beta}^{m, \Phi}(G) = W_{\theta, \beta}^{m, \Phi}(G) = W_{\theta, \beta}^{m, \Phi}(G)$ .

This result will be needed in establishing a generalization of Theorem 1 to the Orlicz-Sobolev spaces to be presented below. It is due to Donaldson and Trudinger [1] for bounded domains, and an extension for the unbounded case is due to Adams [1]. We consider $\beta = 1$ and $\theta(x) = |x|$ in what follows.

So far we discussed the case of Dirichlet's equation (1) of finite order. But its analog of infinite order has some new features. We briefly indicate
9.1 Orlicz-Sobolev spaces for PDEs

this case, which is not yet thoroughly investigated, to complete the picture. Recall that $W_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G)$ is the Orlicz-Sobolev space of order $m$ with weights $\beta$ , and $W_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G) \supset W_{\theta,\beta}^{m+1,\Phi}(G)$ where the norms satisfy $N_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(f) \leq N_{\theta,\beta}^{m+1,\Phi}(f)$ . Moreover, these are Banach spaces. Their closed subspaces $W_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G)$ have the property (from the standard structure theory of Orlicz spaces) that $(W_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G))^* = W_{\zeta,\beta}^{m,\Psi}(G)$ where $(\Phi,\Psi)$ and $(\theta,\zeta)$ are complementary N-pairs. We replace $m$ by $-m$ for dual spaces, since as $m$ increases the $W_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G)$ decrease and hence their dual spaces satisfy $(W_{\theta,\beta}^{m,\Phi}(G))^* \subset (W_{\theta,\beta}^{m+1,\Phi}(G))^*$ . With this property in mind, we may define $W_{\theta,\beta}^{\infty,\Phi}(G)$ and $W_{\zeta,\beta}^{-\infty,\Psi}(G)$ quite generally as follows. Let $\Theta = \{\theta_\alpha, |\alpha| = 0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ and $\Phi = \{\Phi_\alpha, |\alpha| = 0, 1, 2, \dots\}$ be two sequences of Young functions and $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a domain. One can verify that both $W_{\Theta, \beta}^{\infty, \Phi}(G)$ and $W_{\Theta, \beta}^{\infty, \Phi}(G)$ are Banach spaces, but they can be trivial, i.e., may have only "0" element. The Dirichlet problem (1) can be restated for these spaces as follows. This work allows one to generalize most of the analysis appearing in Dubinskij [1]. Note that for a nontrivial solution, it is necessary that $W_{\theta, \beta}^{\infty, \Phi}(G)$ and hence $W_{\zeta, \beta}^{-\infty, \Psi}(G)$ be nontrivial, and Proposition 10 gives a reasonable sufficient condition for this to happen.

It is clear from the above work that an explicit expression for the solution of a PDE can not be expected. After the existence and uniqueness results, we would like to find its (continuous) dependence on the initial data. One establishes that the solution belongs to a function space such as $L^{\Phi}(G)$ , and its properties should be studied. On the other hand, the mapping $L$ of equation (1) can be considered, and one finds that it generates a class of (linear) operators forming a semi-group (or an "evolutionary" family) on the space $L^{\Phi}(G)$ which is typically positive. The latter property of the semigroup is intimately related to Markov processes and thus to stochastic theory, more particularly to diffusions. This connection leads to an extensive analysis
9.1 Orlicz-Sobolev spaces for PDEs

when both these areas are combined. Here we indicate how this relation gives an "explicit" representation of solutions in terms of expectations of certain functionals of diffusion processes, thereby showing other possibilities of this method with connections to some work in the preceding chapter.

with initial condition $u(0,x) = f(x)$ . Here the $(k \times k)$ -matrix of coefficients $A(x) = [a_{ij}(x)]$ is assumed to be positive definite and has constant rank (i.e., is independent of $x$ ), and $c(\cdot)$ is bounded and continuous satisfying a Lipschitz condition. It is desired to obtain a unique solution in a class of functions $u$ , when $f(\cdot)$ is a bounded continuous function. The probabilistic method is to associate a stochastic differential equation (SDE) to (37) whose solution is a Markov process, thus relating it to a class of continuous semi-groups whose generators are the operators $L$ of (37). This will produce essentially the desired solution. We now describe this method precisely but briefly.

An interesting part of this method is its representation given by (40) which is also known as the Feynman-Kac formula. Details and extensions of this result may be found in the leisurely treatment of the subject given by Freidlin [1]. If in (37) or (38) $L$ is replaced by $L + \varepsilon L^1$ or $\sigma$ by $(\sigma + \sqrt{\varepsilon} \sigma')$ where $\varepsilon \searrow 0$ , then one can study the stability properties of the solutions $u^\varepsilon$ or $X_t^\varepsilon$ , and the work leads to the large derivations analysis of Section 8.1 with their rate functions [namely vector Young functions] playing an important part. This is also discussed in Freidlin [1]. One can moreover ask whether the class $\mathcal{K}$ above, of solutions of the Cauchy problem, may be replaced by an Orlicz space $L^\Phi(\mathbb{R}^k)$ . These questions lead to separate, nontrivial parts of the subject and it connects with many deeper aspects of PDE analysis. Instead of the Cauchy problem, the Dirichlet equation can be studied similarly. Without going to PDE, but concentrating on the semi-group part, these equations (both Cauchy and others) were discussed by Goldstein [1], and a comparison of both approaches will be rewarding. We shall now proceed to some other applications of PDE and Orlicz spaces.
9.2 Removable singularities, PDE and Orlicz spaces

A well-known result in complex function theory, due to Riemann, states that a holomorphic function $f$ on the punctured disc with the property that $\lim_{z \to 0} zf(z) = 0$ is holomorphic through out the disc. So the singularity at the origin is removable. Bochner has generalized this as: if $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is an open domain and $\mathcal{L}(G)$ is a class of functions, $A \subset G$ is closed, such that for any $f \in \mathcal{L}(G), f(x)[d(x,A)]^q$ is small uniformly for $x$ in compact subsets of $G, q > 0, d(x,A)$ being the distance of $x$ from $A$ , then any singularity of the $f$ 's on $A$ can be removed, i.e., $A$ is a "removable singular set" for $\mathcal{L}(G)$ . A generalized version of this concept is as follows. Let $P(x,D) = \sum_{|\alpha| \leq m} a_\alpha(x) D^\alpha$ be a partial differential operator with a formal adjoint denoted as $P^*(x,D)$ given
9.2 Removable singularities, PDE and Orlicz spaces
by

$$
P ^ {*} (x, D) f = \sum_ {| \alpha | \leq m} (- 1) ^ {| \alpha |} D ^ {\alpha} (a _ {\alpha} f), \quad f \in \mathcal {L} (G),
$$

where $a_{\alpha} \in C^{\infty}(G)$ . Then a closed set $A \subset G$ is said to be removable for $\mathcal{L}(G)$ if for each $f \in \mathcal{L}(G)$ such that $P(x, D)f(x) = 0$ for $x \in G \setminus A$ , one has $P(x, D)f(x) = 0$ for all $x \in G$ . If $C \subset \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and $\varepsilon > 0$ , let $C_{\varepsilon}$ be an $\varepsilon$ -neighborhood of $C$ , i.e., $C_{\varepsilon} = \{x : d(x, C) < \varepsilon\}$ . Then a consequence of Bochner's (by now classical) result is that if $\mathcal{L}(G)$ is the set of locally $p^{th}$ power ( $p \geq 1$ ) $\mu$ -integrable functions ( $\mu = \text{Leb. meas.}$ ) and $f \in \mathcal{L}(G)$ satisfies $P(x, D)f = 0$ on $G \setminus A$ as well as

$$
\lim  _ {\varepsilon \searrow 0} \inf  [ \varepsilon^ {d - n} \mu (C _ {\varepsilon}) ] ^ {p ^ {\prime}} \| \chi_ {C _ {\varepsilon}} f \| _ {p} = 0,
$$

where $d = n - mp'$ , $p' = p / (p - 1)$ and $C_{\varepsilon}$ is a neighborhood of $C$ for any compact $C \subset A$ , then $A$ is a removable set. Here $m$ is the order of the operator $P(x, D)$ . This result immediately raises the question that the Lebesgue measure $\mu$ may be replaced by a (sharper) metric measure on $\mathbb{R}^n$ , such as the Minkowski content or the Hausdorff measure and obtain a more precise characterization. In fact, Bochner has also obtained in the 1950's a result replacing $\mu(C_{\varepsilon})$ by upper Minkowski content. Then these sharper assertions were established in a refined form for (weakly) $p^{th}$ power integrable functions, using Hausdorff measure by Harvey and Polking [1]. Their result was extended to Orlicz spaces by Shapiro [1] whose theorem will be presented below after recalling the concept of a Hausdorff (metric) measure, since it is a somewhat specialized notion which however is a basic component of geometric measure theory and is also needed in this study.

Definition 1. Let $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be an open set, $\Phi$ an N-function and $A \subset G$ a closed set. If $P(x, D)$ is a partial differential operator of degree $m$ , recalled above, then $A$ is termed a removable set for $P(x, D)$ relative to $L^{\Phi}(G)$ , if $P(x, D)f = 0$ weakly in $G \setminus A$ in the sense that

$$
\int_ {G \backslash A} P (x, D) f (x) \cdot g (x) d \mu (x) = \int_ {G \backslash A} P ^ {*} (x, D) g (x) \cdot f (x) d \mu (x) = 0
$$
for $f \in L^{\Phi}(G)$ and $g \in C_c^\infty (G)$ , implies that $P(x,D)f = 0$ weakly on $G$ , i.e., the integral is 0 on $G$ itself, with $\mu$ again as the Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}^n$ .

Moreover, $H_{h}(\cdot)$ is regular in the sense that for each $A\subset M$ there is a $G_{\delta}$ set $B\supset A$ such that $H_{h}(A) = H_{h}(B)$ . [For a quick view of this result and related properties, see e.g., Rao [20], Sec. 2.5. p.86ff.] If $h(t) = t^{\alpha}$ , $\alpha >0$ , then $H_{h}(\cdot)$ is denoted $H_{\alpha}(\cdot)$ and called the Hausdorff $\alpha$ -dimensional (outer) measure, and we call $H_{h}(\cdot)$ a generalized Hausdorff metric (outer) measure or simply an $h$ -measure. If $H_{\alpha}(\cdot)$ is the $\alpha$ -dimensional (outer) measure, then a set $B\subset M$ is said to have Hausdorff dimension $\beta$ , defined as

To appreciate the sharpness of these concepts, one notes that the Cantor set $C \subset [0,1]$ , with the usual metric, has Lebesgue measure zero, but its Hausdorff dimension $\beta(C) = \log 2 / \log 3$ . Thus $\mu(C) = 0$ , but $H_{\alpha}(C) > 0$ for $\alpha \neq 1$ implying the sharpness of $H_{\alpha}$ . If $\alpha = 1$ , $M = \mathbb{R}$ , then $H_1(\cdot) = \mu^*(\cdot)$ (the Lebesgue outer measure) and if $M = \mathbb{R}^n$ , then $H_{\alpha}(A) = 0$ for $A \subset M$ , satisfying $\alpha > \beta(A)$ , and $\beta(A) \leq n$ ; and if $\alpha = n$ , then $H_n(\cdot) = K_n\mu(\cdot)$ , where $K_1 = 1$ and for $n \geq 2$ , $K_n = \left(\frac{4}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}}\Gamma\left(1 + \frac{n}{2}\right) > 1$ , which is a nontrivial result. However, every countable set $C$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has $\beta(C) = 0$ . These facts will be used in what follows. A readable account (with proofs of the above statements), completely devoted to Hausdorff measures, is the book by Rogers [1] which is a helpful source. We now present Shapiro's [1] result on removable sets in $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ , stated in some generality obtainable from his proof.

Theorem 2. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a complementary pair of $N$ -functions and $h(t) = t^n \Psi(t^{-m})$ , $1 \leq m \leq n-1$ for $n \geq 2$ , be nondecreasing. Suppose that $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is an open set and $A \subset G$ a closed $\sigma$ -compact $\sigma$ -finite subset for $H_h(\cdot)$ , i.e., $A = \cup_{k=1}^{\infty} A_k$ , $A_k$ compact and $H_h(A_k) < \infty$ . If $P(x, D) = \sum_{|\alpha| \leq m} c_\alpha(x) D^\alpha$ is a partial differential operator of order $m$ with $c_\alpha \in C^m(G)$ (i.e., $m$ -times continuously differentiable functions), then $A$ is a removable set for $P(x, D)$ relative to the class $L_0^\Phi(G)$ , the latter being the closed separable subspace generated by $C_0^\infty(G)$ in $L^\Phi(G)$ .

Remark. This result extends the classical case that $\Phi(t) = |t|^p$ , $1 < p < \infty$ , since $h(t) = c|t|^{n - \frac{mp}{p - 1}}$ for $1 \leq m \leq n - 1$ , $n \geq 2$ and $n - \frac{mp}{p - 1} > 0$ , is

considered with $P(x, D)$ as a Laplacian. But it can be more general than the power function since for instance $\Phi(t)$ defined by

Since both $P(x, D)$ and $\Phi$ can be more general, the result applies to many more situations than the Lebesgue theory. We shall sketch the details of proof extending and using some results from Harvey and Polking [1].

Proof. We first consider the case that $H_{h}(A) < \infty$ and later, using the regularity of the metric measure $H_{h}(\cdot)$ , extend the result to the general case, as given in the theorem. Then we approximate $H_{h}(A)$ with values of $H_{h}(\cdot)$ on dyadic cubes, following standard procedures in measure theory. Note that a compact set need not have finite $H_{h}(\cdot)$ measure (Cantor set for $H_{\alpha}(\cdot)$ being an example) and a set $C$ with $H_{\alpha}(C) < \infty$ may not be precompact, so we need care in the desired approximations. The argument is presented in steps.

(I) Under the hypothesis that $H_{h}(A) < \infty$ , $A$ is closed and $P(x, D)f = 0$ weakly on $G \setminus A$ , relative to $f \in L_0^\Phi(G)$ , we assert that $P(x, D)f = 0$ on $G$ itself weakly. For the following argument one needs a result on the "partition of identity" subordinate to a covering, in a special form given by Harvey and Polking ([1], Lemma 3.1) which is needed for our work. This is stated for the space filling collection of closed dyadic nonoverlapping cubes $Q_j$ (i.e., of disjoint interiors) of length $2^{-k}$ with vertices having coordinates of the form $p2^{-k}$ , $p$ being an integer. These are used in approximating an $\alpha$ -dimensional Hausdorff measure $H_\alpha$ of a set $A \subset R^n$ , by $L_\alpha(A) = \lim_{\varepsilon \searrow 0} L_\alpha^\varepsilon(A)$ where

$$
L _ {\alpha} ^ {\varepsilon} (A) = \inf  \left\{\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} s _ {j} ^ {\alpha}: A \subset \cup_ {j = 1} ^ {\infty} Q _ {j}, s _ {j} \leq \varepsilon \right\},
$$
with $s_j$ denoting the (side) length of the cube $Q_j$ . Then one can verify that there are constants $c_\alpha, C_\alpha > 0$ such that

$$
c _ {\alpha} L _ {\alpha} (A) \leq H _ {h} (A) \leq C _ {\alpha} L _ {\alpha} (A), A \subset \mathbb {R} ^ {n}.
$$

(IV) To complete the argument, we have to remove the restriction that $H_{h}(A) < \infty$ . Thus let $P(x,D)f = 0$ weakly on $G \setminus A$ , $f \in L_0^\Phi (G)$ . Consider $C_0 = \{y \in G : P(x,D)f = 0 \text{ weakly on } B(y,\rho) \text{ for some } \rho > 0\}$ where $B(y,\rho)$ is again a ball centered at $y$ and radius $\rho$ . Then the set $C_0(\subset G)$ is open and so $F = G \setminus C_0$ is (relatively) closed. We now assert that $F = \emptyset$ so that $G = C_0$ obtains. Our hypothesis implies that $G \setminus A = C_0$ , so $F \subset A$ . Hence by $\sigma$ -compactness of $A$ , we have

Now if $F$ is nonempty, then by the classical Baire category theorem (cf., Sims [1], p. 126), it is nonmeagre; and hence there exist some $x^{0}$ and $r_0 > 0$ such that $x^{0} \in F$ , $B(x^{0}, 2r_{0}) \subset G$ and for some integer $j$ , $F \cap A_{j}$ is dense in $B(x^{0}, r_{0}) \cap F$ . Thus $F \cap A_{j}$ being a closed subset of $G$ , from the last property above, we have that $H_{h}(B(x^{0}, r_{0}) \cap F) < \infty$ . So by the special case proved above, $P(x, D)f = 0$ weakly in $B(x^{0}, r_{0}) \backslash B(x^{0}, r_{0}) \cap F$ and since $B(x^{0}, r_{0}) \cap F$ is relatively closed, $P(x, D)f = 0$ weakly on $B(x^{0}, r_{0})$ . By definition, then $x_{0} \in C_{0}$ and so $x_{0} \in F$ . But $C_{0} \cap F = \emptyset$ and we have a contradiction. Thus $F = \emptyset$ must be true, and consequently the original assertion must hold.

Although the basic properties of Orlicz spaces in the form of $h(\cdot)$ and Hausdorff measure are important at crucial points, the small details are numerous, and the validity depends on the careful verification of all these subresults. The special property, given as a lemma in the proof, is also essential.

The strong type inequality depends on a different line of attack, and so we refer the details in the original paper of Cianchi [2]. Here are some comments. Recall that in obtaining an extension of the Hausdorff-Young theorem for Orlicz spaces, in Section 6.4, we noted that the classical method of the $L^p$ -spaces, which conveniently uses the Risez convexity theorem, has to be significantly changed since interpolation between Lebesgue spaces will not take us outside the $L^p$ -structure. No (non $L^p$ ) intermediate Orlicz spaces are covered. On the other hand, if we have the inequalities on (sub)linear operators between Orlicz spaces, then our account in Chapter 6 of the companion volume (cf. Rao and Ren [1], Secs. 6.3 and 6.4) applies to a wide class of intermediate (Orlicz) spaces. The problems in Section 6.4 (of the present book) showed that one has to obtain a priori strong type inequalities between Orlicz spaces themselves, involving hard work. Thus the proof of Part (ii) proceeds through such interpolation theorems. The Sobolev embedding is one such key example. Others, with interpolation ideas using the $L^p$ -theory, will now be extended which complements the general work of the previous volume; but now it is somewhat tailored to the PDE analysis.

To begin with, we observe that the H-L maximal operator $M$ is of weak (1,1) type as well as strong $(\infty, \infty)$ type. The latter means that $M: L^{\infty}(R^n) \to L^{\infty}(R^n)$ is bounded, and in fact $\|Mf\|_{\infty} \leq \|f\|_{\infty}$ . Also $M$ is sublinear. The methods we use here work for quasilinear operators as well. So let us start with the following simple case, which is an extension of the operator $M$ to Orlicz spaces. It is due to Gallardo [1].

Reexamining the strong $(\Phi, \Phi)$ -type inequality, which is based on the weak $(1,1)$ and strong $(\infty, \infty)$ inequalities, one can ask whether similar results are obtainable if we have $(p, p)$ and $(\infty, \infty)$ instead. More generally, can we consider weak type $(p, q)$ and $(r, s)$ and find intermediate Orlicz space bounds for $(\Phi_1, \Phi_2)$ ? There are considerable difficulties here. We present a couple of results on weak $(p, p)$ and $(\infty, \infty)$ types for $1 \leq p < \infty$ , or $(1, p')$ and $(p, \infty)$ -types for $1 < p \leq \infty$ with $p' = \frac{p}{p - 1}$ , based on the work of Cianchi's ([1]-[3]). It will be seen that the (nearly optimal) methods are somewhat different from those in the preceding two results, but now they can be given more generally for quasilinear operators. Their specialization for H-L operators is possible, perhaps indicating the necessity of the conditions used, but will not be considered here. We also sketch an application via Theorem 1, to a Dirichlet problem, on the key role played by these inequalities.

It is observed that there is no general Riesz-Thorin type interpolation applicable to large classes of Young functions, than what we have given in our earlier monograph.

We next turn to another type of application for composition and Nemitsky (nonlinear) operators in the final section of this chapter.
9.4 Composition and Nemitsky operators in Orlicz spaces

So far we have considered quasi (and semi)-linear operators on Orlicz spaces, but there are important applications in which more general classes of nonlinear operators appear. Consequently we discuss briefly in this section two classes of nonlinear mappings namely composition (also called substitution) and more inclusively Nemitsky (or superposition) operators, introduced as follows.

Definition 1. Let $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a domain (i.e., a nonempty open set), and $F: G \times \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}$ be a mapping such that $F(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a Carathéodory function in the sense that (a) for each $y \in \mathbb{R}^m$ , $F(\cdot, y)$ is $\mu$ -measurable on $G$ ( $\mu = \text{Lebesgue measure}$ ), and (b) $F(x, \cdot)$ is continuous for a.e. $(x)$ . Then (I) the mapping

is called a Nemitsky operator, and (II) if $m = 1$ , $F$ is independent of $G$ (i.e., $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ ), and is continuous, then it is termed a composition operator, so that $T_F: f \mapsto F(f)$ , is well-defined for $f$ in a suitable function space.

Although the concepts appear simple, they include vast areas of analysis. We consider special classes of these operators to illustrate their wide applicability. Let us first discuss simple examples of (linear) compositions induced by measurable transformations on (general) measure spaces. Thus let $(\Omega, \Sigma,

Such induced operators are a subclass of linear operators on Banach function spaces, and a great deal of specialized (and sharper) results can be established by considering different mappings $S$ and triples $(\Omega, \Sigma, \nu)$ . If $\Omega \subset \mathcal{C}$ and $L^{\Phi}(\Omega)$ is replaced by analytic function classes, a detailed analysis of compositions is given in the recent book by Cowen and MacCluer [1], and other types are detailed in Singh and Manhas [1].

Here is an example of a nonlinear composition, leading to a different type of analysis, where we consider Sobolev spaces for treatment. When the operator is nonlinear, its boundedness and continuity are distinct, and this already shows a need for additional work on the problem. The embedding results of the preceding sections are a certain special aspect of compositions. We now elaborate the general problem. The following result, due to Bourdaud [1], explains the issues.

Theorem 4. Let $W^{m,p}(\mathbb{R})$ , $m \geq 2$ , be a Sobolev space that can be embedded continuously into $L^{\infty}(\mathbb{R})$ . Let $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a continuous mapping with $F(0) = 0$ . Consider the nonlinear composition operator $T_F$ on $W^{m,p}(\mathbb{R})$ into $\mathcal{M}(\mathbb{R})$ , the space of real $\mu$ -measurable functions, where $\mu$ is the Lebesgue measure. Then $T_F(W^{m,p}(\mathbb{R})) \subset W^{m,p}(\mathbb{R})$ and the inclusion is a continuous embedding iff $F \in W_{loc}^{m,p}(\mathbb{R})$ , i.e., $F$ is in this space on every compact subset of $\mathbb{R}$ and $T_F$ is thus a "differentiable composition operator".

The details of proof are many, and the reader is referred to the original paper, or alternatively to the recent book by Runst and Sickel [1], p.268. The result shows real differences between the induced linear and the nonlinear composition operators generated by functions, such as $F$ . An extension to Orlicz-Sobolev spaces $W^{m,\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is possible. A more inclusive case in the context of Nemitsky operators will be discussed below.

Recall that, in Definition 1, there is a function $F: G \times \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}$ generating the Nemesky operator $T_F$ on various spaces of functions. This is a Carathéodory function which, for achieving some generality and for applications in PDE, is usually assumed to be: $F(\cdot, t)$ is (Lebesgue) $\mu$ -measurable on $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ for $t = (t_1, \dots, t_m) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ , and $F(x, t_1, \dots, t_m)$ is separately continuous in $t_k$ , $k = 1, \dots, m$ for each $x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ . In Theorem 6 below, we consider

where $f = (f_{1}, f_{2}, \dots, f_{m}) \in \times_{k=1}^{m} W^{1,M_{k}}(G)$ . If $m = 1$ , $f(= f_{1}) \in W^{k,p}(G)$ , one should define the $k^{th}$ derivative $\frac{\partial^k f}{\partial x_i^k}$ of $f$ in some weak sense in each
9.4 Composition and Nemitsky operators in Orlicz spaces

coordinate $x_{i}$ , and this presents some complications because there will be implicit differentiation together with chain rules in this context. Thus a reasonable assumption is to consider $f$ to be locally, (meaning on compact sets) absolutely continuous in each coordinate, i.e., on almost every line $L$ parallel to a coordinate axis, $f$ is assumed to be locally absolutely continuous on $L \cap G$ , as well as the function $F(\cdot, t)$ . But then the derivatives belong to equivalent classes and for further work (i.e., to iterate the procedure for higher derivatives) it is necessary to select members from these equivalence classes. Hence some type of a "lifting operation" is needed, and such an operation is only possible if these classes are essentially bounded (cf., e.g., Rao [20], Chapter 8 on the lifting map). Several additional conditions are needed to simplify the problem. We discuss it here first for $k = 1$ . Thus if $A(G)$ denotes the class of functions locally absolutely continuous on $L \cap G$ for almost every line $L$ parallel to the coordinate axis, let $A'(G)$ be the class of functions $\tilde{f}$ such that $\partial_{x_i} \tilde{f}$ exists for each $i$ , and $f$ coincides a.e. with an element $\tilde{f} \in A(G)$ . Hence each equivalence class of the Lebesgue differential $\partial_{x_i} f$ of $f \in A(G)$ , agrees a.e. with $\partial_{x_i} \tilde{f}$ of $\tilde{f} \in A'(G)$ . To proceed further, one restricts $G$ to have a general cone property (cf. Thm. 1.1) in that for each $\varepsilon > 0$ , $G = \cup_{j=1}^{m} G_j$ and $G_j = \cup_{x \in B_j} (x + Q_j)$ , $\text{diam}(B_j) < \varepsilon$ with $Q_j$ as a parallelepiped with a vertex at 0 ( $B_j \subset \overline{G}_j$ ). Thus $G$ is a "good" domain.

Under these conditions a classical theorem of E. Gagliardo has been suitably modified and then a fundamental embedding theorem with Nemitsky operators on Sobolev spaces $W^{1,p}(G) \to W^{1,p}(G)$ has been established by Marcus and Mizel [1]. It is extended to Orlicz spaces by Grahame Hardy [2] which we now present as follows.

Proposition 5. Let $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a bounded domain with the (above noted) cone property, and $\Phi$ an $N$ -function. Suppose $f \in A'(G)$ and its derivative $\partial_{x_i}f \in L^\Phi (G), i = 1,2,\dots ,n$ . Then $f \in L^{\Phi}(G)$ itself.
We thus present an Orlicz-Sobolev space version of a result on Nemitsky operators, where the various conditions are abstracted from the Lebesgue case (i.e., of Sobolev spaces).

Theorem 6.(G. Hardy [2]) Let $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a bounded domain having the cone property, and $F: G \times \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}$ be a locally absolutely continuous Carathéodory function. Suppose $\Phi, M_k$ and $Q_k, k = 1, 2, \dots, m$ are $N$ -functions satisfying the following sets of conditions [recall that the (generalized) Sobolev conjugate function $M_k^*$ of $M_k$ is well defined and is again an $N$ -function, as seen in Proposition 1.5]:

(III) there exist nonnegative functions $a, b, a_k, b_{kj}$ such that

$$
\left| \partial_ {x _ {i}} F (x, t) \right| \leq a (x) + b (t) \quad \text {a . e .} \quad \text {o n} \quad G, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots , n

and

$$
