for almost all $x$ and all $t$ , $(x, t) \in G \times \mathbb{R}^m$ , where $a \in L^\Phi(G)$ , $b: \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}^+$ and $b_{kj}: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^+$ are continuous and Borel functions respectively; $a_k \in L^{Q_k}(G)$ , $b_{kk}$ are continuous, and finally define the composition operators $T_b: L^{M_1^*}(G) \times \dots \times L^{M_m^*}(G) \to L^\Phi(G)$ and similarly $T_{b_{kj}}: L^{M_j^*}(G) \to L^{Q_k}(G)$ , $1 \leq k, j \leq m$ .

Then the Nemitsky operator $T_{F}$ induced by $F$ , maps $\times_{k=1}^{m} W^{1, M_{k}}(G)$ into $W^{1, \Phi}(G)$ , and is bounded as well as weakly continuous, i.e., $(f_{1}^{r}, f_{2}^{r}, \dots, f_{m}^{r}) \to (f_{1}, f_{2}, \dots, f_{m})$ as $r \to \infty$ in norm implies $F(\cdot, f_{1}^{r}, \dots, f_{m}^{r}) \to F(\cdot, f_{1}, \dots, f_{m})$ weakly in $W^{1, \Phi}(G)$ . [This continuity is stronger than weak continuity, and is also termed in the literature, "demi-continuity". Here $T_{F}$ is defined on the tensor product space $\times_{k=1}^{m} W^{1, M_{k}}(G)$ equipped with the product norm, or equivalently the maximum of the individual norms as in Section I.4.] Further if we enlarge the range space of $T_{F}$ to $L^{\Phi}(G)$ , from the subspace $W^{1, \Phi}(G)$ , then $T_{F}$ is actually continuous relative to the norms in both spaces.

The details of proof of this result are many and have to be studied first in the Lebesgue-Sobolev case which is due to Marcus and Mizel [1]. Then the above version to Orlicz-Sobolev spaces is extended by G. Hardy [2]. Here we present the statement to show how several specialized conditions have to be imposed in order to cope up with different types of nonlinearities. However, the following examples show that Theorem 6 includes a substantial part (not all) of the Marcus and Mizel theorem, and also contains others that can not be obtained through the corresponding Lebesgue-Sobolev results.

Example 7. Let $n \geq 2$ , $1 < p < q_k < n$ and $q_k' = \frac{pq_k}{q_k - p}$ (i.e., $\frac{1}{q_k} + \frac{1}{q_k'} = \frac{1}{p}$ ). If $\Phi(u) = |u|^p$ and $\Phi_k(u) = \frac{p}{q_k} |u|^{\frac{q_k}{p}}$ , $\Psi_k(v) = \frac{p}{q_k'} |v|^{\frac{q_k'}{p}}$ , then $\Phi \in \triangle_2 \cap \nabla_2$ , $\Phi_k \in \triangle_2$ . \limsup_{v\to \infty}\frac{\Phi_{*}^{-1}(v)}{v[\Phi_{*}^{-1}(v)]^{\prime}}\leq \beta^{*},\quad \text{or}\quad \limsup_{u\to \infty}\frac{u\Phi_{*}^{\prime}(u)}{\Phi_{*}(u)}\leq \beta^{*},
$$
by letting $v = \Phi_{*}(u)$ . This gives (6).

Remark 9. If $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ and $1 < A_{\Phi} \leq B_{\Phi} < n$ , where $A_{\Phi} = \lim \inf_{u \to \infty} \frac{u\phi(u)}{\Phi(u)}$ (cf. Section I.1), then its Sobolev conjugate $N$ -function $\Phi_* \in \triangle_2(\infty) \cap \nabla_2(\infty)$ and

where $A_{\Phi_*} = \lim_{u \to \infty} \inf_{\Phi(u)} \frac{u \Phi'_*(u)}{\Phi(u)}$ . In particular, if $A_{\Phi} = B_{\Phi} = C_{\Phi}$ (say), where $C_{\Phi} = \lim_{u \to \infty} \frac{u \phi(u)}{\Phi(u)}$ , $1 < C_{\Phi} < n$ , then $A_{\Phi_*} = B_{\Phi_*} = C_{\Phi_*}$ where
For example, if $\Phi(u) = [(1 + |u|)\log(1 + |u|) - |u|]^{p}$ , $1 < p < n$ , then $C_{\Phi} = p$ , and its Sobolev conjugate $N$ -function $\Phi_*$ has quantitative index
9.4 Composition and Nemitsky operators in Orlicz spaces

$C_{\Phi_*} = C_{\Phi}^* (= \frac{np}{n - p})$ , the Sobolev conjugate number of $p$ . Lemma 8 and the remark show that for a bounded domain $G \subset R^n$ , if $L^{\Phi}(G)$ ( $W^{k,\Phi}(G)$ ) is separable (reflexive), then $L^{\Phi_*(G)}$ ( $W^{k,\Phi_*(G)}$ ) is also separable (reflexive).

Example 10. Let $n \geq 2$ , $1 < p < n$ , $\Phi(u) = |u|^p$ , $\Phi_k(u) = (1 + |u|) \log(1 + |u|) - |u|$ and $\Psi_k(v) = e^{|v|} - |v| - 1$ . Then $M_k = \Phi \circ \Phi_k \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ since $\lim_{u \to \infty} \frac{uM_k'(u)}{M_k(u)} = p$ . By Lemma 8, $M_k^* \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ . Thus the conditions of Theorem 6 are satisfied. But $L^{M_k}(G)$ is not a Lebesgue space and $W^{1,M_k}(G)$ is not a Sobolev space. However Theorem 6 covers these spaces that are not included in the classical work.

Thus far we considered only the first order Orlicz-Sobolev spaces, and there is naturally a $k^{th}$ order version. It involves, as may be anticipated from Theorem 6, $k^{th}$ order Sobolev conjugate $N$ -functions, and we shall briefly discuss it for completeness.

Recall that for an $N$ -function $\Phi$ and integer $n \geq 2$ , the Sobolev conjugate $\Phi_*$ is the inverse of $\Phi_*^{-1}$ given by (7), and $\Phi_*$ is an $N$ -function if $\Phi_*^{-1}(1) < \infty$ but $\Phi_*^{-1}(\infty) = \infty$ (cf. Proposition 1.5). If $\Phi_1^* = \Phi_*$ and inductively $\Phi_k^*$ is defined as in Section 1, all of which depend on $n$ , then there exists the smallest $J_0 \geq 1$ such that $\Phi_{J_0 + 1}^*$ is not an $N$ -function ( $1 \leq J_0 \leq n$ ), since $\Phi_{J_0 + 1}^*(\infty) < \infty$ . Then a Nemitsky operator in $W^{k,\Phi}(G)$ can again be considered, and in fact the following result for $\mathbb{R}^m$ , $m = 1$ , is obtained by J. Appell and G. Hardy [1].

Theorem 11. Let $G \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a bounded domain with the cone property. Let $\Phi$ be an $N$ -function, $\Phi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ and $F: G \times \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a $C^k$ -function such that $k > J_0 + 1$ where $J_0$ is the exponent of the maximal Sobolev conjugate given above. Then the Nemitsky operator $T_F$ is defined on $W^{k,\Phi}(G)$ into itself, and moreover is bounded and continuous (in their norm topologies).

We refer the reader to the original paper for a proof which uses some properties of a class of spaces, called "multiplicator" Orlicz spaces of independent interest. Briefly, if $\Phi_1, \Phi_2$ are $N$ -functions and $\Phi_1 \prec \Phi_2$ for large values, or that $\Phi_1 \prec \Phi_2$ (i.e., $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{\Phi_2^{-1}(t)}{\Phi_1^{-1}(t)} = 0$ ) we define $(\Phi_1: \Phi_2)$ by the relation

and if $\lim_{u\to \infty}\sup_{\Phi_2(u)}\frac{\Phi_1(\lambda u)}{\Phi_2(u)} < \infty$ for some $\lambda >0$ , then we set $(\Phi_1:\Phi_2)(u) = 0$ for $|u|\leq 1, = \infty$ for $|u| > 1$ . It is seen that $(\Phi_1:\Phi_2)$ is a Young function. Then one finds $L^{\Phi_1}(G)\bullet L^{\Phi_2}(G) = L^{(\Phi_1:\Phi_2)}(G)$ when the left side space is endowed

with the (norm) functional
If $\Phi_1(u) = |u|^{p_1} / p_1$ , $\Phi_2(u) = |u|^{p_2} / p_2$ , then $\Phi_1 \prec \prec \Phi_2$ iff $p_1 < p_2$ , and hence

$$
\left(\Phi_ {1}: \Phi_ {2}\right) (u) = \frac {\left| u \right| ^ {r}}{r}, \quad \frac {1}{r} = \frac {1}{p _ {1}} - \frac {1}{p _ {2}}.
$$

These spaces are of interest in the work on (nonlinear) integral operators, in addition to Nemitsky (or superposition) operators. For an extended discussion of this topic we refer the reader to the work by Appell and Zabrejko ([1], p.62) where $L^{\Phi_1} \bullet L^{\Phi_2}$ is denoted by $L^{\Phi_1} / L^{\Phi_2}$ which is not a quotient.

To note the difference between the linear integral operators and of the nonlinear kind, we also include a brief discussion, arising from a key application. A classical problem raised by Gelfand and Vilenkin ([1], p.275) can be stated as follows.

Let $C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ be the Schwartz space of infinitely differentiable functions on $\mathbb{R}$ with compact supports. If $f, f_n \in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ , then $f_n \to f \in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ provided $\{f_n, n \geq 1, f\}$ all vanish outside a compact set and $f_n^{(k)}(x) \to f^{(k)}(x)$ uniformly as $n \to \infty$ , for $k \geq 0$ . This defines a topology under which $C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ becomes a complete locally convex linear topological space. Let $M: C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R}) \to L^p(P)$ (or $L^\Phi(P)$ ) on a probability space $(\Omega, \Sigma, P)$ , be a continuous linear map, i.e., $f_n \to f$ in $C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ implies $M(f_n) \to M(f)$ in $L^p(P)$ , or only in probability. Such an $M(\cdot)$ is called a generalized random functional. It is said to have independent values, if $M(f_1)$ and $M(f_2)$ are independent random variables whenever $f_1 \cdot f_2 = 0$ , i.e., $f_1, f_2$ have disjoint supports. The structure of $M$ can be studied by considering its Fourier transform

$$
L (f) = \int_ {\Omega} e ^ {i M (f)} d P.
$$

The independence hypothesis implies immediately that for $f_1 \cdot f_2 = 0$ , $L(af_1 + bf_2) = L(af_1)L(bf_2)$ for all $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ , $f_1, f_2 \in C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ . Such an $L(\cdot): C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R}) \to \mathbb{C}$ is termed a local functional by Gelfand and Vilenkin who raised the problem of their characterization. For instance $L(f) = e^{\tilde{M}(f)}$ where $\tilde{M}(\cdot)$ is a superposition functional defined by

$$
\tilde {M} (f) = \int_ {\mathbb {R}} F (x, f (x), f ^ {\prime} (x), \dots , f ^ {(n)} (x)) d x, f \in C _ {c} ^ {\infty} (\mathbb {R})
$$
is of interest here and $L = e^{T_F}$ . If $C_c^\infty(\mathbb{R})$ is replaced by $C_c^m(\mathbb{R})$ , or $C_c(G)$ , where $G$ is a locally compact group (or by an Orlicz space), each of the
9.4 Composition and Nemitsky operators in Orlicz spaces

characterizations is nontrivial. For the case that $G$ is a locally compact space and $\tilde{M}: C_c(G) \to \mathbb{R}$ , which is additive on functions of disjoint supports, a characterization is given in the book (Rao [20], p.517 ff). If $\tilde{M}$ is defined on Lebesgue spaces and having the same property, then Marcus and Mizel [2] have characterized $\tilde{M}$ . It will be of interest to extend these results to $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ also, based on the above ideas. There are many other possibilities and several different spaces to consider. We have to leave the subject at this point.

Finally it is of interest to remark that the study of Nemitsky operators on Orlicz-Sobolev spaces of infinite order is also useful. There are some new problems here since the generalized Sobolev conjugates are not available any more, and projective and injective limits of spaces play key roles, as noted in Dubinskij [1]. This is largely an unexplored area.

Bibliographical notes. We have given most of the references in the text, and it suffices to make some brief remarks here. Section 1 presents a broad framework for the PDE analysis in the context of Orlicz spaces exhibiting a flexibility afforded by it in applications. The basic motivation for the existence and uniqueness in the context of Dirichlet's problem is followed, adopting a readable presentation of Mikhailov [1] which shows a clear role of functional analysis in this subject leading to a use of weighted Orlicz-Sobolev spaces of finite order. The case of (generalized or higher order) Sobolev conjugate $N$ -functions and the corresponding embedding results are based on Donaldson and Trudinger [1] and its extension by Adams [1], the latter (generalized) result (Theorem 1.7) appears perhaps for the first time only in this book. The results on infinite order Orlicz-Sobolev spaces are treated briefly, partly for space reasons (although they have an interesting role in certain stochastic differential equations) and partly because the subject is not yet well-developed. Our discussion may help motivate researchers to explore this area further.

Section 2 discuss some key results on removable singularities in the generality introduced by Bochner and further developed by Harvey and Polking [1] in the Lebesgue context. The main result (Theorem 2.2) is due to Shapiro [1] in its extension to Orlicz spaces. This indicates an interesting widening of the area of applications. Similarly the weak comparison principle plays a key role for the uniqueness results in PDE, and we briefly included a result (Theorem 2.3) which also is due to Shapiro [2] to emphasize this fact.

We then consider several results based on strong and weak type inequalities, and consequent interpolation analysis. They complement and sharpen similar ones given in the companion volume (cf. Rao and Ren [1], Chapters V and VI). Many of these results involve intricate computations using various
IX. Nonlinear PDEs and Orlicz Spaces

classifications and order relations of Young functions. This work is recent and Theorem 1.9 as well as most of Section 3 is taken from the papers of Cianchi's ([1]-[3]). Theorems 3.2 and 3.3 are due to Gallardo [1], which show the optimality of these conditions. We thank Dr. Cianchi for sending us her interesting work prior to publication.

In the final Section 4 of the chapter we consider composition and (superposition or) Nemitsky operators. The former has both linear and nonlinear components. The linear part specialized the structural analysis of operators using methods of linear functional analysis, which are better understood. We include a brief account of these, with Propositions 4.2 and 4.3 from Kumar [1], and indicating other types of compositions on analytic or other function spaces. We then turn to nonlinear analysis in the rest of the section. The material on nonlinear superposition or Nemitsky operators in the Lebesgue case is studied in several papers by Marcus and Mizel of which we refer to some basic results in [1]. They also treat local functionals on various spaces. Some of their results have been extended by G. Hardy [2] in several parts. The Gelfand-Vilenkin problem has deep probabilistic significance, and some aspects of it have been considered in Rao [15] on the continuous function spaces (giving also earlier references to some other works), and on Lebesgue spaces by Mizel [1]. The methods in various spaces differ considerably showing the intricate nature of the subject. Applications to nonlinear analysis and PDE of Nemitsky operators have been summarized in Runst and Sickel [1], and a general survey of these operators on different spaces is in Appell and Zabrejko [1]. We thus conclude the work at this point, but also outlined numerous problems of interest for future investigations.
9.4 Composition and Nemitsky operators in Orlicz spaces
Chapter X
Miscellaneous Applications

This final chapter is devoted to various applications that are raised by the analysis in some of the preceding works but could not be included there. First we treat the (new) class of Beurling-Orlicz algebras to the extent known. The topics considered in the other sections are as follows. Studying another geometric invariant, called the Riesz angle of Banach lattices, the second section contains an analysis as specialized and sharpened to Orlicz spaces. Section 3 complements the earlier work on embedding properties of Orlicz spaces restricted to discrete measure, hence sequence spaces. The material here is due to Ren and it sharpens aspects of Chapter I. Section 4 is devoted to results on uniform Gâteaux differentiability of norms when the underlying measure space is restricted to be just diffuse or discrete. The final section is devoted to generalized Orlicz spaces, when the measure is finite, but the $\phi$ -function is allowed to be concave. Most of the work here is recent and complements that of the earlier general study, containing refinements.
10.1 Beurling-Orlicz algebras

It was shown in Section I.3, in particular in Propositions I.3.13 and 14, that $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is closed under convolution if $\Phi$ is a Young function such that $\Phi'(0) > 0$ . Hence $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ is an algebra under convolution. It is possible that $\Phi \in \triangle_2$ but some times $\Phi \notin \triangle_2$ . In these studies $\mu$ is the Lebesgue measure. However, there are other convolution algebras with more general $\mu$ that is absolutely continuous relative to Lebesgue measure. Such were introduced by Beurling in the context of Lebesgue spaces and we present their extensions to Orlicz spaces, motivated by the preceding $L^{\Phi}(\mathbb{R})$ algebras. The basic idea is to consider positive upper semicontinuous submultiplicative weight functions as densities of the new measures and then study the structure of the resulting classes, to be called Beurling-Orlicz algebras. More precisely,

let $w: \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{+}$ be a mapping, termed a weight function, satisfying: (i) $1 \leq w(x) < \infty, x \in \mathbb{R}$ , (ii) $w(x + y) \leq w(x)w(y), x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ , and (iii) $w(\cdot)$ is Borel measurable. For instance, $w(x) = (1 + |x|)^{\alpha}, \alpha \geq 0$ satisfies these conditions. Since $\log w$ is subadditive, it (and hence $w$ ) is bounded on compact intervals, although not necessarily continuous, but can and will be assumed upper semicontinuous by replacing it with $\tilde{w}(x) = \lim_{y \to 0} \sup w(x + y)$ , if necessary. [For a discussion of subadditive Borel functions, one may consult Hille and Phillips [1], Chapter VII.]
We first present an easy extension of Proposition I.3.14 as:

If $\tilde{\mu}: A \longmapsto \int_{A} w \, d\mu$ , $A \subset \mathbb{R}$ is a Borel set and $\mu$ the Lebesgue measure, then $L^{\Phi}(\tilde{\mu})$ on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \tilde{\mu})$ , is a convolution algebra in that, '\* denoting convolution,

Let $0 \neq g \in L^{\Phi}(\tilde{\mu})$ and $K = \|g\|_{\Phi}$ , so that $\int_{\mathbb{R}} \Phi\left(\frac{g}{K}\right) d\tilde{\mu} \leq 1$ . Hence we get from the inequality $|x| \Phi'(0) \leq \Phi(x)$ and the $L^{1}(\tilde{\mu})$ -norm,

10.1 Beurling-Orlicz algebras

The convolution algebras $L^1(\tilde{\mu})$ were introduced by Beurling [1], and are often called Beurling algebras for the weights $w$ (cf. Reiter [1], p.14). The corresponding $L^\Phi(\tilde{\mu})$ can (and will) be termed Beurling-Orlicz algebras. These objects admit a further interesting and deep extensions as noted by Beurling himself in 1955, but published only in 1964 in [2], for the Lebesgue spaces. In the latter paper, he introduced certain subspaces of $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ of the following type which we present with an Orlicz space extension, again with weights.

Let $\Phi$ be a Young function satisfying a $\triangle'$ condition globally. Suppose its complementary function $\Psi$ is continuous and has the property $\Psi(xy) \leq b_y\Psi(x), x > 0$ where $b_y \geq 1$ for each $y > 0$ . This is a form of the $\triangle_2$ -condition. Recall that $\Phi \in \triangle'$ means that $\Phi(xy) \leq C\Phi(x)\Phi(y), x, y > 0$ for some $C > 0$ , and replacing $\Phi$ by $\frac{\tilde{\Phi}}{C}$ , we may (and do) take $C = 1$ for convenience. Let $W$ be a set of weights satisfying $W = \{w > 0 : \int_{\mathbb{R}} w d\mu \leq N(w) < \infty\}$ where $w$ is a measurable (weight) function, and for each $w \in W$ and $\lambda > 0, \lambda w \in W$ . Here $N(\cdot)$ is a norm with the property that $N(w_1 * w_2) \leq N(w_1)N(w_2)$ where '\*' denotes convolution. Suppose moreover that $\{W, N(\cdot)\}$ is complete in the sense that $\{w_n, n \geq 1\} \subset W$ , $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} N(w_n) < \infty$ implies $w = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} w_n \in W$ and $N(w) \leq \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} N(w_n)$ . An example of $N(\cdot)$ is $\| \cdot \|_1$ or $\| \cdot \|_\Phi$ of Proposition 1, where $\Phi$ is suitably normalized which is possible. Next consider a subset $W_0$ of $W$ given by $W_0 = \{w \in W : N(w) = 1\}$ , so that it is the positive spherical shell in $W$ . Suppose that $\Phi(1) = 1$ , for convenience, by a redefinition if necessary. Then we can obtain a modified (or weighted) Hölder inequality as follows:

10.1 Beurling-Orlicz algebras

Discussion 3. Taking $w(\cdot)$ to be also bounded in the above proposition, it is clear that $L_{w}^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is a Banach algebra contained properly in $L^{1}(\mu)$ , the classical convolution algebra. Other subalgebras can be studied. An interesting such class was considered abstractly by Segal as follows. Let $L^{1}(G)$ be the convolution algebra on a locally compact group $G$ with a (left) Haar measure $\mu$ on it. Then a set $S \subset L^{1}(G)$ is called a Segal algebra if there is a norm $\| \cdot \|_{s}$ under which it becomes a Banach algebra, and $\| \cdot \|_{s}$ has the properties: (i) $\| \tau_{a}f\|_{s} = \| f\|_{s}$ where $(\tau_{a}f)(x) = f(ax), a \in G$ , (i.e., $\tau_{a}$ is a translation operator), and (ii) $a \mapsto \| \tau_{a}f\|_{s}$ is continuous in the sense that for each $\varepsilon > 0$ there is a neighborhood $U$ of the identity of $G$ , such that $\| \tau_{a}f - f\|_{s} < \varepsilon$ for all $a \in U$ . Thus with $w(x) = 1$ (or a constant), and $\Phi'(0) > 0$ , then $L^{\Phi}(G) \subset L^{1}(G)$ and is a Banach algebra under convolution of Proposition 1, but the mapping $a \mapsto \| \tau_{a}f\|_{(\Phi)}$ will be continuous iff $\Phi \in \triangle_{2}$ . Thus $L^{\Phi}(\mu)$ is still a convolution (Banach) algebra even if $\Phi \notin \triangle_{2}$ (but $\Phi'(0) > 0$ ), so that there are examples of convolution algebras $L^{\Phi}(G) \subset L^{1}(G)$ which are both Segal as well as non-Segal algebras. It will thus be interesting to extend the analysis of Segal algebras, given by Reiter [2] to Beurling-Orlicz algebras yielding new properties of the latter. This studt is not available at present.
In the rest of the chapter we consider other geometric properties of potential interest in applications complementing the earlier analysis in this book.
10.2 Riesz angles of Orlicz spaces

A new geometric invariant, called the Riesz angle, was introduced by Borwein and Sims [1] for certain Banach lattices, and then established some fixed point properties. This invariant will now be analyzed for (real) Orlicz spaces, which are Banach lattices, if the underlying measures are either purely discrete or diffuse. Let us first state the concept of a Riesz angle.

Example 14. If $1 < p < \infty$ , then $a(l^p) = a(L^p(\Omega)) = 2^{\frac{1}{p}}$ , where $\Omega = [0,1]$ or $\mathbb{R}^+$ . This was established by Borwein and Sims [1], using an inequality due to Bohnenblust [1]. A direct proof is as follows. Let $\Phi_p(u) = |u|^p$ . Then $l^p = l^{(\Phi_p)}$ and $L^p(\Omega) = L^{(\Phi_p)}(\Omega)$ . But $\frac{\Phi_p^{-1}(u)}{\Phi_p^{-1}(2u)} = 2^{-\frac{1}{p}}$ , $0 < u < \infty$ . Hence the result follows from Theorems 2, 11 and 7.

10.3 Embedding theorems for sequence spaces

In our companion volume [1], Section V.3, analysis of weakly compact embedding and sharp conditions for diffuse measures were presented. Here we consider sequence spaces, and the results as well as methods are generally different. The details in this (discrete=counting measure) case have been obtained by Ren, and are presented here for completeness.

10.4 Differentiability properties of Orlicz spaces
In this section, we specialize some abstract Banach space concepts and present necessary and sufficient conditions under which the norm of an Orlicz
X. Miscellaneous Applications
space is uniformly Gâteaux differentiable. These results largely complement those of Section 7.2 of our companion volume [1]. We recall the concept.

Definition 1. Let $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)$ be a Banach space with $S(\mathcal{X})$ denoting its unit sphere, $S(\mathcal{X}) = \{x\in \mathcal{X}:\| x\| = 1\}$ . Then the norm $\| \cdot \|$ is (a) Gâteaux differentiable if for every $x\in S(\mathcal{X})$ , $y\in S(\mathcal{X})$
exists, and is uniformly Gâteaux differentiable if the limit is uniform in $x \in S(\mathcal{X})$ for each fixed $y \in S(\mathcal{X})$ .

(b) Fréchet differentiable if the limit in (1) exists uniformly in $y \in S(\mathcal{X})$ for each fixed $x \in S(\mathcal{X})$ , and is uniformly Fréchet differentiable, if the limit in (1) exists uniformly for the pair $(x, y) \in S(\mathcal{X}) \times S(\mathcal{X})$ .

If the norm of $\mathcal{X}$ is Gâteaux differentiable, we also say for short, as in Day [1], that $\mathcal{X}$ is Gâteaux differentiable and denote $\mathcal{X} \in (G)$ . Similarly, for the other corresponding concepts, we denote $\mathcal{X} \in (UG)$ , $\mathcal{X} \in (F)$ and $\mathcal{X} \in (UF)$ respectively. It is seen that $\mathcal{X} \in (G)$ iff $\mathcal{X} \in (S)$ (smooth), $\mathcal{X} \in (F)$ iff $\mathcal{X} \in (SS)$ (strongly smooth) and that $\mathcal{X} \in (UF)$ iff $\mathcal{X} \in (US)$ (uniformly smooth). It is also clear that $\mathcal{X} \in (UF) \Longrightarrow \mathcal{X} \in (F) \Longrightarrow \mathcal{X} \in (G)$ and $\mathcal{X} \in (UF) \Longrightarrow \mathcal{X} \in (UG) \Longrightarrow \mathcal{X} \in (G)$ , but $\mathcal{X} \in (F) \Longrightarrow \mathcal{X} \in (UG)$ and $\mathcal{X} \in (UG) \Longrightarrow \mathcal{X} \in (F)$ in general.

Before we study $(UG)$ -differentiability of Orlicz spaces first recall a basic result on (G) differentiability of the closed separable subspace $M^{(\Phi)}$ of $L^{(\Phi)}$ endowed with the gauge norm and $M^{\Phi}$ of $L^{\Phi}$ endowed with the Orlicz norm, on a finite measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ , with $\mu$ nonatomic.

Theorem 3. Let $\Phi$ be an $N$ -function and let $L^{(\Phi)}, L^{\Phi}$ be the Orlicz spaces, with the two norms, on a finite nonatomic measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ . To simplify the proofs of the results on $(UG)$ differentiability of the Orlicz spaces, we again need to recall some concepts and results on abstract Banach spaces. [Again we follow Day [1] for the most part.]

Definition 4. (a) We say that a Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is weakly uniformly convex, denoted $\mathcal{X} \in (WUR)$ , if $x_{n}, y_{n} \in S(\mathcal{X})$ and $\|x_{n} + y_{n}\| \to 2$ imply $(x_{n} - y_{n}) \to 0$ weakly. (b) The dual space $\mathcal{X}^{*}$ of $\mathcal{X}$ is said to be weak* uniformly convex, in symbols $\mathcal{X}^{*} \in (W^{*}UR)$ , if $f_{n}, g_{n} \in S(\mathcal{X}^{*})$ and $\|f_{n} + g_{n}\|_{\mathcal{X}^{*}} \to 2$ imply $(f_{n} - g_{n}) \to 0$ weak*, i.e., $\lim_{n \to \infty}(f_{n} - g_{n})(x) = 0$ for each $x \in \mathcal{X}$ .

Lemma 5. (Smulyan [1]) Let $\mathcal{X}$ be a Banach space and $\mathcal{X}^*$ be its dual space. We have (i) $\mathcal{X}^* \in (UG)$ iff $\mathcal{X} \in (WUR)$ ; (ii) $\mathcal{X} \in (UG)$ iff $\mathcal{X}^* \in (W^*UR)$ .

Theorem 6. Let $\Phi(u) = \int_0^{|u|} \phi(t)$ and $\Psi(v) = \int_0^{|v|} \psi(s) ds$ define a complementary pair of $N$ -functions and let $L^\Phi = L^\Phi(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ with $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ being nonatomic, $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ . Then the following assertions are equivalent.

Theorem 7. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a pair of complementary $N$ -functions and let $L^{(\Phi)} = L^{(\Phi)}(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ with $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ being as in Theorem 3. Then the following assertions are equivalent.

Let $u_{1} = 0, v_{1} = 0, u_{n} = 2^{n}, v_{n} = 2^{n}, 0 < \varepsilon < \frac{1}{4}, u_{n}^{\prime} = (1 + \varepsilon)u_{n}, v_{n}^{\prime} = (2^{n} + \frac{1}{2}), n = 2, 3, \dots$ . Join the points $(u_{1}, v_{1})$ to $(u_{2}, v_{2})$ ; $(u_{n}, v_{n})$ to $(u_{n}^{\prime}, v_{n}^{\prime})$ ; and $(u_{n}^{\prime}, v_{n}^{\prime})$ to $(u_{n+1}, v_{n+1})$ by straight line segments. Let this function be $\psi$ . Then

$$
\frac {\psi ((1 + \varepsilon) u _ {n})}{\psi (u _ {n})} = \frac {\psi (u _ {n} ^ {\prime})}{\psi (u _ {n})} = \frac {v _ {n} ^ {\prime}}{v _ {n}} = \frac {2 ^ {n} + \frac {1}{2}}{2 ^ {n}} \rightarrow 1,
$$

as $n \to \infty$ so that $\Psi(v) = \int_0^{|v|} \psi(s) ds$ is not uniformly convex for large $v$ , although $\Psi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ . The inverse $\phi$ of $\psi$ is continuous and satisfies $\phi(2u) \leq 4\phi(u), u > 0$ . Thus $\Phi(u) = \int_0^{|u|} \phi(t) dt$ defines $\Phi$ in $\triangle_2(\infty)$ . By Theorem 7 and Proposition 8, $L^{(\Phi)} \notin (UG)$ and $L^{(\Phi)} \in (F)$ .

Now we turn to the sequence spaces $l^{\Phi}$ (with the Orlicz norm) and $l^{(\Phi)}$ (with the gauge norm), and their closed separable subspaces $m^{\Phi}, m^{(\Phi)}$ . We record the following special result, which can be found in Chen ([1], p.90-100, and Theorem 2.57).
Proposition 12. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a pair of complementary $N$ -functions.
(i) $m^{\Phi}\in (G)$ iff $\Psi$ is strictly convex on $[0,\Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})]$ .
(ii) $l^{\Phi}\in (G)$ iff $\Psi$ is strictly convex on $[0,\Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})]$ and $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0)$
(iii) $l^{\Phi}\in (F)$ iff $\Psi$ is strictly convex on $[0,\Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})]$ and $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0)\cap \nabla_2(0)$
$(i)^{\prime}m^{(\Phi)}\in (G)$ iff $\Phi^{\prime}$ is continuous on $(0,\Phi^{-1}(\tilde{1}))$
$(ii)'$ $l^{(\Phi)} \in (G)$ iff $\Phi'$ is continuous on $(0, \Phi^{-1}(1))$ and $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0)$ .
$(iii)'$ $l^{(\Phi)} \in (F)$ iff $\Phi'$ is continuous on $(0, \Phi^{-1}(1))$ and $\Phi \in \Delta_2(0) \cap \nabla_2(0)$ .
10.4 Differentiability properties of Orlicz spaces
Theorem 13. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a pair of complementary $N$ -functions. Then the following assertions for the sequence spaces are equivalent.

(i) $l^{\Phi} \in (UG)$ ; (ii) $m^{(\Psi)} \in (WUR)$ ; (iii) $l^{(\Psi)} \in (WUR)$ ; (iv) $a) \Psi$ is strictly convex on $[0, \Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})]$ , (b) $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0) \cap \nabla_2(0)$ , and (c) $\Psi$ is strictly convex on $[\Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{2}), \Psi^{-1}(1)]$ or $\Psi$ is uniformly convex for small values.

Let $a_{n} = \Psi^{-1}(1 - k_{n}v_{n})$ and define two sequences $\{f_n\}, \{g_n\}$ in $m^{(\Psi)}$ as

f _ {n} = \left(a _ {n}, \Psi^ {- 1} \left(v _ {n}\right), \dots , \Psi^ {- 1} \left(v _ {n}\right), 0, 0, \dots\right),

with $dimf_{n} = dimg_{n} = 1 + k_{n}$ . Clearly, $\rho_{\Psi}(f_n) = \rho_{\Psi}(g_n) = \Psi (a_n) + k_nv_n = 1$ so that $\| f_n\|_{(\Psi)} = \| g_n\|_{(\Psi)} = 1,n\geq 1$ . By (10) and (11), we have

$$
\rho_ {\Psi} \left(\frac {f _ {n} + g _ {n}}{2 \left(1 - \frac {1}{2}\right)}\right) = k _ {n} \Psi \left(\frac {\Psi^ {- 1} (v _ {n})}{1 - \frac {1}{n}}\right) > 2 k _ {n} v _ {n} > 1,

proving that $2 = \| f_n\|_{(\Psi)} + \| g_n\|_{(\Psi)} \geq \| f_n + g_n\|_{(\Psi)} > 2(1 - \frac{1}{n})$ and $\lim_{n\to \infty}\| f_n + g_n\|_{(\Psi)} = 2$ . On the other hand, since $a_{n} \geq \Psi^{-1}(1 - \frac{3}{4}) = \Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{4}) > 0$ for all $n \geq 1$ , the sequence $(f_n - g_n) = (2a_n,0,0,\dots)$ does not converge weakly to zero, which contradicts the hypothesis. Thus $\Psi \in \triangle_2(0)$ and then $l^{(\Psi)} = m^{(\Psi)}$ , proving (ii) $\Longrightarrow$ (iii).
Theorem 14. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a pair of complementary $N$ -functions. Then the following assertions are equivalent:

Then $\Psi(v) = \int_0^{|v|} \psi(s) ds$ is strictly convex on $[0, \infty)$ since $\psi$ is strictly increasing on $[0, \infty)$ . Let $\Phi$ be the complementary function to $\Psi$ . Then $\Phi'$ is continuous on $(0, \infty)$ . We claim that $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0) \cap \nabla_2(0)$ . If $v \in [2^{-(n+1)}, 2^{-n})$ , then $\frac{\psi(2v)}{\psi(v)} \leq \frac{2(n+1)}{n} \leq 4, n \geq 2$ . Hence we have for $0 \leq v < \frac{1}{2}$

$$
\Psi (2 v) \leq 2 v \psi (2 v) \leq 8 v \psi (v) \leq 6 4 \left[ \frac {v}{2} \psi (\frac {v}{2}) \right] \leq 6 4 \Psi (v),

i.e., $\Psi \in \triangle_2(0)$ so that $\Phi \in \nabla_2(0)$ . If $v_n \in [2^{-(n+1)}, 2^{-n})$ , then $3v_n > 2^{-n}$ and $\frac{\psi(3v_n)}{\psi(v_n)} \to 3$ as $n \to \infty$ . Thus there is an $n_0 \geq 1$ such that $2\psi(v) \leq \psi(3v)$ for $0 \leq v < 2^{-n_0}$ so that $\Phi'(2u) \leq 3\Phi'(u)$ if $0 \leq u < u_0 = \psi(2^{-n_0})$ , which proves that $\Phi \in \triangle_2(0)$ . By Proposition 12 (iii)', $l^{(\Phi)} \in (F)$ . On the other hand, if $v_n = 2^{-(n+1)}$ , then $2^{-(n+1)} < (1 + \frac{1}{2})v_n < 2^{-n}$ and

Remark 18. Example 17 is due to T. F. Wang (communicated to Ren), which is analogous to Example 11 given by Milnes [1]. It implies that there exist $N$ -functions $\Psi \in \nabla_2(0)$ which fail to be uniformly convex for small values, while Example 11 shows that there are $N$ -functions $\Psi \in \nabla_2(\infty)$ that fail to be uniformly convex for large values.

For a measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ with $\mu(\Omega) = \infty$ and $\mu$ nonatomic, we may consider $\Omega = [0, \infty) = \mathbb{R}^{+}$ with the Lebesgue measure and record a couple of results of the same type for reference as follows.
Theorem 19. For an $N$ -function $\Phi$ , the following are equivalent:
(i) $L^{\Phi}[0,\infty)\in (F)$ ; (ii) $L^{(\Phi)}[0,\infty)\in (F)$ ; (iii) $L^{\Phi}[0,\infty)\in (UG)$ ; (iv) $\Phi'$ is continuous on $(0,\infty)$ and $\Phi \in \triangle_2\cap \nabla_2$ .
Theorem 20. $L^{(\Phi)}[0,\infty) \in (UG)$ iff $\Psi$ is uniformly convex on $[0,\infty)$ , where $\Psi$ is complementary to $\Phi$ .
X. Miscellaneous Applications

Problem 21. As seen in Remark 15, $l^{\Phi} \in (UG) \Rightarrow l^{\Phi} \in (F)$ but not conversely. A natural question then is the following. Does there exist a class of Banach spaces $\mathcal{X}$ for which $\mathcal{X} \in (F) \Rightarrow \mathcal{X} \in (UG)$ but not necessarily conversely?

We conclude this section with the (UF) differentiability of Orlicz spaces for completeness. The following result is variously proved by Luxemburg [1], Milnes [1], Akimovic [1] and Kaminska [1]. Some of the proofs can be found in Rao and Ren [1], Section 7.2, and in Chen [1]. By $\mathcal{X} \in (UR)$ we note that the Banach space $\mathcal{X}$ is uniformly convex (or uniformly rotund).

Theorem 22. Let $(\Phi, \Psi)$ be a pair of complementary $N$ -functions, and let $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ be a measure space with $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ and $\mu$ nonatomic. Then the following are equivalent:

(i) $L^{\Phi} \in (UF)$ ; (ii) $L^{(\Phi)} \in (UF)$ ; (iii) $L^{(\Psi)} \in (UR)$ ; (iv) $L^{\Psi} \in (UR)$ ; (v) $\Psi \in \triangle_2(\infty)$ , $\Psi$ is strictly convex on $[0, \infty)$ and uniformly convex for large values. [If $\mu(\Omega) = \infty$ , (v) is replaced by $(v)'$ : $\Psi \in \triangle_2$ and $\Psi$ is uniformly convex on $[0, \infty)$ .]
Theorem 23. (Kaminska [1]) For an $N$ -pair $(\Phi, \Psi)$ , the following are equivalent:

(i) $l^{\Phi} \in (UF)$ ; (ii) $l^{(\Psi)} \in (UR)$ ; (iii) $(a)\Psi$ is strictly convex on $[0, \Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})]$ , (b) $\Psi \in \triangle_2(0)$ , and (c) $\Psi$ is uniformly convex for small values. [Note that (iii) (a) with (iii) (c) imply that $\Psi$ is uniformly convex on $[0, \Psi^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})]$ .]

Theorem 24. (Tao [1]) For an $N$ -pair $(\Phi, \Psi)$ , the following are equivalent: $(i) l^{(\Phi)} \in (UF)$ ; $(ii) l^{\Psi} \in (UR)$ ; $(iii) a \Phi'$ is continuous on $(0, \Phi^{-1}(1))$ , $(b) \Psi \in \triangle_2(0)$ , and $(c) \Psi$ is uniformly convex for small values.
10.5 $L^{\Phi^{-1}}$ -spaces and applications

Recall that in Section VI.5, a generalized Orlicz space $L^{\phi}(\Omega)$ on $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$ is defined by a $\phi$ -function $\phi: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^{+}$ , that is increasing, continuous, $\phi(-u) = \phi(u)$ , $\phi(u) = 0$ iff $u = 0$ , and $\lim_{u \to \infty} \phi(u) = +\infty$ . The $\phi$ -functions, include convex and concave functions, as $\phi_p(u) = |u|^p, 0 < p < \infty$ , are examples so that we have just $L^{\infty}(\Omega) \subset L^{\phi}(\Omega) \subset L^{0}(\Omega)$ , since $L^{\infty}(\Omega) \subset L^{p}(\Omega)(0 < p < \infty) \subset L^{0}(\Omega)$ , when $\mu(\Omega) < \infty$ and $L^{0}(\Omega) = \{f: f \text{ is } \mu -$ measurable and is finite a.e. on } \Omega\}. For the classical spaces $L^{p}(\Omega), 0 < p < 1$ , one defines the $F$ -norm by $\|f\|_p = \int_{\Omega} |f|^p d\mu$ , and for $1 < p_1 < p_2 < \infty$ one has
in which all the spaces are defined on the same finite measure space $(\Omega, \Sigma, \mu)$

and $L^{p_i}$ are the usual Lebesgue spaces, $i = 1,2$ . On the other hand, A. C. Zaanen (MR 34(1967), $\# 6434$ ) observed that a convex function $\phi$ need not satisfy the Lesniewicz condition $\int_1^\infty \frac{\phi(u)}{u^2} < \infty$ (see Theorem VI.5.4), since

$$
\frac {\phi (t)}{4 t} = \frac {\phi (t)}{(2 t) ^ {2}} t \leq \int_ {t} ^ {2 t} \frac {\phi (u)}{u ^ {2}} d u \rightarrow 0 a s t \rightarrow \infty ,

i.e., $\lim_{t\to \infty}\frac{\phi(t)}{t} = 0$ , and $L^1 [0,2\pi)\subset L^\phi [0,2\pi)$ . In this section which is in the form of an addendum to Chapter VI, we establish the theory of $L^{\Phi^{-1}}$ -space defined by the inverse $\Phi^{-1}$ of an $N$ -function $\Phi$ , which is adapted from a recent doctoral thesis by J. C. Wang [1]. We present this in 5 subsections, consisting of the basic structure, somewhat different from our volume ([1], pp. 405-411), and application Fourier analysis.

Remark. There are two different definitions for the Fréchet space. The first definition is that an F-space is a topologically complete linear metric space under the metric $d(f, g) = \| f - g \|$ with $\| \cdot \|$ being an F-norm, and a Fréchet space is a locally convex F-space. By this definition, $L^p[0,1]$ and $l^p$ with $0 < p < 1$ are F-spaces that are not Fréchet spaces. The second definition is that a Fréchet space is an F-space as above, which is not necessarily locally convex (cf., e.g., Rao and Ren [1], p.401).

Example 22. Let $0 < p < 1$ and $L^p[0,1]$ be endowed with the F-norm $\|f\|_p = \int_0^1 |f|^p d\mu$ , $\mu$ being the Lebesgue measure. Then a set $S \subset L^p[0,1]$ is compact iff (i) $S$ is compact in measure, and (ii) $S$ has equi-absolutely continuous F-norm $\|\cdot\|_p$ . Further, if $0 < p_1 < p_2 < 1$ , $S \subset L^{p_2}[0,1]$ , $S$ is 'compact in $\mu$ -measure' and $\sup_{f \in S} \|f\|_{p_2} \leq C < \infty$ , then $S$ is similarly compact in $L^{p_1}[0,1]$ .
Subsection 4. Local boundedness and $\Phi^{-1}$ -variation

Recall that a set $S$ in an F-space $(\mathcal{X},\| \cdot \|)$ is metrically bounded if $S \subset \{f\in \mathcal{X}:\| f\| \leq r\}$ for some $r > 0$ , and $S$ is topologically bounded if for each neighborhood $U$ of zero there is an $r > 0$ such that $S\subset rU$ ; in other words for any $\alpha_{n}\searrow 0$ and $f_{n}\in S$ one has $\lim_{n\to \infty}\| \alpha_nf_n\| = 0$ . These two concepts need not coincide (cf., Example 23 below). Further, an F-space is locally bounded if it contains a topologically bounded neighborhood of zero.
Example 23. If an $N$ -function $\Phi \notin \triangle_2(\infty)$ , then there is a metrically bounded set $S$ in $L^{\Phi^{-1}}[0,1]$ , which is not topologically bounded.

$$

Proof. Let an $N$ -function be defined by $\Phi_p(u) = |u|^{\frac{1}{p}} \in \nabla_2(\infty)$ , then $L^p[0, 2\pi) = L^{\Phi_p^{-1}}[0, 2\pi)$ and $\|f\|_{(\Phi_p^{-1})} = f_0^{2\pi} |f(x)|^p dx = \|f\|_p$ . The assertions follow from Corollary 31 and Theorem 36. $\square$

$$

$$

We may then call (41) the Lesniewicz condition in the case that $\phi = \Phi^{-1}$ .
2. Since $\Phi^{-1}$ is a special $\phi$ -function, one can also define the "old" F-norm $\| \cdot \|_{\Phi^{-1}}$ as in Section VI.5 by

Our "new" F-norm $\| \cdot \|_{(\Phi^{-1})}$ defined by (2) is simpler than (47). For instance, if $f = a\chi_G, G \subset \Omega, 0 < \mu(G) < \infty$ , then $\| f \|_{(\Phi^{-1})} = \Phi^{-1}(a)\mu(G)$ , but $\| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}}$ is the solution $c$ of the equation $\Phi^{-1}(\frac{a}{c})\mu(G) = c$ . Moreover, we have (i) $\| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}} = 1$ iff $\| f \|_{(\Phi^{-1})} = 1$ , (ii) if $0 < \| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}} < 1$ , then $\| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}}^2 < \| f \|_{(\Phi^{-1})} < \| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}}$ ; and (iii) if $\| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}} > 1$ , then $\| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}} < \| f \|_{(\Phi^{-1})} < \| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}}^2$ .
For, since the $\phi$ -function $\Phi^{-1} \in \triangle_2$ ( $\Phi^{-1}(2u) \leq 2\Phi^{-1}(u), u \geq 0$ ), we have for $0 \neq f \in L^{\Phi^{-1}}(\Omega)$
(cf. Rao and Ren [1], p.402). Thus, (i) follows from (48). If $0 < \| f \|_{\Phi^{-1}} < 1$ , then by (48)

Bibliographical notes. A class of subalgebras of the convolution algebra $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ , relative to certain weights were first introduced by Beurling in the late 1930's for which the classical Fourier analysis results such as the Wiener-Lévy theorem and others were extended. This rich class based on the Lebesgue space $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ were analyzed and termed Beurling algebras by Reiter [1]. In the context of $L^\Phi(\mathbb{R}), \Phi'(0) > 0$ , we find that there is a natural extension to $L_w^\Phi(\mathbb{R})$ , and (appropriately) termed them Beurling-Orlicz algebras. These new (convolution) algebras have a good prospect of analysis, but so far only a rudimentary study is completed. We introduce this class in Section 1 to have it available for future research, especially to extend Beurling's [2] results on new convolution operators for these algebras. This as well as the remaining sections contain various complements and miscellaneous results. Thus Sections 2-5 are devoted to specialized, and sharp analysis of Orlicz spaces when the underlying measure space is either purely discrete or of (finite) diffuse type. They discuss the following classes, the exact references having been included in the text, we only indicate a view of the work covered.

Thus Section 2 treats a geometric invariant, Riesz angles, of Orlicz spaces and relates it to certain other invariants such as Kottman constants and reflexivity properties studied earlier. This concept has interest in applications related to nonexpansive mappings and fixed point theory. Then Section 3 complements the work on embedding theorems considered in the earlier volume, by obtaining sharp results for the sequence spaces. This is largely worked out by Ren for this book. Similarly Section 4 contains several additions to the differentiability and smoothness of Orlicz sequence spaces or those based on diffuse measures, paying special attention to both the gauge and Orlicz norms. The final section on certain nonlocally convex (or a class of generalized) Orlicz spaces contains several extensions of known results for $L^{\Phi}$ -spaces with $\Phi$ an $N$ -function, to $L^{\Phi^{-1}}$ -spaces, $\Phi^{-1}$ being a symmetric concave function. The work is based on an unpublished thesis of Wang [1], recently completed under the direction of Ren, and is presented with the permission of the author. This is a long section and contains several complements (for sequence and diffuse cases) to the Orlicz space versions which we hope will be a useful reference for applications.
